[[Oomiyanome no Okami]] | type = Former Prefectural Shrine, Beppyo Shrine | founded = Traditionally [[Tenki]] 5 (1057) | main hall= [[Irimoya-zukuri]] | alternate names = | Pilgrimage Sites = | festival = May 5 | Rites = [[Hatsuuma|Hatsuuma festival]] (First Horse Day of the Second Lunar Month) | map = {{location map|Japan Iwate|lat_deg=39|lat_min=33|lat_sec=32.1|lon_deg=141|lon_min=04|lon_sec=40.2|label=Shiwa Inari Shrine|position=center|width=200|float=center}} }} '''Shiwa Inari Shrine''' (志和稲荷神社) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located on the western foothills of the Masuzawa settlement in [[Shiwa Town]], [[Shiwa District, Iwate|Shiwa District]], [[Iwate Prefecture]], on the left bank of the [[Takinakawa River]]. It is a former prefectural shrine and currently a Beppyo Shrine under the Association of Shinto Shrines. Locally, it is sometimes considered one of the [[Inari shrine#Three Great Inari Shrines of Japan|Three Great Inari Shrines of Japan]]{{Cite web|url=https://www.lafrance.co.jp/company.htm |title=La France Onsen Kaisha|publisher=[[Shiwa Town]] [[Third Sector]] Shiwa Machizukuri Kikaku |accessdate=2018-11-16 }}{{Cite web|url=http://www.tsukinowa-iwate.com/press/index8p40.html |title=Featured Information on Meijo Kigan |publisher=[[Tsukinowa Sake Brewery]] |accessdate=2018-11-16 }} or one of the [[Inari shrine#Five Great Inari Shrines of Japan|Five Great Inari Shrines of Japan]]{{Cite web|url=http://www.iwatekeizai.org/watching/watching_201801/8066/ |title=Shiwa Inari Shrine in Shiwa Town |publisher=[[Iwate Bank]] Iwate Economic Research Institute |date=2018-01 |accessdate=2018-12-16 }}{{Citation needed|date=2018-12|title=Sources claiming Shiwa Inari Shrine as one of the Five Great Inari Shrines of Japan}}. Nearby, there is another shrine called [[Shiwa Koinari Shrine]]. == Enshrined Deities == The enshrined deities are [[Ukanomitama|Ukanomitama no Okami]] (Inari Okami), [[Sarutahiko|Sarutahiko no Okami]], and [[Oomiyanome no Okami]]. The shrine considers Oomiyanome no Okami to be another name for [[Ame-no-Uzume|Ame-no-Uzume no Mikoto]]. During the [[Edo period]], the shrine also worshipped [[Juichimen Kannon]] as its principal Buddha.『Goryobun Shato』. This document, compiled in the mid-18th century, lists temples and shrines within the Morioka domain. == History == According to tradition, the shrine was founded in [[Tenki]] 5 (1057) when [[Minamoto no Yoriyoshi]] enshrined a divided spirit from [[Fushimi Inari Taisha]] in Kyoto to pray for victory in his campaign against the Abe clan during the [[Former Nine Years' War]]. Later, the Fujiwara clan members who ruled Shiwa District, such as Hiramune Takanari, are said to have rebuilt the shrine.『Kadokawa Japan Place name Dictionary』. Another tradition claims that [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] rebuilt the shrine during the [[1189|Oshu War]] in 1189.『Iwate Place names』. There is also a tradition stating that the shrine was founded during the [[Daido]] period (806-810), and another stating that [[Shiba Ienaga]] enshrined the deity from Fushimi Inari when he came to Shiwa District in [[Kenmu]] 2 (1335) as the commander of Oshu.『Shinto Dictionary』 Vol. 2, Heibonsha, 1939. The shrine received patronage from the Shiba clan, who ruled Shiwa District throughout the [[Muromachi period]]. In [[Tensho]] 16 (1588), [[Shiba Tsunenao]] built the shrine building,The shrine possesses a building plaque dated May 28, 1588. but after Tsunenao was ousted by [[Nanbu Nobunao]] that same year, the Nanbu clan became the shrine's patrons. After being attacked by Nobunao, Tsunenao hid in the shrine's affiliated temple, Joju-in, and then fled to Sanno-kai (a small basin in the upper reaches of the Takinakawa River, in present-day Tsuchidate, Shiwa Town). The Nanbu clan's patronage continued, with records showing that Nanbu Nobunao donated over 24 koku of rice fields in [[Keicho]] 4 (1599). Additional donations were made, including 20 koku from Hirasawa Village (present-day Hirasawa, Shiwa Town) in 1612, and another 10 koku in 1614. In [[Shoo]] 1 (1652), Nanbu Nobuaki donated 7 koku of shrine land. By [[Kaei]] 2 (1849), the total donations from the Nanbu domain amounted to 70 koku. festivals and other events were frequently attended by representatives from the Nanbu clan, confirming the shrine's status as a place of worship for the Nanbu family. In [[Tenpo]] 5 (1834), Nanbu Toshiharu constructed a pilgrimage road from [[Morioka]] to the shrine, modeled after the [[Nikko Kaido]], a distance of about 15 km (5 ri). The shrine was ranked as a [[Village Shrine]] in the early [[Meiji]] period and was elevated to a [[Prefectural Shrine]] in [[Taisho]] 7 (1918). After World War II, it joined the Association of Shinto Shrines and was designated as a Beppyo Shrine in [[Showa]] 32 (1957). == Rituals == Until the early Meiji period, the shrine was influenced by the syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism and was managed by the Shugen-do temple Joju-in. == Grounds == The shrine grounds feature a cedar tree over 1,000 years old, known as the "Inariyama Great Cedar," which is considered sacred. There is a custom of searching for white fox hair at the base of the cedar tree to pray for longevity. == References == {{Reflist}} == External Links == *[http://www.shiwa-oinarisan.jp Shiwa Inari Shrine] (official site) *[http://www.ganshinsei.jp/shiwachou/118shiwainarijinja.html Shiwa Inari Shrine] (Iwate Prefecture Shinto Youth Association) {{Shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Shiwa Inari Shrine}} [[Sarutahiko|Sarutahiko no Okami]]
[[Oomiyanome no Okami]] | type = Former Prefectural Shrine, Beppyo Shrine | founded = Traditionally [[Tenki]] 5 (1057) | main hall= [[Irimoya-zukuri]] | alternate names = | Pilgrimage Sites = | festival = May 5 | Rites = [[Hatsuuma|Hatsuuma festival]] (First Horse Day of the Second Lunar Month) | map = {{location map|Japan Iwate|lat_deg=39|lat_min=33|lat_sec=32.1|lon_deg=141|lon_min=04|lon_sec=40.2|label=Shiwa Inari Shrine|position=center|width=200|float=center}} }} '''Shiwa Inari Shrine''' (志和稲荷神社) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located on the western foothills of the Masuzawa settlement in [[Shiwa Town]], [[Shiwa District, Iwate|Shiwa District]], [[Iwate Prefecture]], on the left bank of the [[Takinakawa River]]. It is a former prefectural shrine and currently a Beppyo Shrine under the Association of Shinto Shrines. Locally, it is sometimes considered one of the [[Inari shrine#Three Great Inari Shrines of Japan|Three Great Inari Shrines of Japan]]{{Cite web|url=https://www.lafrance.co.jp/company.htm |title=La France Onsen Kaisha|publisher=[[Shiwa Town]] [[Third Sector]] Shiwa Machizukuri Kikaku |accessdate=2018-11-16 }}{{Cite web|url=http://www.tsukinowa-iwate.com/press/index8p40.html |title=Featured Information on Meijo Kigan |publisher=[[Tsukinowa Sake Brewery]] |accessdate=2018-11-16 }} or one of the [[Inari shrine#Five Great Inari Shrines of Japan|Five Great Inari Shrines of Japan]]{{Cite web|url=http://www.iwatekeizai.org/watching/watching_201801/8066/ |title=Shiwa Inari Shrine in Shiwa Town |publisher=[[Iwate Bank]] Iwate Economic Research Institute |date=2018-01 |accessdate=2018-12-16 }}{{Citation needed|date=2018-12|title=Sources claiming Shiwa Inari Shrine as one of the Five Great Inari Shrines of Japan}}. Nearby, there is another shrine called [[Shiwa Koinari Shrine]]. == Enshrined Deities == The enshrined deities are [[Ukanomitama|Ukanomitama no Okami]] (Inari Okami), [[Sarutahiko|Sarutahiko no Okami]], and [[Oomiyanome no Okami]]. The shrine considers Oomiyanome no Okami to be another name for [[Ame-no-Uzume|Ame-no-Uzume no Mikoto]]. During the [[Edo period]], the shrine also worshipped [[Juichimen Kannon]] as its principal Buddha.『Goryobun Shato』. This document, compiled in the mid-18th century, lists temples and shrines within the Morioka domain. == History == According to tradition, the shrine was founded in [[Tenki]] 5 (1057) when [[Minamoto no Yoriyoshi]] enshrined a divided spirit from [[Fushimi Inari Taisha]] in Kyoto to pray for victory in his campaign against the Abe clan during the [[Former Nine Years' War]]. Later, the Fujiwara clan members who ruled Shiwa District, such as Hiramune Takanari, are said to have rebuilt the shrine.『Kadokawa Japan Place name Dictionary』. Another tradition claims that [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] rebuilt the shrine during the [[1189|Oshu War]] in 1189.『Iwate Place names』. There is also a tradition stating that the shrine was founded during the [[Daido]] period (806-810), and another stating that [[Shiba Ienaga]] enshrined the deity from Fushimi Inari when he came to Shiwa District in [[Kenmu]] 2 (1335) as the commander of Oshu.『Shinto Dictionary』 Vol. 2, Heibonsha, 1939. The shrine received patronage from the Shiba clan, who ruled Shiwa District throughout the [[Muromachi period]]. In [[Tensho]] 16 (1588), [[Shiba Tsunenao]] built the shrine building,The shrine possesses a building plaque dated May 28, 1588. but after Tsunenao was ousted by [[Nanbu Nobunao]] that same year, the Nanbu clan became the shrine's patrons. After being attacked by Nobunao, Tsunenao hid in the shrine's affiliated temple, Joju-in, and then fled to Sanno-kai (a small basin in the upper reaches of the Takinakawa River, in present-day Tsuchidate, Shiwa Town). The Nanbu clan's patronage continued, with records showing that Nanbu Nobunao donated over 24 koku of rice fields in [[Keicho]] 4 (1599). Additional donations were made, including 20 koku from Hirasawa Village (present-day Hirasawa, Shiwa Town) in 1612, and another 10 koku in 1614. In [[Shoo]] 1 (1652), Nanbu Nobuaki donated 7 koku of shrine land. By [[Kaei]] 2 (1849), the total donations from the Nanbu domain amounted to 70 koku. festivals and other events were frequently attended by representatives from the Nanbu clan, confirming the shrine's status as a place of worship for the Nanbu family. In [[Tenpo]] 5 (1834), Nanbu Toshiharu constructed a pilgrimage road from [[Morioka]] to the shrine, modeled after the [[Nikko Kaido]], a distance of about 15 km (5 ri). The shrine was ranked as a [[Village Shrine]] in the early [[Meiji]] period and was elevated to a [[Prefectural Shrine]] in [[Taisho]] 7 (1918). After World War II, it joined the Association of Shinto Shrines and was designated as a Beppyo Shrine in [[Showa]] 32 (1957). == Rituals == Until the early Meiji period, the shrine was influenced by the syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism and was managed by the Shugen-do temple Joju-in. == Grounds == The shrine grounds feature a cedar tree over 1,000 years old, known as the "Inariyama Great Cedar," which is considered sacred. There is a custom of searching for white fox hair at the base of the cedar tree to pray for longevity. == References == {{Reflist}} == External Links == *[http://www.shiwa-oinarisan.jp Shiwa Inari Shrine] (official site) *[http://www.ganshinsei.jp/shiwachou/118shiwainarijinja.html Shiwa Inari Shrine] (Iwate Prefecture Shinto Youth Association) {{Shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Shiwa Inari Shrine}} Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Shiwa Inari Shrine | image = [[File:志和稲荷神社 拝殿.jpg|thumb|Shiwa Inari Shrine Worship Hall]] | location = [[Iwate Prefecture]][[Shiwa District, Iwate|Shiwa District]][[Shiwa, Iwate|Shiwa Town]] Masuzawa Aza Maehira 17-1 | coordinates = {{Coord|39|33|32.1|N|141|04|40.2|E|scale:25000_region:JP-03_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deity = [[Ukanomitama|Ukanomitama no Okami]]
[[Sarutahiko|Sarutahiko no Okami]]
[[Oomiyanome no Okami]] | type = Former Prefectural Shrine, Beppyo Shrine | founded = Traditionally [[Tenki]] 5 (1057) | main hall= [[Irimoya-zukuri]] | alternate names = | Pilgrimage Sites = | festival = May 5 | Rites = [[Hatsuuma|Hatsuuma festival]] (First Horse Day of the Second Lunar Month) | map = {{location map|Japan Iwate|lat_deg=39|lat_min=33|lat_sec=32.1|lon_deg=141|lon_min=04|lon_sec=40.2|label=Shiwa Inari Shrine|position=center|width=200|float=center}} }} '''Shiwa Inari Shrine''' (志和稲荷神社) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located on the western foothills of the Masuzawa settlement in [[Shiwa Town]], [[Shiwa District, Iwate|Shiwa District]], [[Iwate Prefecture]], on the left bank of the [[Takinakawa River]]. It is a former prefectural shrine and currently a Beppyo Shrine under the Association of Shinto Shrines. Locally, it is sometimes considered one of the [[Inari shrine#Three Great Inari Shrines of Japan|Three Great Inari Shrines of Japan]]{{Cite web|url=https://www.lafrance.co.jp/company.htm |title=La France Onsen Kaisha|publisher=[[Shiwa Town]] [[Third Sector]] Shiwa Machizukuri Kikaku |accessdate=2018-11-16 }}{{Cite web|url=http://www.tsukinowa-iwate.com/press/index8p40.html |title=Featured Information on Meijo Kigan |publisher=[[Tsukinowa Sake Brewery]] |accessdate=2018-11-16 }} or one of the [[Inari shrine#Five Great Inari Shrines of Japan|Five Great Inari Shrines of Japan]]{{Cite web|url=http://www.iwatekeizai.org/watching/watching_201801/8066/ |title=Shiwa Inari Shrine in Shiwa Town |publisher=[[Iwate Bank]] Iwate Economic Research Institute |date=2018-01 |accessdate=2018-12-16 }}{{Citation needed|date=2018-12|title=Sources claiming Shiwa Inari Shrine as one of the Five Great Inari Shrines of Japan}}. Nearby, there is another shrine called [[Shiwa Koinari Shrine]]. == Enshrined Deities == The enshrined deities are [[Ukanomitama|Ukanomitama no Okami]] (Inari Okami), [[Sarutahiko|Sarutahiko no Okami]], and [[Oomiyanome no Okami]]. The shrine considers Oomiyanome no Okami to be another name for [[Ame-no-Uzume|Ame-no-Uzume no Mikoto]]. During the [[Edo period]], the shrine also worshipped [[Juichimen Kannon]] as its principal Buddha.『Goryobun Shato』. This document, compiled in the mid-18th century, lists temples and shrines within the Morioka domain. == History == According to tradition, the shrine was founded in [[Tenki]] 5 (1057) when [[Minamoto no Yoriyoshi]] enshrined a divided spirit from [[Fushimi Inari Taisha]] in Kyoto to pray for victory in his campaign against the Abe clan during the [[Former Nine Years' War]]. Later, the Fujiwara clan members who ruled Shiwa District, such as Hiramune Takanari, are said to have rebuilt the shrine.『Kadokawa Japan Place name Dictionary』. Another tradition claims that [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] rebuilt the shrine during the [[1189|Oshu War]] in 1189.『Iwate Place names』. There is also a tradition stating that the shrine was founded during the [[Daido]] period (806-810), and another stating that [[Shiba Ienaga]] enshrined the deity from Fushimi Inari when he came to Shiwa District in [[Kenmu]] 2 (1335) as the commander of Oshu.『Shinto Dictionary』 Vol. 2, Heibonsha, 1939. The shrine received patronage from the Shiba clan, who ruled Shiwa District throughout the [[Muromachi period]]. In [[Tensho]] 16 (1588), [[Shiba Tsunenao]] built the shrine building,The shrine possesses a building plaque dated May 28, 1588. but after Tsunenao was ousted by [[Nanbu Nobunao]] that same year, the Nanbu clan became the shrine's patrons. After being attacked by Nobunao, Tsunenao hid in the shrine's affiliated temple, Joju-in, and then fled to Sanno-kai (a small basin in the upper reaches of the Takinakawa River, in present-day Tsuchidate, Shiwa Town). The Nanbu clan's patronage continued, with records showing that Nanbu Nobunao donated over 24 koku of rice fields in [[Keicho]] 4 (1599). Additional donations were made, including 20 koku from Hirasawa Village (present-day Hirasawa, Shiwa Town) in 1612, and another 10 koku in 1614. In [[Shoo]] 1 (1652), Nanbu Nobuaki donated 7 koku of shrine land. By [[Kaei]] 2 (1849), the total donations from the Nanbu domain amounted to 70 koku. festivals and other events were frequently attended by representatives from the Nanbu clan, confirming the shrine's status as a place of worship for the Nanbu family. In [[Tenpo]] 5 (1834), Nanbu Toshiharu constructed a pilgrimage road from [[Morioka]] to the shrine, modeled after the [[Nikko Kaido]], a distance of about 15 km (5 ri). The shrine was ranked as a [[Village Shrine]] in the early [[Meiji]] period and was elevated to a [[Prefectural Shrine]] in [[Taisho]] 7 (1918). After World War II, it joined the Association of Shinto Shrines and was designated as a Beppyo Shrine in [[Showa]] 32 (1957). == Rituals == Until the early Meiji period, the shrine was influenced by the syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism and was managed by the Shugen-do temple Joju-in. == Grounds == The shrine grounds feature a cedar tree over 1,000 years old, known as the "Inariyama Great Cedar," which is considered sacred. There is a custom of searching for white fox hair at the base of the cedar tree to pray for longevity. == References == {{Reflist}} == External Links == *[http://www.shiwa-oinarisan.jp Shiwa Inari Shrine] (official site) *[http://www.ganshinsei.jp/shiwachou/118shiwainarijinja.html Shiwa Inari Shrine] (Iwate Prefecture Shinto Youth Association) {{Shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Shiwa Inari Shrine}} It was then automatically transferred to everybodywiki and recovered from there. In the future it will be good to import the revisions. Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Shiwa Inari Shrine | image = [[File:志和稲荷神社 拝殿.jpg|thumb|Shiwa Inari Shrine Worship Hall]] | location = [[Iwate Prefecture]][[Shiwa District, Iwate|Shiwa District]][[Shiwa, Iwate|Shiwa Town]] Masuzawa Aza Maehira 17-1 | coordinates = {{Coord|39|33|32.1|N|141|04|40.2|E|scale:25000_region:JP-03_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deity = [[Ukanomitama|Ukanomitama no Okami]]
[[Sarutahiko|Sarutahiko no Okami]]
[[Oomiyanome no Okami]] | type = Former Prefectural Shrine, Beppyo Shrine | founded = Traditionally [[Tenki]] 5 (1057) | main hall= [[Irimoya-zukuri]] | alternate names = | Pilgrimage Sites = | festival = May 5 | Rites = [[Hatsuuma|Hatsuuma festival]] (First Horse Day of the Second Lunar Month) | map = {{location map|Japan Iwate|lat_deg=39|lat_min=33|lat_sec=32.1|lon_deg=141|lon_min=04|lon_sec=40.2|label=Shiwa Inari Shrine|position=center|width=200|float=center}} }} '''Shiwa Inari Shrine''' (志和稲荷神社) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located on the western foothills of the Masuzawa settlement in [[Shiwa Town]], [[Shiwa District, Iwate|Shiwa District]], [[Iwate Prefecture]], on the left bank of the [[Takinakawa River]]. It is a former prefectural shrine and currently a Beppyo Shrine under the Association of Shinto Shrines. Locally, it is sometimes considered one of the [[Inari shrine#Three Great Inari Shrines of Japan|Three Great Inari Shrines of Japan]]{{Cite web|url=https://www.lafrance.co.jp/company.htm |title=La France Onsen Kaisha|publisher=[[Shiwa Town]] [[Third Sector]] Shiwa Machizukuri Kikaku |accessdate=2018-11-16 }}{{Cite web|url=http://www.tsukinowa-iwate.com/press/index8p40.html |title=Featured Information on Meijo Kigan |publisher=[[Tsukinowa Sake Brewery]] |accessdate=2018-11-16 }} or one of the [[Inari shrine#Five Great Inari Shrines of Japan|Five Great Inari Shrines of Japan]]{{Cite web|url=http://www.iwatekeizai.org/watching/watching_201801/8066/ |title=Shiwa Inari Shrine in Shiwa Town |publisher=[[Iwate Bank]] Iwate Economic Research Institute |date=2018-01 |accessdate=2018-12-16 }}{{Citation needed|date=2018-12|title=Sources claiming Shiwa Inari Shrine as one of the Five Great Inari Shrines of Japan}}. Nearby, there is another shrine called [[Shiwa Koinari Shrine]]. == Enshrined Deities == The enshrined deities are [[Ukanomitama|Ukanomitama no Okami]] (Inari Okami), [[Sarutahiko|Sarutahiko no Okami]], and [[Oomiyanome no Okami]]. The shrine considers Oomiyanome no Okami to be another name for [[Ame-no-Uzume|Ame-no-Uzume no Mikoto]]. During the [[Edo period]], the shrine also worshipped [[Juichimen Kannon]] as its principal Buddha.『Goryobun Shato』. This document, compiled in the mid-18th century, lists temples and shrines within the Morioka domain. == History == According to tradition, the shrine was founded in [[Tenki]] 5 (1057) when [[Minamoto no Yoriyoshi]] enshrined a divided spirit from [[Fushimi Inari Taisha]] in Kyoto to pray for victory in his campaign against the Abe clan during the [[Former Nine Years' War]]. Later, the Fujiwara clan members who ruled Shiwa District, such as Hiramune Takanari, are said to have rebuilt the shrine.『Kadokawa Japan Place name Dictionary』. Another tradition claims that [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] rebuilt the shrine during the [[1189|Oshu War]] in 1189.『Iwate Place names』. There is also a tradition stating that the shrine was founded during the [[Daido]] period (806-810), and another stating that [[Shiba Ienaga]] enshrined the deity from Fushimi Inari when he came to Shiwa District in [[Kenmu]] 2 (1335) as the commander of Oshu.『Shinto Dictionary』 Vol. 2, Heibonsha, 1939. The shrine received patronage from the Shiba clan, who ruled Shiwa District throughout the [[Muromachi period]]. In [[Tensho]] 16 (1588), [[Shiba Tsunenao]] built the shrine building,The shrine possesses a building plaque dated May 28, 1588. but after Tsunenao was ousted by [[Nanbu Nobunao]] that same year, the Nanbu clan became the shrine's patrons. After being attacked by Nobunao, Tsunenao hid in the shrine's affiliated temple, Joju-in, and then fled to Sanno-kai (a small basin in the upper reaches of the Takinakawa River, in present-day Tsuchidate, Shiwa Town). The Nanbu clan's patronage continued, with records showing that Nanbu Nobunao donated over 24 koku of rice fields in [[Keicho]] 4 (1599). Additional donations were made, including 20 koku from Hirasawa Village (present-day Hirasawa, Shiwa Town) in 1612, and another 10 koku in 1614. In [[Shoo]] 1 (1652), Nanbu Nobuaki donated 7 koku of shrine land. By [[Kaei]] 2 (1849), the total donations from the Nanbu domain amounted to 70 koku. festivals and other events were frequently attended by representatives from the Nanbu clan, confirming the shrine's status as a place of worship for the Nanbu family. In [[Tenpo]] 5 (1834), Nanbu Toshiharu constructed a pilgrimage road from [[Morioka]] to the shrine, modeled after the [[Nikko Kaido]], a distance of about 15 km (5 ri). The shrine was ranked as a [[Village Shrine]] in the early [[Meiji]] period and was elevated to a [[Prefectural Shrine]] in [[Taisho]] 7 (1918). After World War II, it joined the Association of Shinto Shrines and was designated as a Beppyo Shrine in [[Showa]] 32 (1957). == Rituals == Until the early Meiji period, the shrine was influenced by the syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism and was managed by the Shugen-do temple Joju-in. == Grounds == The shrine grounds feature a cedar tree over 1,000 years old, known as the "Inariyama Great Cedar," which is considered sacred. There is a custom of searching for white fox hair at the base of the cedar tree to pray for longevity. == References == {{Reflist}} == External Links == *[http://www.shiwa-oinarisan.jp Shiwa Inari Shrine] (official site) *[http://www.ganshinsei.jp/shiwachou/118shiwainarijinja.html Shiwa Inari Shrine] (Iwate Prefecture Shinto Youth Association) {{Shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Shiwa Inari Shrine}} {{draft}} {{Promising draft|reason=the high amount of work put into it over a long time, see edit history}} {{afc comment|1=Page originally made on english wikipedia in draftspace. g13 deleted and archived on everybodywiki. Go to [[:en:Wikipedia:Requests for undeletion]] to request undeletion of original page [[:en:draft:Shiwa Inari Shrine]][[en:draft:Shiwa Inari Shrine]] and import the revisions to here for attribution. View on everybodywiki here [[:every:Shiwa Inari Shrine|Shiwa Inari Shrine]]. [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 21:01, 16 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created by Immanuelle as a wikipedia draft. It was then automatically transferred to everybodywiki and recovered from there. In the future it will be good to import the revisions. Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Shiwa Inari Shrine | image = [[File:志和稲荷神社 拝殿.jpg|thumb|Shiwa Inari Shrine Worship Hall]] | location = [[Iwate Prefecture]][[Shiwa District, Iwate|Shiwa District]][[Shiwa, Iwate|Shiwa Town]] Masuzawa Aza Maehira 17-1 | coordinates = {{Coord|39|33|32.1|N|141|04|40.2|E|scale:25000_region:JP-03_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deity = [[Ukanomitama|Ukanomitama no Okami]]
[[Sarutahiko|Sarutahiko no Okami]]
[[Oomiyanome no Okami]] | type = Former Prefectural Shrine, Beppyo Shrine | founded = Traditionally [[Tenki]] 5 (1057) | main hall= [[Irimoya-zukuri]] | alternate names = | Pilgrimage Sites = | festival = May 5 | Rites = [[Hatsuuma|Hatsuuma festival]] (First Horse Day of the Second Lunar Month) | map = {{location map|Japan Iwate|lat_deg=39|lat_min=33|lat_sec=32.1|lon_deg=141|lon_min=04|lon_sec=40.2|label=Shiwa Inari Shrine|position=center|width=200|float=center}} }} '''Shiwa Inari Shrine''' (志和稲荷神社) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located on the western foothills of the Masuzawa settlement in [[Shiwa Town]], [[Shiwa District, Iwate|Shiwa District]], [[Iwate Prefecture]], on the left bank of the [[Takinakawa River]]. It is a former prefectural shrine and currently a Beppyo Shrine under the Association of Shinto Shrines. Locally, it is sometimes considered one of the [[Inari shrine#Three Great Inari Shrines of Japan|Three Great Inari Shrines of Japan]]{{Cite web|url=https://www.lafrance.co.jp/company.htm |title=La France Onsen Kaisha|publisher=[[Shiwa Town]] [[Third Sector]] Shiwa Machizukuri Kikaku |accessdate=2018-11-16 }}{{Cite web|url=http://www.tsukinowa-iwate.com/press/index8p40.html |title=Featured Information on Meijo Kigan |publisher=[[Tsukinowa Sake Brewery]] |accessdate=2018-11-16 }} or one of the [[Inari shrine#Five Great Inari Shrines of Japan|Five Great Inari Shrines of Japan]]{{Cite web|url=http://www.iwatekeizai.org/watching/watching_201801/8066/ |title=Shiwa Inari Shrine in Shiwa Town |publisher=[[Iwate Bank]] Iwate Economic Research Institute |date=2018-01 |accessdate=2018-12-16 }}{{Citation needed|date=2018-12|title=Sources claiming Shiwa Inari Shrine as one of the Five Great Inari Shrines of Japan}}. Nearby, there is another shrine called [[Shiwa Koinari Shrine]]. == Enshrined Deities == The enshrined deities are [[Ukanomitama|Ukanomitama no Okami]] (Inari Okami), [[Sarutahiko|Sarutahiko no Okami]], and [[Oomiyanome no Okami]]. The shrine considers Oomiyanome no Okami to be another name for [[Ame-no-Uzume|Ame-no-Uzume no Mikoto]]. During the [[Edo period]], the shrine also worshipped [[Juichimen Kannon]] as its principal Buddha.『Goryobun Shato』. This document, compiled in the mid-18th century, lists temples and shrines within the Morioka domain. == History == According to tradition, the shrine was founded in [[Tenki]] 5 (1057) when [[Minamoto no Yoriyoshi]] enshrined a divided spirit from [[Fushimi Inari Taisha]] in Kyoto to pray for victory in his campaign against the Abe clan during the [[Former Nine Years' War]]. Later, the Fujiwara clan members who ruled Shiwa District, such as Hiramune Takanari, are said to have rebuilt the shrine.『Kadokawa Japan Place name Dictionary』. Another tradition claims that [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] rebuilt the shrine during the [[1189|Oshu War]] in 1189.『Iwate Place names』. There is also a tradition stating that the shrine was founded during the [[Daido]] period (806-810), and another stating that [[Shiba Ienaga]] enshrined the deity from Fushimi Inari when he came to Shiwa District in [[Kenmu]] 2 (1335) as the commander of Oshu.『Shinto Dictionary』 Vol. 2, Heibonsha, 1939. The shrine received patronage from the Shiba clan, who ruled Shiwa District throughout the [[Muromachi period]]. In [[Tensho]] 16 (1588), [[Shiba Tsunenao]] built the shrine building,The shrine possesses a building plaque dated May 28, 1588. but after Tsunenao was ousted by [[Nanbu Nobunao]] that same year, the Nanbu clan became the shrine's patrons. After being attacked by Nobunao, Tsunenao hid in the shrine's affiliated temple, Joju-in, and then fled to Sanno-kai (a small basin in the upper reaches of the Takinakawa River, in present-day Tsuchidate, Shiwa Town). The Nanbu clan's patronage continued, with records showing that Nanbu Nobunao donated over 24 koku of rice fields in [[Keicho]] 4 (1599). Additional donations were made, including 20 koku from Hirasawa Village (present-day Hirasawa, Shiwa Town) in 1612, and another 10 koku in 1614. In [[Shoo]] 1 (1652), Nanbu Nobuaki donated 7 koku of shrine land. By [[Kaei]] 2 (1849), the total donations from the Nanbu domain amounted to 70 koku. festivals and other events were frequently attended by representatives from the Nanbu clan, confirming the shrine's status as a place of worship for the Nanbu family. In [[Tenpo]] 5 (1834), Nanbu Toshiharu constructed a pilgrimage road from [[Morioka]] to the shrine, modeled after the [[Nikko Kaido]], a distance of about 15 km (5 ri). The shrine was ranked as a [[Village Shrine]] in the early [[Meiji]] period and was elevated to a [[Prefectural Shrine]] in [[Taisho]] 7 (1918). After World War II, it joined the Association of Shinto Shrines and was designated as a Beppyo Shrine in [[Showa]] 32 (1957). == Rituals == Until the early Meiji period, the shrine was influenced by the syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism and was managed by the Shugen-do temple Joju-in. == Grounds == The shrine grounds feature a cedar tree over 1,000 years old, known as the "Inariyama Great Cedar," which is considered sacred. There is a custom of searching for white fox hair at the base of the cedar tree to pray for longevity. == References == {{Reflist}} == External Links == *[http://www.shiwa-oinarisan.jp Shiwa Inari Shrine] (official site) *[http://www.ganshinsei.jp/shiwachou/118shiwainarijinja.html Shiwa Inari Shrine] (Iwate Prefecture Shinto Youth Association) {{Shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Shiwa Inari Shrine}} revision.text:
{{draft}} {{Promising draft|reason=the high amount of work put into it over a long time, see edit history}} {{afc comment|1=Page originally made on english wikipedia in draftspace. g13 deleted and archived on everybodywiki. Go to [[:en:Wikipedia:Requests for undeletion]] to request undeletion of original page [[:en:draft:Shiwa Inari Shrine]][[en:draft:Shiwa Inari Shrine]] and import the revisions to here for attribution. View on everybodywiki here [[:every:Shiwa Inari Shrine|Shiwa Inari Shrine]]. [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 21:01, 16 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created by Immanuelle as a wikipedia draft. It was then automatically transferred to everybodywiki and recovered from there. In the future it will be good to import the revisions. Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Shiwa Inari Shrine | image = [[File:志和稲荷神社 拝殿.jpg|thumb|Shiwa Inari Shrine Worship Hall]] | location = [[Iwate Prefecture]][[Shiwa District, Iwate|Shiwa District]][[Shiwa, Iwate|Shiwa Town]] Masuzawa Aza Maehira 17-1 | coordinates = {{Coord|39|33|32.1|N|141|04|40.2|E|scale:25000_region:JP-03_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deity = [[Ukanomitama|Ukanomitama no Okami]]
[[Sarutahiko|Sarutahiko no Okami]]
[[Oomiyanome no Okami]] | type = Former Prefectural Shrine, Beppyo Shrine | founded = Traditionally [[Tenki]] 5 (1057) | main hall= [[Irimoya-zukuri]] | alternate names = | Pilgrimage Sites = | festival = May 5 | Rites = [[Hatsuuma|Hatsuuma festival]] (First Horse Day of the Second Lunar Month) | map = {{location map|Japan Iwate|lat_deg=39|lat_min=33|lat_sec=32.1|lon_deg=141|lon_min=04|lon_sec=40.2|label=Shiwa Inari Shrine|position=center|width=200|float=center}} }} '''Shiwa Inari Shrine''' (志和稲荷神社) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located on the western foothills of the Masuzawa settlement in [[Shiwa Town]], [[Shiwa District, Iwate|Shiwa District]], [[Iwate Prefecture]], on the left bank of the [[Takinakawa River]]. It is a former prefectural shrine and currently a Beppyo Shrine under the Association of Shinto Shrines. Locally, it is sometimes considered one of the [[Inari shrine#Three Great Inari Shrines of Japan|Three Great Inari Shrines of Japan]]{{Cite web|url=https://www.lafrance.co.jp/company.htm |title=La France Onsen Kaisha|publisher=[[Shiwa Town]] [[Third Sector]] Shiwa Machizukuri Kikaku |accessdate=2018-11-16 }}{{Cite web|url=http://www.tsukinowa-iwate.com/press/index8p40.html |title=Featured Information on Meijo Kigan |publisher=[[Tsukinowa Sake Brewery]] |accessdate=2018-11-16 }} or one of the [[Inari shrine#Five Great Inari Shrines of Japan|Five Great Inari Shrines of Japan]]{{Cite web|url=http://www.iwatekeizai.org/watching/watching_201801/8066/ |title=Shiwa Inari Shrine in Shiwa Town |publisher=[[Iwate Bank]] Iwate Economic Research Institute |date=2018-01 |accessdate=2018-12-16 }}{{Citation needed|date=2018-12|title=Sources claiming Shiwa Inari Shrine as one of the Five Great Inari Shrines of Japan}}. Nearby, there is another shrine called [[Shiwa Koinari Shrine]]. == Enshrined Deities == The enshrined deities are [[Ukanomitama|Ukanomitama no Okami]] (Inari Okami), [[Sarutahiko|Sarutahiko no Okami]], and [[Oomiyanome no Okami]]. The shrine considers Oomiyanome no Okami to be another name for [[Ame-no-Uzume|Ame-no-Uzume no Mikoto]]. During the [[Edo period]], the shrine also worshipped [[Juichimen Kannon]] as its principal Buddha.『Goryobun Shato』. This document, compiled in the mid-18th century, lists temples and shrines within the Morioka domain. == History == According to tradition, the shrine was founded in [[Tenki]] 5 (1057) when [[Minamoto no Yoriyoshi]] enshrined a divided spirit from [[Fushimi Inari Taisha]] in Kyoto to pray for victory in his campaign against the Abe clan during the [[Former Nine Years' War]]. Later, the Fujiwara clan members who ruled Shiwa District, such as Hiramune Takanari, are said to have rebuilt the shrine.『Kadokawa Japan Place name Dictionary』. Another tradition claims that [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] rebuilt the shrine during the [[1189|Oshu War]] in 1189.『Iwate Place names』. There is also a tradition stating that the shrine was founded during the [[Daido]] period (806-810), and another stating that [[Shiba Ienaga]] enshrined the deity from Fushimi Inari when he came to Shiwa District in [[Kenmu]] 2 (1335) as the commander of Oshu.『Shinto Dictionary』 Vol. 2, Heibonsha, 1939. The shrine received patronage from the Shiba clan, who ruled Shiwa District throughout the [[Muromachi period]]. In [[Tensho]] 16 (1588), [[Shiba Tsunenao]] built the shrine building,The shrine possesses a building plaque dated May 28, 1588. but after Tsunenao was ousted by [[Nanbu Nobunao]] that same year, the Nanbu clan became the shrine's patrons. After being attacked by Nobunao, Tsunenao hid in the shrine's affiliated temple, Joju-in, and then fled to Sanno-kai (a small basin in the upper reaches of the Takinakawa River, in present-day Tsuchidate, Shiwa Town). The Nanbu clan's patronage continued, with records showing that Nanbu Nobunao donated over 24 koku of rice fields in [[Keicho]] 4 (1599). Additional donations were made, including 20 koku from Hirasawa Village (present-day Hirasawa, Shiwa Town) in 1612, and another 10 koku in 1614. In [[Shoo]] 1 (1652), Nanbu Nobuaki donated 7 koku of shrine land. By [[Kaei]] 2 (1849), the total donations from the Nanbu domain amounted to 70 koku. festivals and other events were frequently attended by representatives from the Nanbu clan, confirming the shrine's status as a place of worship for the Nanbu family. In [[Tenpo]] 5 (1834), Nanbu Toshiharu constructed a pilgrimage road from [[Morioka]] to the shrine, modeled after the [[Nikko Kaido]], a distance of about 15 km (5 ri). The shrine was ranked as a [[Village Shrine]] in the early [[Meiji]] period and was elevated to a [[Prefectural Shrine]] in [[Taisho]] 7 (1918). After World War II, it joined the Association of Shinto Shrines and was designated as a Beppyo Shrine in [[Showa]] 32 (1957). == Rituals == Until the early Meiji period, the shrine was influenced by the syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism and was managed by the Shugen-do temple Joju-in. == Grounds == The shrine grounds feature a cedar tree over 1,000 years old, known as the "Inariyama Great Cedar," which is considered sacred. There is a custom of searching for white fox hair at the base of the cedar tree to pray for longevity. == References == {{Reflist}} == External Links == *[http://www.shiwa-oinarisan.jp Shiwa Inari Shrine] (official site) *[http://www.ganshinsei.jp/shiwachou/118shiwainarijinja.html Shiwa Inari Shrine] (Iwate Prefecture Shinto Youth Association) {{Shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Shiwa Inari Shrine}} Locally, it is sometimes considered one of the [[Inari shrine#Three Great Inari Shrines of Japan|Three Great Inari Shrines of Japan]]{{Cite web|url=https://www.lafrance.co.jp/company.htm |title=La France Onsen Kaisha|publisher=[[Shiwa Town]] [[Third Sector]] Shiwa Machizukuri Kikaku |accessdate=2018-11-16 }}{{Cite web|url=http://www.tsukinowa-iwate.com/press/index8p40.html |title=Featured Information on Meijo Kigan |publisher=[[Tsukinowa Sake Brewery]] |accessdate=2018-11-16 }} or one of the [[Inari shrine#Five Great Inari Shrines of Japan|Five Great Inari Shrines of Japan]]{{Cite web|url=http://www.iwatekeizai.org/watching/watching_201801/8066/ |title=Shiwa Inari Shrine in Shiwa Town |publisher=[[Iwate Bank]] Iwate Economic Research Institute |date=2018-01 |accessdate=2018-12-16 }}{{Citation needed|date=2018-12|title=Sources claiming Shiwa Inari Shrine as one of the Five Great Inari Shrines of Japan}}. The shrine received patronage from the Shiba clan, who ruled Shiwa District throughout the [[Muromachi period]]. In [[Tensho]] 16 (1588), [[Shiba Tsunenao]] built the shrine building,The shrine possesses a building plaque dated May 28, 1588. but after Tsunenao was ousted by [[Nanbu Nobunao]] that same year, the Nanbu clan became the shrine's patrons. After being attacked by Nobunao, Tsunenao hid in the shrine's affiliated temple, Joju-in, and then fled to Sanno-kai (a small basin in the upper reaches of the Takinakawa River, in present-day Tsuchidate, Shiwa Town). Additional donations were made, including 20 koku from Hirasawa Village (present-day Hirasawa, Shiwa Town) in 1612, and another 10 koku in 1614. In [[Shoo]] 1 (1652), Nanbu Nobuaki donated 7 koku of shrine land. By [[Kaei]] 2 (1849), the total donations from the Nanbu domain amounted to 70 koku. festivals and other events were frequently attended by representatives from the Nanbu clan, confirming the shrine's status as a place of worship for the Nanbu family. In [[Tenpo]] 5 (1834), Nanbu Toshiharu constructed a pilgrimage road from [[Morioka]] to the shrine, modeled after the [[Nikko Kaido]], a distance of about 15 km (5 ri). The shrine was ranked as a [[Village Shrine]] in the early [[Meiji]] period and was elevated to a [[Prefectural Shrine]] in [[Taisho]] 7 (1918). {{draft}} {{Promising draft|reason=the high amount of work put into it over a long time, see edit history}} {{afc comment|1=Page originally made on english wikipedia in draftspace. g13 deleted and archived on everybodywiki. Go to [[:en:Wikipedia:Requests for undeletion]] to request undeletion of original page [[:en:draft:Shiwa Inari Shrine]][[en:draft:Shiwa Inari Shrine]] and import the revisions to here for attribution. View on everybodywiki here [[:every:Shiwa Inari Shrine|Shiwa Inari Shrine]]. [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 21:01, 16 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created by Immanuelle as a wikipedia draft. It was then automatically transferred to everybodywiki and recovered from there. In the future it will be good to import the revisions. Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Shiwa Inari Shrine | image = [[File:志和稲荷神社 拝殿.jpg|thumb|Shiwa Inari Shrine Worship Hall]] | location = [[Iwate Prefecture]][[Shiwa District, Iwate|Shiwa District]][[Shiwa, Iwate|Shiwa Town]] Masuzawa Aza Maehira 17-1 | coordinates = {{Coord|39|33|32.1|N|141|04|40.2|E|scale:25000_region:JP-03_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deity = [[Ukanomitama|Ukanomitama no Okami]]
[[Sarutahiko|Sarutahiko no Okami]]
[[Oomiyanome no Okami]] | type = Former Prefectural Shrine, Beppyo Shrine | founded = Traditionally [[Tenki]] 5 (1057) | main hall= [[Irimoya-zukuri]] | alternate names = | Pilgrimage Sites = | festival = May 5 | Rites = [[Hatsuuma|Hatsuuma festival]] (First Horse Day of the Second Lunar Month) | map = {{location map|Japan Iwate|lat_deg=39|lat_min=33|lat_sec=32.1|lon_deg=141|lon_min=04|lon_sec=40.2|label=Shiwa Inari Shrine|position=center|width=200|float=center}} }} '''Shiwa Inari Shrine''' (志和稲荷神社) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located on the western foothills of the Masuzawa settlement in [[Shiwa Town]], [[Shiwa District, Iwate|Shiwa District]], [[Iwate Prefecture]], on the left bank of the [[Takinakawa River]]. It is a former prefectural shrine and currently a Beppyo Shrine under the Association of Shinto Shrines. Locally, it is sometimes considered one of the [[Inari shrine#Three Great Inari Shrines of Japan|Three Great Inari Shrines of Japan]]{{Cite web|url=https://www.lafrance.co.jp/company.htm |title=La France Onsen Kaisha|publisher=[[Shiwa Town]] [[Third Sector]] Shiwa Machizukuri Kikaku |accessdate=2018-11-16 }}{{Cite web|url=http://www.tsukinowa-iwate.com/press/index8p40.html |title=Featured Information on Meijo Kigan |publisher=[[Tsukinowa Sake Brewery]] |accessdate=2018-11-16 }} or one of the [[Inari shrine#Five Great Inari Shrines of Japan|Five Great Inari Shrines of Japan]]{{Cite web|url=http://www.iwatekeizai.org/watching/watching_201801/8066/ |title=Shiwa Inari Shrine in Shiwa Town |publisher=[[Iwate Bank]] Iwate Economic Research Institute |date=2018-01 |accessdate=2018-12-16 }}{{Citation needed|date=2018-12|title=Sources claiming Shiwa Inari Shrine as one of the Five Great Inari Shrines of Japan}}. Nearby, there is another shrine called [[Shiwa Koinari Shrine]]. == Enshrined Deities == The enshrined deities are [[Ukanomitama|Ukanomitama no Okami]] (Inari Okami), [[Sarutahiko|Sarutahiko no Okami]], and [[Oomiyanome no Okami]]. The shrine considers Oomiyanome no Okami to be another name for [[Ame-no-Uzume|Ame-no-Uzume no Mikoto]]. During the [[Edo period]], the shrine also worshipped [[Juichimen Kannon]] as its principal Buddha.『Goryobun Shato』. This document, compiled in the mid-18th century, lists temples and shrines within the Morioka domain. == History == According to tradition, the shrine was founded in [[Tenki]] 5 (1057) when [[Minamoto no Yoriyoshi]] enshrined a divided spirit from [[Fushimi Inari Taisha]] in Kyoto to pray for victory in his campaign against the Abe clan during the [[Former Nine Years' War]]. Later, the Fujiwara clan members who ruled Shiwa District, such as Hiramune Takanari, are said to have rebuilt the shrine.『Kadokawa Japan Place name Dictionary』. Another tradition claims that [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] rebuilt the shrine during the [[1189|Oshu War]] in 1189.『Iwate Place names』. There is also a tradition stating that the shrine was founded during the [[Daido]] period (806-810), and another stating that [[Shiba Ienaga]] enshrined the deity from Fushimi Inari when he came to Shiwa District in [[Kenmu]] 2 (1335) as the commander of Oshu.『Shinto Dictionary』 Vol. 2, Heibonsha, 1939. The shrine received patronage from the Shiba clan, who ruled Shiwa District throughout the [[Muromachi period]]. In [[Tensho]] 16 (1588), [[Shiba Tsunenao]] built the shrine building,The shrine possesses a building plaque dated May 28, 1588. but after Tsunenao was ousted by [[Nanbu Nobunao]] that same year, the Nanbu clan became the shrine's patrons. After being attacked by Nobunao, Tsunenao hid in the shrine's affiliated temple, Joju-in, and then fled to Sanno-kai (a small basin in the upper reaches of the Takinakawa River, in present-day Tsuchidate, Shiwa Town). The Nanbu clan's patronage continued, with records showing that Nanbu Nobunao donated over 24 koku of rice fields in [[Keicho]] 4 (1599). Additional donations were made, including 20 koku from Hirasawa Village (present-day Hirasawa, Shiwa Town) in 1612, and another 10 koku in 1614. In [[Shoo]] 1 (1652), Nanbu Nobuaki donated 7 koku of shrine land. By [[Kaei]] 2 (1849), the total donations from the Nanbu domain amounted to 70 koku. festivals and other events were frequently attended by representatives from the Nanbu clan, confirming the shrine's status as a place of worship for the Nanbu family. In [[Tenpo]] 5 (1834), Nanbu Toshiharu constructed a pilgrimage road from [[Morioka]] to the shrine, modeled after the [[Nikko Kaido]], a distance of about 15 km (5 ri). The shrine was ranked as a [[Village Shrine]] in the early [[Meiji]] period and was elevated to a [[Prefectural Shrine]] in [[Taisho]] 7 (1918). After World War II, it joined the Association of Shinto Shrines and was designated as a Beppyo Shrine in [[Showa]] 32 (1957). == Rituals == Until the early Meiji period, the shrine was influenced by the syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism and was managed by the Shugen-do temple Joju-in. == Grounds == The shrine grounds feature a cedar tree over 1,000 years old, known as the "Inariyama Great Cedar," which is considered sacred. There is a custom of searching for white fox hair at the base of the cedar tree to pray for longevity. == References == {{Reflist}} == External Links == *[http://www.shiwa-oinarisan.jp Shiwa Inari Shrine] (official site) *[http://www.ganshinsei.jp/shiwachou/118shiwainarijinja.html Shiwa Inari Shrine] (Iwate Prefecture Shinto Youth Association) {{Shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Shiwa Inari Shrine}} During the [[Edo period]], the shrine also worshipped [[Juichimen Kannon]] as its principal Buddha.『Goryobun Shato』. This document, compiled in the mid-18th century, lists temples and shrines within the Morioka domain. == History == According to tradition, the shrine was founded in [[Tenki]] 5 (1057) when [[Minamoto no Yoriyoshi]] enshrined a divided spirit from [[Fushimi Inari Taisha]] in Kyoto to pray for victory in his campaign against the Abe clan during the [[Former Nine Years' War]]. Later, the Fujiwara clan members who ruled Shiwa District, such as Hiramune Takanari, are said to have rebuilt the shrine.『Kadokawa Japan Place name Dictionary』. Another tradition claims that [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] rebuilt the shrine during the [[1189|Oshu War]] in 1189.『Iwate Place names』. There is also a tradition stating that the shrine was founded during the [[Daido]] period (806-810), and another stating that [[Shiba Ienaga]] enshrined the deity from Fushimi Inari when he came to Shiwa District in [[Kenmu]] 2 (1335) as the commander of Oshu.『Shinto Dictionary』 Vol. 2, Heibonsha, 1939. The shrine received patronage from the Shiba clan, who ruled Shiwa District throughout the [[Muromachi period]]. In [[Tensho]] 16 (1588), [[Shiba Tsunenao]] built the shrine building,The shrine possesses a building plaque dated May 28, 1588. but after Tsunenao was ousted by [[Nanbu Nobunao]] that same year, the Nanbu clan became the shrine's patrons. After being attacked by Nobunao, Tsunenao hid in the shrine's affiliated temple, Joju-in, and then fled to Sanno-kai (a small basin in the upper reaches of the Takinakawa River, in present-day Tsuchidate, Shiwa Town). The Nanbu clan's patronage continued, with records showing that Nanbu Nobunao donated over 24 koku of rice fields in [[Keicho]] 4 (1599). Additional donations were made, including 20 koku from Hirasawa Village (present-day Hirasawa, Shiwa Town) in 1612, and another 10 koku in 1614. In [[Shoo]] 1 (1652), Nanbu Nobuaki donated 7 koku of shrine land. By [[Kaei]] 2 (1849), the total donations from the Nanbu domain amounted to 70 koku. festivals and other events were frequently attended by representatives from the Nanbu clan, confirming the shrine's status as a place of worship for the Nanbu family. In [[Tenpo]] 5 (1834), Nanbu Toshiharu constructed a pilgrimage road from [[Morioka]] to the shrine, modeled after the [[Nikko Kaido]], a distance of about 15 km (5 ri). The shrine was ranked as a [[Village Shrine]] in the early [[Meiji]] period and was elevated to a [[Prefectural Shrine]] in [[Taisho]] 7 (1918). After World War II, it joined the Association of Shinto Shrines and was designated as a Beppyo Shrine in [[Showa]] 32 (1957). == Rituals == Until the early Meiji period, the shrine was influenced by the syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism and was managed by the Shugen-do temple Joju-in. == Grounds == The shrine grounds feature a cedar tree over 1,000 years old, known as the "Inariyama Great Cedar," which is considered sacred. There is a custom of searching for white fox hair at the base of the cedar tree to pray for longevity. == References == {{Reflist}} == External Links == *[http://www.shiwa-oinarisan.jp Shiwa Inari Shrine] (official site) *[http://www.ganshinsei.jp/shiwachou/118shiwainarijinja.html Shiwa Inari Shrine] (Iwate Prefecture Shinto Youth Association) {{Shinto-stub}} == References == {{reflist}} {{Draft categories| }} {{Inari Faith}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗}} {{Source Wikipedia}} {{afc comment|1=see [[every:Shiwa Inari Shrine]] for the history[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 02:25, 31 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Inari Shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Iwate Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Shiwa, Iwate]] [[Category:History of Shiwa, Iwate]] [[Category:Inari shrines]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Unsorted]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]] [[Category: tier 1]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category: Completion tier 1]] [[Category:Shiwa Inari Shrine]] [[Category:bump1-19]] [[Category:refreshing categories imported on 2024-01-19]] [[Category:refreshing imported pages]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:Pages from everybodywiki under right title]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:completion tier 1]] [[Category:pages which might possibly be incomplete but are most likely complete]] [[Category:I think I cleared all the incomplete or blank pages earlier]] }} [[en:draft:Shiwa Inari Shrine]] [[sn:Shiwa Inari Shrine]] [[en:Shiwa Inari Shrine]] [[ja:志和稲荷神社]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Shiwa Inari Shrine}}

Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Shiwa Inari Shrine | image = [[File:志和稲荷神社 拝殿.jpg|thumb|Shiwa Inari Shrine Worship Hall]] | location = [[Iwate Prefecture]][[Shiwa District, Iwate|Shiwa District]][[Shiwa, Iwate|Shiwa Town]] Masuzawa Aza Maehira 17-1 | coordinates = {{Coord|39|33|32.1|N|141|04|40.2|E|scale:25000_region:JP-03_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deity = [[Ukanomitama|Ukanomitama no Okami]]
[[Sarutahiko|Sarutahiko no Okami]]
[[Oomiyanome no Okami]] | type = Former Prefectural Shrine, Beppyo Shrine | founded = Traditionally [[Tenki]] 5 (1057) | main hall= [[Irimoya-zukuri]] | alternate names = | Pilgrimage Sites = | festival = May 5 | Rites = [[Hatsuuma|Hatsuuma festival]] (First Horse Day of the Second Lunar Month) | map = {{location map|Japan Iwate|lat_deg=39|lat_min=33|lat_sec=32.1|lon_deg=141|lon_min=04|lon_sec=40.2|label=Shiwa Inari Shrine|position=center|width=200|float=center}} }} '''Shiwa Inari Shrine''' (志和稲荷神社) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located on the western foothills of the Masuzawa settlement in [[Shiwa Town]], [[Shiwa District, Iwate|Shiwa District]], [[Iwate Prefecture]], on the left bank of the [[Takinakawa River]]. It is a former prefectural shrine and currently a Beppyo Shrine under the Association of Shinto Shrines. Locally, it is sometimes considered one of the [[Inari shrine#Three Great Inari Shrines of Japan|Three Great Inari Shrines of Japan]]{{Cite web|url=https://www.lafrance.co.jp/company.htm |title=La France Onsen Kaisha|publisher=[[Shiwa Town]] [[Third Sector]] Shiwa Machizukuri Kikaku |accessdate=2018-11-16 }}{{Cite web|url=http://www.tsukinowa-iwate.com/press/index8p40.html |title=Featured Information on Meijo Kigan |publisher=[[Tsukinowa Sake Brewery]] |accessdate=2018-11-16 }} or one of the [[Inari shrine#Five Great Inari Shrines of Japan|Five Great Inari Shrines of Japan]]{{Cite web|url=http://www.iwatekeizai.org/watching/watching_201801/8066/ |title=Shiwa Inari Shrine in Shiwa Town |publisher=[[Iwate Bank]] Iwate Economic Research Institute |date=2018-01 |accessdate=2018-12-16 }}{{Citation needed|date=2018-12|title=Sources claiming Shiwa Inari Shrine as one of the Five Great Inari Shrines of Japan}}. Nearby, there is another shrine called [[Shiwa Koinari Shrine]]. == Enshrined Deities == The enshrined deities are [[Ukanomitama|Ukanomitama no Okami]] (Inari Okami), [[Sarutahiko|Sarutahiko no Okami]], and [[Oomiyanome no Okami]]. The shrine considers Oomiyanome no Okami to be another name for [[Ame-no-Uzume|Ame-no-Uzume no Mikoto]]. During the [[Edo period]], the shrine also worshipped [[Juichimen Kannon]] as its principal Buddha.『Goryobun Shato』. This document, compiled in the mid-18th century, lists temples and shrines within the Morioka domain. == History == According to tradition, the shrine was founded in [[Tenki]] 5 (1057) when [[Minamoto no Yoriyoshi]] enshrined a divided spirit from [[Fushimi Inari Taisha]] in Kyoto to pray for victory in his campaign against the Abe clan during the [[Former Nine Years' War]]. Later, the Fujiwara clan members who ruled Shiwa District, such as Hiramune Takanari, are said to have rebuilt the shrine.『Kadokawa Japan Place name Dictionary』. Another tradition claims that [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] rebuilt the shrine during the [[1189|Oshu War]] in 1189.『Iwate Place names』. There is also a tradition stating that the shrine was founded during the [[Daido]] period (806-810), and another stating that [[Shiba Ienaga]] enshrined the deity from Fushimi Inari when he came to Shiwa District in [[Kenmu]] 2 (1335) as the commander of Oshu.『Shinto Dictionary』 Vol. 2, Heibonsha, 1939. The shrine received patronage from the Shiba clan, who ruled Shiwa District throughout the [[Muromachi period]]. In [[Tensho]] 16 (1588), [[Shiba Tsunenao]] built the shrine building,The shrine possesses a building plaque dated May 28, 1588. but after Tsunenao was ousted by [[Nanbu Nobunao]] that same year, the Nanbu clan became the shrine's patrons. After being attacked by Nobunao, Tsunenao hid in the shrine's affiliated temple, Joju-in, and then fled to Sanno-kai (a small basin in the upper reaches of the Takinakawa River, in present-day Tsuchidate, Shiwa Town). The Nanbu clan's patronage continued, with records showing that Nanbu Nobunao donated over 24 koku of rice fields in [[Keicho]] 4 (1599). Additional donations were made, including 20 koku from Hirasawa Village (present-day Hirasawa, Shiwa Town) in 1612, and another 10 koku in 1614. In [[Shoo]] 1 (1652), Nanbu Nobuaki donated 7 koku of shrine land. By [[Kaei]] 2 (1849), the total donations from the Nanbu domain amounted to 70 koku. festivals and other events were frequently attended by representatives from the Nanbu clan, confirming the shrine's status as a place of worship for the Nanbu family. In [[Tenpo]] 5 (1834), Nanbu Toshiharu constructed a pilgrimage road from [[Morioka]] to the shrine, modeled after the [[Nikko Kaido]], a distance of about 15 km (5 ri). The shrine was ranked as a [[Village Shrine]] in the early [[Meiji]] period and was elevated to a [[Prefectural Shrine]] in [[Taisho]] 7 (1918). After World War II, it joined the Association of Shinto Shrines and was designated as a Beppyo Shrine in [[Showa]] 32 (1957). == Rituals == Until the early Meiji period, the shrine was influenced by the syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism and was managed by the Shugen-do temple Joju-in. == Grounds == The shrine grounds feature a cedar tree over 1,000 years old, known as the "Inariyama Great Cedar," which is considered sacred. There is a custom of searching for white fox hair at the base of the cedar tree to pray for longevity. == References == {{Reflist}} == External Links == *[http://www.shiwa-oinarisan.jp Shiwa Inari Shrine] (official site) *[http://www.ganshinsei.jp/shiwachou/118shiwainarijinja.html Shiwa Inari Shrine] (Iwate Prefecture Shinto Youth Association) {{Shinto-stub}} == References == {{reflist}} {{Draft categories| }} {{Inari Faith}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗}} {{Source Wikipedia}} {{afc comment|1=see [[every:Shiwa Inari Shrine]] for the history[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 02:25, 31 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Inari Shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Iwate Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Shiwa, Iwate]] [[Category:History of Shiwa, Iwate]] [[Category:Inari shrines]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Unsorted]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]] [[Category: tier 1]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category: Completion tier 1]] [[Category:Shiwa Inari Shrine]] [[Category:bump1-19]] [[Category:refreshing categories imported on 2024-01-19]] [[Category:refreshing imported pages]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:Pages from everybodywiki under right title]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:completion tier 1]] [[Category:pages which might possibly be incomplete but are most likely complete]] [[Category:I think I cleared all the incomplete or blank pages earlier]] }} [[en:draft:Shiwa Inari Shrine]] [[sn:Shiwa Inari Shrine]] [[en:Shiwa Inari Shrine]] [[ja:志和稲荷神社]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Shiwa Inari Shrine}}

The shrine received patronage from the Shiba clan, who ruled Shiwa District throughout the [[Muromachi period]]. In [[Tensho]] 16 (1588), [[Shiba Tsunenao]] built the shrine building,The shrine possesses a building plaque dated May 28, 1588. but after Tsunenao was ousted by [[Nanbu Nobunao]] that same year, the Nanbu clan became the shrine's patrons. After being attacked by Nobunao, Tsunenao hid in the shrine's affiliated temple, Joju-in, and then fled to Sanno-kai (a small basin in the upper reaches of the Takinakawa River, in present-day Tsuchidate, Shiwa Town). The Nanbu clan's patronage continued, with records showing that Nanbu Nobunao donated over 24 koku of rice fields in [[Keicho]] 4 (1599). Additional donations were made, including 20 koku from Hirasawa Village (present-day Hirasawa, Shiwa Town) in 1612, and another 10 koku in 1614. In [[Shoo]] 1 (1652), Nanbu Nobuaki donated 7 koku of shrine land. By [[Kaei]] 2 (1849), the total donations from the Nanbu domain amounted to 70 koku. festivals and other events were frequently attended by representatives from the Nanbu clan, confirming the shrine's status as a place of worship for the Nanbu family. In [[Tenpo]] 5 (1834), Nanbu Toshiharu constructed a pilgrimage road from [[Morioka]] to the shrine, modeled after the [[Nikko Kaido]], a distance of about 15 km (5 ri). The shrine was ranked as a [[Village Shrine]] in the early [[Meiji]] period and was elevated to a [[Prefectural Shrine]] in [[Taisho]] 7 (1918). After World War II, it joined the Association of Shinto Shrines and was designated as a Beppyo Shrine in [[Showa]] 32 (1957). == Rituals == Until the early Meiji period, the shrine was influenced by the syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism and was managed by the Shugen-do temple Joju-in. == Grounds == The shrine grounds feature a cedar tree over 1,000 years old, known as the "Inariyama Great Cedar," which is considered sacred. There is a custom of searching for white fox hair at the base of the cedar tree to pray for longevity. == References == {{Reflist}} == External Links == *[http://www.shiwa-oinarisan.jp Shiwa Inari Shrine] (official site) *[http://www.ganshinsei.jp/shiwachou/118shiwainarijinja.html Shiwa Inari Shrine] (Iwate Prefecture Shinto Youth Association) {{Shinto-stub}} == References == {{reflist}} {{Draft categories| }} {{Inari Faith}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗}} {{Source Wikipedia}} {{afc comment|1=see [[every:Shiwa Inari Shrine]] for the history[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 02:25, 31 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Inari Shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Iwate Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Shiwa, Iwate]] [[Category:History of Shiwa, Iwate]] [[Category:Inari shrines]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Unsorted]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]] [[Category: tier 1]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category: Completion tier 1]] [[Category:Shiwa Inari Shrine]] [[Category:refreshing categories imported on 2024-01-19]] [[Category:refreshing imported pages]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:Pages from everybodywiki under right title]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:completion tier 1]] [[Category:pages which might possibly be incomplete but are most likely complete]] [[Category:I think I cleared all the incomplete or blank pages earlier]] }} [[en:draft:Shiwa Inari Shrine]] [[sn:Shiwa Inari Shrine]] [[en:Shiwa Inari Shrine]] [[ja:志和稲荷神社]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Shiwa Inari Shrine}}

[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 21:01, 16 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created by Immanuelle as a wikipedia draft. It was then automatically transferred to everybodywiki and recovered from there. In the future it will be good to import the revisions. Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Shiwa Inari Shrine | image = [[File:志和稲荷神社 拝殿.jpg|thumb|Shiwa Inari Shrine Worship Hall]] | location = [[Iwate Prefecture]][[Shiwa District, Iwate|Shiwa District]][[Shiwa, Iwate|Shiwa Town]] Masuzawa Aza Maehira 17-1 | coordinates = {{Coord|39|33|32.1|N|141|04|40.2|E|scale:25000_region:JP-03_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deity = [[Ukanomitama|Ukanomitama no Okami]]
[[Sarutahiko|Sarutahiko no Okami]]
[[Oomiyanome no Okami]] | type = Former Prefectural Shrine, Beppyo Shrine | founded = Traditionally [[Tenki]] 5 (1057) | main hall= [[Irimoya-zukuri]] | alternate names = | Pilgrimage Sites = | festival = May 5 | Rites = [[Hatsuuma|Hatsuuma festival]] (First Horse Day of the Second Lunar Month) | map = {{location map|Japan Iwate|lat_deg=39|lat_min=33|lat_sec=32.1|lon_deg=141|lon_min=04|lon_sec=40.2|label=Shiwa Inari Shrine|position=center|width=200|float=center}} }} '''Shiwa Inari Shrine''' (志和稲荷神社) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located on the western foothills of the Masuzawa settlement in [[Shiwa Town]], [[Shiwa District, Iwate|Shiwa District]], [[Iwate Prefecture]], on the left bank of the [[Takinakawa River]]. It is a former prefectural shrine and currently a Beppyo Shrine under the Association of Shinto Shrines. Locally, it is sometimes considered one of the [[Inari shrine#Three Great Inari Shrines of Japan|Three Great Inari Shrines of Japan]]{{Cite web|url=https://www.lafrance.co.jp/company.htm |title=La France Onsen Kaisha|publisher=[[Shiwa Town]] [[Third Sector]] Shiwa Machizukuri Kikaku |accessdate=2018-11-16 }}{{Cite web|url=http://www.tsukinowa-iwate.com/press/index8p40.html |title=Featured Information on Meijo Kigan |publisher=[[Tsukinowa Sake Brewery]] |accessdate=2018-11-16 }} or one of the [[Inari shrine#Five Great Inari Shrines of Japan|Five Great Inari Shrines of Japan]]{{Cite web|url=http://www.iwatekeizai.org/watching/watching_201801/8066/ |title=Shiwa Inari Shrine in Shiwa Town |publisher=[[Iwate Bank]] Iwate Economic Research Institute |date=2018-01 |accessdate=2018-12-16 }}{{Citation needed|date=2018-12|title=Sources claiming Shiwa Inari Shrine as one of the Five Great Inari Shrines of Japan}}. Nearby, there is another shrine called [[Shiwa Koinari Shrine]]. == Enshrined Deities == The enshrined deities are [[Ukanomitama|Ukanomitama no Okami]] (Inari Okami), [[Sarutahiko|Sarutahiko no Okami]], and [[Oomiyanome no Okami]]. The shrine considers Oomiyanome no Okami to be another name for [[Ame-no-Uzume|Ame-no-Uzume no Mikoto]]. During the [[Edo period]], the shrine also worshipped [[Juichimen Kannon]] as its principal Buddha.『Goryobun Shato』. This document, compiled in the mid-18th century, lists temples and shrines within the Morioka domain. == History == According to tradition, the shrine was founded in [[Tenki]] 5 (1057) when [[Minamoto no Yoriyoshi]] enshrined a divided spirit from [[Fushimi Inari Taisha]] in Kyoto to pray for victory in his campaign against the Abe clan during the [[Former Nine Years' War]]. Later, the Fujiwara clan members who ruled Shiwa District, such as Hiramune Takanari, are said to have rebuilt the shrine.『Kadokawa Japan Place name Dictionary』. Another tradition claims that [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] rebuilt the shrine during the [[1189|Oshu War]] in 1189.『Iwate Place names』. There is also a tradition stating that the shrine was founded during the [[Daido]] period (806-810), and another stating that [[Shiba Ienaga]] enshrined the deity from Fushimi Inari when he came to Shiwa District in [[Kenmu]] 2 (1335) as the commander of Oshu.『Shinto Dictionary』 Vol. 2, Heibonsha, 1939. The shrine received patronage from the Shiba clan, who ruled Shiwa District throughout the [[Muromachi period]]. In [[Tensho]] 16 (1588), [[Shiba Tsunenao]] built the shrine building,The shrine possesses a building plaque dated May 28, 1588. but after Tsunenao was ousted by [[Nanbu Nobunao]] that same year, the Nanbu clan became the shrine's patrons. After being attacked by Nobunao, Tsunenao hid in the shrine's affiliated temple, Joju-in, and then fled to Sanno-kai (a small basin in the upper reaches of the Takinakawa River, in present-day Tsuchidate, Shiwa Town). The Nanbu clan's patronage continued, with records showing that Nanbu Nobunao donated over 24 koku of rice fields in [[Keicho]] 4 (1599). Additional donations were made, including 20 koku from Hirasawa Village (present-day Hirasawa, Shiwa Town) in 1612, and another 10 koku in 1614. In [[Shoo]] 1 (1652), Nanbu Nobuaki donated 7 koku of shrine land. By [[Kaei]] 2 (1849), the total donations from the Nanbu domain amounted to 70 koku. festivals and other events were frequently attended by representatives from the Nanbu clan, confirming the shrine's status as a place of worship for the Nanbu family. In [[Tenpo]] 5 (1834), Nanbu Toshiharu constructed a pilgrimage road from [[Morioka]] to the shrine, modeled after the [[Nikko Kaido]], a distance of about 15 km (5 ri). The shrine was ranked as a [[Village Shrine]] in the early [[Meiji]] period and was elevated to a [[Prefectural Shrine]] in [[Taisho]] 7 (1918). After World War II, it joined the Association of Shinto Shrines and was designated as a Beppyo Shrine in [[Showa]] 32 (1957). == Rituals == Until the early Meiji period, the shrine was influenced by the syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism and was managed by the Shugen-do temple Joju-in. == Grounds == The shrine grounds feature a cedar tree over 1,000 years old, known as the "Inariyama Great Cedar," which is considered sacred. There is a custom of searching for white fox hair at the base of the cedar tree to pray for longevity. == References == {{Reflist}} == External Links == *[http://www.shiwa-oinarisan.jp Shiwa Inari Shrine] (official site) *[http://www.ganshinsei.jp/shiwachou/118shiwainarijinja.html Shiwa Inari Shrine] (Iwate Prefecture Shinto Youth Association) {{Shinto-stub}} == References == {{reflist}} {{Draft categories| }} {{Inari Faith}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗}} {{Source Wikipedia}} {{afc comment|1=see [[every:Shiwa Inari Shrine]] for the history[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 02:25, 31 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Inari Shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Iwate Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Shiwa, Iwate]] [[Category:History of Shiwa, Iwate]] [[Category:Inari shrines]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Unsorted]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]] [[Category: tier 1]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category: Completion tier 1]] [[Category:Shiwa Inari Shrine]] [[Category:refreshing categories imported on 2024-01-19]] [[Category:refreshing imported pages]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:Pages from everybodywiki under right title]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:completion tier 1]] [[Category:pages which might possibly be incomplete but are most likely complete]] }} [[en:draft:Shiwa Inari Shrine]] [[sn:Shiwa Inari Shrine]] [[en:Shiwa Inari Shrine]] [[ja:志和稲荷神社]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Shiwa Inari Shrine}}

Additional donations were made, including 20 koku from Hirasawa Village (present-day Hirasawa, Shiwa Town) in 1612, and another 10 koku in 1614. In [[Shoo]] 1 (1652), Nanbu Nobuaki donated 7 koku of shrine land. By [[Kaei]] 2 (1849), the total donations from the Nanbu domain amounted to 70 koku. festivals and other events were frequently attended by representatives from the Nanbu clan, confirming the shrine's status as a place of worship for the Nanbu family. In [[Tenpo]] 5 (1834), Nanbu Toshiharu constructed a pilgrimage road from [[Morioka]] to the shrine, modeled after the [[Nikko Kaido]], a distance of about 15 km (5 ri). The shrine was ranked as a [[Village Shrine]] in the early [[Meiji]] period and was elevated to a [[Prefectural Shrine]] in [[Taisho]] 7 (1918). After World War II, it joined the Association of Shinto Shrines and was designated as a Beppyo Shrine in [[Showa]] 32 (1957). == Rituals == Until the early Meiji period, the shrine was influenced by the syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism and was managed by the Shugen-do temple Joju-in. == Grounds == The shrine grounds feature a cedar tree over 1,000 years old, known as the "Inariyama Great Cedar," which is considered sacred. There is a custom of searching for white fox hair at the base of the cedar tree to pray for longevity. == References == {{Reflist}} == External Links == *[http://www.shiwa-oinarisan.jp Shiwa Inari Shrine] (official site) *[http://www.ganshinsei.jp/shiwachou/118shiwainarijinja.html Shiwa Inari Shrine] (Iwate Prefecture Shinto Youth Association) {{Shinto-stub}} == References == {{reflist}} {{Draft categories| }} {{Inari Faith}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗}} {{Source Wikipedia}} {{afc comment|1=see [[every:Shiwa Inari Shrine]] for the history[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 02:25, 31 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Inari Shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Iwate Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Shiwa, Iwate]] [[Category:History of Shiwa, Iwate]] [[Category:Inari shrines]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Unsorted]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category: Completion tier 1]] [[Category:Shiwa Inari Shrine]] [[Category:refreshing categories imported on 2024-01-19]] [[Category:refreshing imported pages]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:Pages from everybodywiki under right title]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:completion tier 1]] }} [[en:draft:Shiwa Inari Shrine]] [[sn:Shiwa Inari Shrine]] [[en:Shiwa Inari Shrine]] [[ja:志和稲荷神社]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Shiwa Inari Shrine}}
