{{draft}} {{Promising draft|reason=the high amount of work put into it over a long time, see edit history}} {{afc comment|1=Page originally made on english wikipedia in draftspace. g13 deleted and archived on everybodywiki. Go to [[:en:Wikipedia:Requests for undeletion]] to request undeletion of original page [[:en:draft:Shiwa Inari Shrine]] and import the revisions to here for attribution. View on everybodywiki here [[:every:Shiwa Inari Shrine|Shiwa Inari Shrine]]. [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 21:01, 16 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created by Immanuelle as a wikipedia draft. It was then automatically transferred to everybodywiki and recovered from there. In the future it will be good to import the revisions. Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Shiwa Inari Shrine | image = [[File:志和稲荷神社 拝殿.jpg|thumb|Shiwa Inari Shrine Worship Hall]] | location = [[Iwate Prefecture]][[Shiwa District, Iwate|Shiwa District]][[Shiwa, Iwate|Shiwa Town]] Masuzawa Aza Maehira 17-1 | coordinates = {{Coord|39|33|32.1|N|141|04|40.2|E|scale:25000_region:JP-03_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deity = [[Ukanomitama|Ukanomitama no Okami]]
[[Sarutahiko|Sarutahiko no Okami]]
[[Oomiyanome no Okami]] | type = Former Prefectural Shrine, Beppyo Shrine | founded = Traditionally [[Tenki]] 5 (1057) | main hall= [[Irimoya-zukuri]] | alternate names = | Pilgrimage Sites = | festival = May 5 | Rites = [[Hatsuuma|Hatsuuma festival]] (First Horse Day of the Second Lunar Month) | map = {{location map|Japan Iwate|lat_deg=39|lat_min=33|lat_sec=32.1|lon_deg=141|lon_min=04|lon_sec=40.2|label=Shiwa Inari Shrine|position=center|width=200|float=center}} }} '''Shiwa Inari Shrine''' (志和稲荷神社) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located on the western foothills of the Masuzawa settlement in [[Shiwa Town]], [[Shiwa District, Iwate|Shiwa District]], [[Iwate Prefecture]], on the left bank of the [[Takinakawa River]]. It is a former prefectural shrine and currently a Beppyo Shrine under the Association of Shinto Shrines. Locally, it is sometimes considered one of the [[Inari shrine#Three Great Inari Shrines of Japan|Three Great Inari Shrines of Japan]]{{Cite web|url=https://www.lafrance.co.jp/company.htm |title=La France Onsen Kaisha|publisher=[[Shiwa Town]] [[Third Sector]] Shiwa Machizukuri Kikaku |accessdate=2018-11-16 }}{{Cite web|url=http://www.tsukinowa-iwate.com/press/index8p40.html |title=Featured Information on Meijo Kigan |publisher=[[Tsukinowa Sake Brewery]] |accessdate=2018-11-16 }} or one of the [[Inari shrine#Five Great Inari Shrines of Japan|Five Great Inari Shrines of Japan]]{{Cite web|url=http://www.iwatekeizai.org/watching/watching_201801/8066/ |title=Shiwa Inari Shrine in Shiwa Town |publisher=[[Iwate Bank]] Iwate Economic Research Institute |date=2018-01 |accessdate=2018-12-16 }}{{Citation needed|date=2018-12|title=Sources claiming Shiwa Inari Shrine as one of the Five Great Inari Shrines of Japan}}. Nearby, there is another shrine called [[Shiwa Koinari Shrine]]. == Enshrined Deities == The enshrined deities are [[Ukanomitama|Ukanomitama no Okami]] (Inari Okami), [[Sarutahiko|Sarutahiko no Okami]], and [[Oomiyanome no Okami]]. The shrine considers Oomiyanome no Okami to be another name for [[Ame-no-Uzume|Ame-no-Uzume no Mikoto]]. During the [[Edo period]], the shrine also worshipped [[Juichimen Kannon]] as its principal Buddha.『Goryobun Shato』. This document, compiled in the mid-18th century, lists temples and shrines within the Morioka domain. == History == According to tradition, the shrine was founded in [[Tenki]] 5 (1057) when [[Minamoto no Yoriyoshi]] enshrined a divided spirit from [[Fushimi Inari Taisha]] in Kyoto to pray for victory in his campaign against the Abe clan during the [[Former Nine Years' War]]. Later, the Fujiwara clan members who ruled Shiwa District, such as Hiramune Takanari, are said to have rebuilt the shrine.『Kadokawa Japan Place name Dictionary』. Another tradition claims that [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] rebuilt the shrine during the [[1189|Oshu War]] in 1189.『Iwate Place names』. There is also a tradition stating that the shrine was founded during the [[Daido]] period (806-810), and another stating that [[Shiba Ienaga]] enshrined the deity from Fushimi Inari when he came to Shiwa District in [[Kenmu]] 2 (1335) as the commander of Oshu.『Shinto Dictionary』 Vol. 2, Heibonsha, 1939. The shrine received patronage from the Shiba clan, who ruled Shiwa District throughout the [[Muromachi period]]. In [[Tensho]] 16 (1588), [[Shiba Tsunenao]] built the shrine building,The shrine possesses a building plaque dated May 28, 1588. but after Tsunenao was ousted by [[Nanbu Nobunao]] that same year, the Nanbu clan became the shrine's patrons. After being attacked by Nobunao, Tsunenao hid in the shrine's affiliated temple, Joju-in, and then fled to Sanno-kai (a small basin in the upper reaches of the Takinakawa River, in present-day Tsuchidate, Shiwa Town). The Nanbu clan's patronage continued, with records showing that Nanbu Nobunao donated over 24 koku of rice fields in [[Keicho]] 4 (1599). Additional donations were made, including 20 koku from Hirasawa Village (present-day Hirasawa, Shiwa Town) in 1612, and another 10 koku in 1614. In [[Shoo]] 1 (1652), Nanbu Nobuaki donated 7 koku of shrine land. By [[Kaei]] 2 (1849), the total donations from the Nanbu domain amounted to 70 koku. festivals and other events were frequently attended by representatives from the Nanbu clan, confirming the shrine's status as a place of worship for the Nanbu family. In [[Tenpo]] 5 (1834), Nanbu Toshiharu constructed a pilgrimage road from [[Morioka]] to the shrine, modeled after the [[Nikko Kaido]], a distance of about 15 km (5 ri). The shrine was ranked as a [[Village Shrine]] in the early [[Meiji]] period and was elevated to a [[Prefectural Shrine]] in [[Taisho]] 7 (1918). After World War II, it joined the Association of Shinto Shrines and was designated as a Beppyo Shrine in [[Showa]] 32 (1957). == Rituals == Until the early Meiji period, the shrine was influenced by the syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism and was managed by the Shugen-do temple Joju-in. == Grounds == The shrine grounds feature a cedar tree over 1,000 years old, known as the "Inariyama Great Cedar," which is considered sacred. There is a custom of searching for white fox hair at the base of the cedar tree to pray for longevity. == References == {{Reflist}} == External Links == *[http://www.shiwa-oinarisan.jp Shiwa Inari Shrine] (official site) *[http://www.ganshinsei.jp/shiwachou/118shiwainarijinja.html Shiwa Inari Shrine] (Iwate Prefecture Shinto Youth Association) {{Shinto-stub}} == References == {{reflist}} {{Draft categories| }} {{Inari Faith}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗}} {{Source Wikipedia}} {{afc comment|1=see [[every:Shiwa Inari Shrine]] for the history[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 02:25, 31 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Inari Shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Iwate Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Shiwa, Iwate]] [[Category:History of Shiwa, Iwate]] [[Category:Inari shrines]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Unsorted]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]] [[Category: Completion tier 1]] [[Category:Shiwa Inari Shrine]] [[Category:refreshing categories imported on 2024-01-19]] [[Category:refreshing imported pages]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:Pages from everybodywiki under right title]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:completion tier 1]] }} [[en:draft:Shiwa Inari Shrine]] [[sn:Shiwa Inari Shrine]] [[en:Shiwa Inari Shrine]] [[ja:志和稲荷神社]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Shiwa Inari Shrine}}

There is a custom of searching for white fox hair at the base of the cedar tree to pray for longevity. == References == {{Reflist}} == External Links == *[http://www.shiwa-oinarisan.jp Shiwa Inari Shrine] (official site) *[http://www.ganshinsei.jp/shiwachou/118shiwainarijinja.html Shiwa Inari Shrine] (Iwate Prefecture Shinto Youth Association) {{Shinto-stub}} == References == {{reflist}} {{Draft categories| }} {{Inari Faith}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗}} {{Source Wikipedia}} {{afc comment|1=see [[every:Shiwa Inari Shrine]] for the history[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 02:25, 31 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Inari Shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Iwate Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Shiwa, Iwate]] [[Category:History of Shiwa, Iwate]] [[Category:Inari shrines]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Unsorted]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]] [[Category: Completion tier 1]] [[Category:Shiwa Inari Shrine]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:Pages from everybodywiki under right title]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:completion tier 1]] }} [[en:draft:Shiwa Inari Shrine]] [[sn:Shiwa Inari Shrine]] [[en:Shiwa Inari Shrine]] [[ja:志和稲荷神社]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Shiwa Inari Shrine}}

revision.text:
{{draft}} {{Promising draft|reason=the high amount of work put into it over a long time, see edit history}} {{afc comment|1=Page originally made on english wikipedia in draftspace. g13 deleted and archived on everybodywiki. Go to [[:en:Wikipedia:Requests for undeletion]] to request undeletion of original page [[:en:draft:Shiwa Inari Shrine]] and import the revisions to here for attribution. View on everybodywiki here [[:every:Shiwa Inari Shrine|Shiwa Inari Shrine]]. [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 21:01, 16 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created by Immanuelle as a wikipedia draft. It was then automatically transferred to everybodywiki and recovered from there. In the future it will be good to import the revisions. Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Shiwa Inari Shrine | image = [[File:志和稲荷神社 拝殿.jpg|thumb|Shiwa Inari Shrine Worship Hall]] | location = [[Iwate Prefecture]][[Shiwa District, Iwate|Shiwa District]][[Shiwa, Iwate|Shiwa Town]] Masuzawa Aza Maehira 17-1 | coordinates = {{Coord|39|33|32.1|N|141|04|40.2|E|scale:25000_region:JP-03_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deity = [[Ukanomitama|Ukanomitama no Okami]]
[[Sarutahiko|Sarutahiko no Okami]]
[[Oomiyanome no Okami]] | type = Former Prefectural Shrine, Beppyo Shrine | founded = Traditionally [[Tenki]] 5 (1057) | main hall= [[Irimoya-zukuri]] | alternate names = | Pilgrimage Sites = | festival = May 5 | Rites = [[Hatsuuma|Hatsuuma festival]] (First Horse Day of the Second Lunar Month) | map = {{location map|Japan Iwate|lat_deg=39|lat_min=33|lat_sec=32.1|lon_deg=141|lon_min=04|lon_sec=40.2|label=Shiwa Inari Shrine|position=center|width=200|float=center}} }} '''Shiwa Inari Shrine''' (志和稲荷神社) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located on the western foothills of the Masuzawa settlement in [[Shiwa Town]], [[Shiwa District, Iwate|Shiwa District]], [[Iwate Prefecture]], on the left bank of the [[Takinakawa River]]. It is a former prefectural shrine and currently a Beppyo Shrine under the Association of Shinto Shrines. Locally, it is sometimes considered one of the [[Inari shrine#Three Great Inari Shrines of Japan|Three Great Inari Shrines of Japan]]{{Cite web|url=https://www.lafrance.co.jp/company.htm |title=La France Onsen Kaisha|publisher=[[Shiwa Town]] [[Third Sector]] Shiwa Machizukuri Kikaku |accessdate=2018-11-16 }}{{Cite web|url=http://www.tsukinowa-iwate.com/press/index8p40.html |title=Featured Information on Meijo Kigan |publisher=[[Tsukinowa Sake Brewery]] |accessdate=2018-11-16 }} or one of the [[Inari shrine#Five Great Inari Shrines of Japan|Five Great Inari Shrines of Japan]]{{Cite web|url=http://www.iwatekeizai.org/watching/watching_201801/8066/ |title=Shiwa Inari Shrine in Shiwa Town |publisher=[[Iwate Bank]] Iwate Economic Research Institute |date=2018-01 |accessdate=2018-12-16 }}{{Citation needed|date=2018-12|title=Sources claiming Shiwa Inari Shrine as one of the Five Great Inari Shrines of Japan}}. Nearby, there is another shrine called [[Shiwa Koinari Shrine]]. == Enshrined Deities == The enshrined deities are [[Ukanomitama|Ukanomitama no Okami]] (Inari Okami), [[Sarutahiko|Sarutahiko no Okami]], and [[Oomiyanome no Okami]]. The shrine considers Oomiyanome no Okami to be another name for [[Ame-no-Uzume|Ame-no-Uzume no Mikoto]]. During the [[Edo period]], the shrine also worshipped [[Juichimen Kannon]] as its principal Buddha.『Goryobun Shato』. This document, compiled in the mid-18th century, lists temples and shrines within the Morioka domain. == History == According to tradition, the shrine was founded in [[Tenki]] 5 (1057) when [[Minamoto no Yoriyoshi]] enshrined a divided spirit from [[Fushimi Inari Taisha]] in Kyoto to pray for victory in his campaign against the Abe clan during the [[Former Nine Years' War]]. Later, the Fujiwara clan members who ruled Shiwa District, such as Hiramune Takanari, are said to have rebuilt the shrine.『Kadokawa Japan Place name Dictionary』. Another tradition claims that [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] rebuilt the shrine during the [[1189|Oshu War]] in 1189.『Iwate Place names』. There is also a tradition stating that the shrine was founded during the [[Daido]] period (806-810), and another stating that [[Shiba Ienaga]] enshrined the deity from Fushimi Inari when he came to Shiwa District in [[Kenmu]] 2 (1335) as the commander of Oshu.『Shinto Dictionary』 Vol. 2, Heibonsha, 1939. The shrine received patronage from the Shiba clan, who ruled Shiwa District throughout the [[Muromachi period]]. In [[Tensho]] 16 (1588), [[Shiba Tsunenao]] built the shrine building,The shrine possesses a building plaque dated May 28, 1588. but after Tsunenao was ousted by [[Nanbu Nobunao]] that same year, the Nanbu clan became the shrine's patrons. After being attacked by Nobunao, Tsunenao hid in the shrine's affiliated temple, Joju-in, and then fled to Sanno-kai (a small basin in the upper reaches of the Takinakawa River, in present-day Tsuchidate, Shiwa Town). The Nanbu clan's patronage continued, with records showing that Nanbu Nobunao donated over 24 koku of rice fields in [[Keicho]] 4 (1599). Additional donations were made, including 20 koku from Hirasawa Village (present-day Hirasawa, Shiwa Town) in 1612, and another 10 koku in 1614. In [[Shoo]] 1 (1652), Nanbu Nobuaki donated 7 koku of shrine land. By [[Kaei]] 2 (1849), the total donations from the Nanbu domain amounted to 70 koku. festivals and other events were frequently attended by representatives from the Nanbu clan, confirming the shrine's status as a place of worship for the Nanbu family. In [[Tenpo]] 5 (1834), Nanbu Toshiharu constructed a pilgrimage road from [[Morioka]] to the shrine, modeled after the [[Nikko Kaido]], a distance of about 15 km (5 ri). The shrine was ranked as a [[Village Shrine]] in the early [[Meiji]] period and was elevated to a [[Prefectural Shrine]] in [[Taisho]] 7 (1918). After World War II, it joined the Association of Shinto Shrines and was designated as a Beppyo Shrine in [[Showa]] 32 (1957). == Rituals == Until the early Meiji period, the shrine was influenced by the syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism and was managed by the Shugen-do temple Joju-in. == Grounds == The shrine grounds feature a cedar tree over 1,000 years old, known as the "Inariyama Great Cedar," which is considered sacred. There is a custom of searching for white fox hair at the base of the cedar tree to pray for longevity. == References == {{Reflist}} == External Links == *[http://www.shiwa-oinarisan.jp Shiwa Inari Shrine] (official site) *[http://www.ganshinsei.jp/shiwachou/118shiwainarijinja.html Shiwa Inari Shrine] (Iwate Prefecture Shinto Youth Association) {{Shinto-stub}} == References == {{reflist}} {{Draft categories| }} {{Inari Faith}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗}} {{Source Wikipedia}} {{afc comment|1=see [[every:Shiwa Inari Shrine]] for the history[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 02:25, 31 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Inari Shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Iwate Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Shiwa, Iwate]] [[Category:History of Shiwa, Iwate]] [[Category:Inari shrines]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]] [[Category: Completion tier 1]] [[Category:Shiwa Inari Shrine]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:Pages from everybodywiki under right title]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:completion tier 1]] }} [[en:draft:Shiwa Inari Shrine]] [[sn:Shiwa Inari Shrine]] [[en:Shiwa Inari Shrine]] [[ja:志和稲荷神社]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Shiwa Inari Shrine}}

The shrine was ranked as a [[Village Shrine]] in the early [[Meiji]] period and was elevated to a [[Prefectural Shrine]] in [[Taisho]] 7 (1918). After World War II, it joined the Association of Shinto Shrines and was designated as a Beppyo Shrine in [[Showa]] 32 (1957). == Rituals == Until the early Meiji period, the shrine was influenced by the syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism and was managed by the Shugen-do temple Joju-in. == Grounds == The shrine grounds feature a cedar tree over 1,000 years old, known as the "Inariyama Great Cedar," which is considered sacred. There is a custom of searching for white fox hair at the base of the cedar tree to pray for longevity. == References == {{Reflist}} == External Links == *[http://www.shiwa-oinarisan.jp Shiwa Inari Shrine] (official site) *[http://www.ganshinsei.jp/shiwachou/118shiwainarijinja.html Shiwa Inari Shrine] (Iwate Prefecture Shinto Youth Association) {{Shinto-stub}} == References == {{reflist}} {{Draft categories| }} {{Inari Faith}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗}} {{Source Wikipedia}} {{afc comment|1=see [[every:Shiwa Inari Shrine]] for the history[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 02:25, 31 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Inari Shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Iwate Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Shiwa, Iwate]] [[Category:History of Shiwa, Iwate]] [[Category:Inari shrines]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]] [[Category: Completion tier 1]] [[Category:Shiwa Inari Shrine]] [[Category:Pages from everybodywiki under right title]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]] }} [[en:draft:Shiwa Inari Shrine]] [[sn:Shiwa Inari Shrine]] [[en:Shiwa Inari Shrine]] [[ja:志和稲荷神社]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Shiwa Inari Shrine}}

revision.text:
{{神社 |名称 = 志和稲荷神社 |画像 = [[File:志和稲荷神社 拝殿.jpg|270px|志和稲荷神社]]
拝殿 |所在地 = [[岩手県]][[紫波郡]][[紫波町]]升沢字前平17-1 |位置 = {{Coord|39|33|32.1|N|141|04|40.2|E|scale:25000_region:JP-03_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |祭神 = [[宇迦之御魂大神]]
[[猿田彦大神]]
[[大宮能売大神]] |社格 = 旧[[県社]]・[[別表神社]] |創建 = 伝[[天喜]]5年（[[1057年]]） |本殿 = [[入母屋造]] |別名 = |札所等 = |例祭 = 5月5日 |神事 = [[初午|初午祭]]（旧暦2月初午） |地図={{Location map|Japan Iwate|lat_deg=39|lat_min=33|lat_sec=32.1|lon_deg=141|lon_min=04|lon_sec=40.2|label=志和
稲荷神社|position=center|width=200|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=10|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200|coord={{coord|39|33|32.1|N|141|04|40.2|E}}}} }} '''志和稲荷神社'''（しわいなりじんじゃ）は、[[岩手県]][[紫波郡]][[紫波町]]升沢部落の西部山麓、[[滝名川]]左岸に鎮座する[[神社]]である。[[旧社格]]は[[県社]]で、現在は[[神社本庁]]の[[別表神社]]。 地元では[[稲荷神#日本三大稲荷|日本三大稲荷]]{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.lafrance.co.jp/company.htm |title=ラ・フランス温泉館 会社概要 |publisher=[[紫波町]][[第三セクター]] 紫波まちづくり企画 |accessdate=2018-11-16 }}{{Cite web|和書|url=http://www.tsukinowa-iwate.com/press/index8p40.html |title=掲載情報紹介 銘醸祈願 |publisher=[[月の輪酒造店]] |accessdate=2018-11-16 }} や[[稲荷神#日本五大稲荷|日本五大稲荷]]{{Cite web|和書|url=http://www.iwatekeizai.org/watching/watching_201801/8066/ |title=志和稲荷神社　～紫波町～ |publisher=[[岩手銀行]] 岩手経済研究所 |date=2018-01 |accessdate=2018-12-16 }}{{要出典|date=2018年12月|title=志和稲荷神社が日本五大稲荷の一つであるとする文献}}の一つとされることがある。 すぐ近くには別に[[志和古稲荷神社]]も鎮座する。 == 祭神 == [[ウカノミタマ|宇迦之御魂大神]]（[[稲荷大神]]）、[[サルタヒコ|猿田彦大神]]、[[オオミヤノメ|大宮能売（おおみやのめ）大神]]を祭神とするが、神社では大宮能売大神は[[アメノウズメ|天宇受売命]]の別名としている。 [[江戸時代]]には[[十一面観音]]を[[本地仏]]としていた『御領分社堂』。同書は[[宝暦]]頃（18世紀中葉）成立の盛岡藩領内寺社の書き上げを纏めたもの。。 == 歴史 == [[天喜]]5年（[[1057年]]）、[[源頼義]]が[[安倍頼時]]、[[安倍貞任|貞任]]父子追討（[[前九年の役]]）の為に陣ヶ岡に布陣した際に戦勝を祈願して[[京都|京]]の[[伏見稲荷大社|伏見稲荷神社]]から[[分霊]]を[[勧請]]して創祀したと伝え、その後[[奥州藤原氏|藤原氏]]の一族で[[斯波郡]]を領した樋爪俊衡等が再建したとも『角川日本地名大辞典』。、[[文治]]5年（[[1189年]]）の[[奥州合戦]]に際して[[源頼朝]]が再建したともいう『岩手県の地名』。。また、{{要出典範囲|date=2011年4月|[[大同 (日本)|大同]]年間（806年 - 810年）の創建とする伝承もあり}}、更に[[建武 (日本)|建武]]2年（[[1335年]]）に[[奥州総大将]]として斯波郡に下向した[[斯波家長]]が伏見稲荷を勧請したともいう『神道大辞典』第2巻、平凡社、昭和14年。。 [[室町時代]]を通じて斯波郡郡主である[[斯波氏]]から崇敬され、[[天正]]16年（[[1588年]]）には[[斯波詮直]]による社殿の造営も行われたが当神社所蔵同年5月28日付[[棟札]]。、同年詮直が[[南部信直]]に逐われると、[[南部氏]]が代わって崇敬するようになった。なお、信直に攻められた詮直は当神社の[[別当寺院]]であった成就院に一時身を潜め、その後山王海（滝名川上流の小盆地、現紫波町土館の山間部）へ逃亡したという。 南部氏による崇敬、保護は、[[慶長]]4年（[[1599年]]）の信直による田畑24[[石 (単位)|石]]余の寄進が初見で、その後同17年に平沢村（現紫波町平沢）から20石、翌々19年に10石余の地が加増されており、[[承応]]元年（[[1652年]]）には同氏[[分家]]の[[八戸直栄]]による[[社領]]7石の寄進があった他、その後の[[盛岡藩]]からの3度の寄進もあり、[[嘉永]]2年（[[1849年]]）時点での藩による寄進高は都合70石に及んでいた。また祭礼等には藩主（南部氏当主）の直参、或いは代参が頻繁に行われ、南部一族の[[祈願所]]的性格を有していた事が認められ、[[天保]]5年（[[1834年]]）に藩主[[南部利済|利済]]は手許金で[[日光街道]]に擬えた[[盛岡市|盛岡]]から当神社へ至る5[[里 (尺貫法)|里]]程（およそ15[[キロメートル|キロ]]）の[[参道]]「[[志和稲荷街道]]」を造成している（翌年完成）。 [[明治]]初年に[[村社]]に列格し、[[大正]]7年（[[1918年]]）6月に県社に昇格した。[[戦後]]は神社本庁に参加し、[[昭和]]32年（[[1957年]]）6月にその別表神社とされている。 == 祭祀 == 明治初年まで[[神仏混淆]]の影響で、[[修験道]]寺院である成就院が別当として管掌していた。 == 境内 == 境内には樹齢千年を超える[[杉|杉の木]]があり、「稲荷山大杉」と呼ばれ[[神木]]とされている。長寿を願って杉の根本から白狐の毛を探すという風習がある。 == 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist|2}} == 参考文献 == *『角川日本地名大辞典 岩手県』、角川書店、昭和60年ISBN 4-04-001030-2 *『岩手県の地名』（日本歴史地名大系第3巻）、平凡社、1990年ISBN 4-582-49003-4 == 外部リンク == *[http://www.shiwa-oinarisan.jp 志和稲荷神社]（神社公式） *[http://www.ganshinsei.jp/shiwachou/118shiwainarijinja.html 志和稲荷神社]（岩手県神道青年会） {{神道 横}} {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:しわいなりしんしや}} [[Category:稲荷神社]] [[Category:岩手県の神社]] [[Category:岩手県の旧県社]] [[Category:別表神社]] [[Category:紫波町の建築物]] [[Category:紫波町の歴史]]

revision.text:
{{afc comment|1=see for the history[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 02:25, 31 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=Page originally made on english wikipedia in draftspace. g13 deleted and archived on everybodywiki. Go to [[:en:Wikipedia:Requests for undeletion]] to request undeletion of original page [[:en:draft:Shiwa Inari Shrine]] and import the revisions to here for attribution. View on everybodywiki here [[:every:Shiwa Inari Shrine|Shiwa Inari Shrine]]. [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 21:01, 16 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created by Immanuelle as a wikipedia draft. It was then automatically transferred to everybodywiki and recovered from there. In the future it will be good to import the revisions. Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{draft}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Shiwa Inari Shrine | image = [[File:志和稲荷神社 拝殿.jpg|thumb|Shiwa Inari Shrine Worship Hall]] | location = [[Iwate Prefecture]][[Shiwa District, Iwate|Shiwa District]][[Shiwa, Iwate|Shiwa Town]] Masuzawa Aza Maehira 17-1 | coordinates = {{Coord|39|33|32.1|N|141|04|40.2|E|scale:25000_region:JP-03_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deity = [[Ukanomitama|Ukanomitama no Okami]]
[[Sarutahiko|Sarutahiko no Okami]]
[[Oomiyanome no Okami]] | type = Former Prefectural Shrine, Beppyo Shrine | founded = Traditionally [[Tenki]] 5 (1057) | main hall= [[Irimoya-zukuri]] | alternate names = | Pilgrimage Sites = | festival = May 5 | Rites = [[Hatsuuma|Hatsuuma festival]] (First Horse Day of the Second Lunar Month) | map = {{location map|Japan Iwate|lat_deg=39|lat_min=33|lat_sec=32.1|lon_deg=141|lon_min=04|lon_sec=40.2|label=Shiwa Inari Shrine|position=center|width=200|float=center}} }} '''Shiwa Inari Shrine''' (志和稲荷神社) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located on the western foothills of the Masuzawa settlement in [[Shiwa Town]], [[Shiwa District, Iwate|Shiwa District]], [[Iwate Prefecture]], on the left bank of the [[Takinakawa River]]. It is a former prefectural shrine and currently a Beppyo Shrine under the Association of Shinto Shrines. Locally, it is sometimes considered one of the [[Inari shrine#Three Great Inari Shrines of Japan|Three Great Inari Shrines of Japan]]{{Cite web|url=https://www.lafrance.co.jp/company.htm |title=La France Onsen Kaisha|publisher=[[Shiwa Town]] [[Third Sector]] Shiwa Machizukuri Kikaku |accessdate=2018-11-16 }}{{Cite web|url=http://www.tsukinowa-iwate.com/press/index8p40.html |title=Featured Information on Meijo Kigan |publisher=[[Tsukinowa Sake Brewery]] |accessdate=2018-11-16 }} or one of the [[Inari shrine#Five Great Inari Shrines of Japan|Five Great Inari Shrines of Japan]]{{Cite web|url=http://www.iwatekeizai.org/watching/watching_201801/8066/ |title=Shiwa Inari Shrine in Shiwa Town |publisher=[[Iwate Bank]] Iwate Economic Research Institute |date=2018-01 |accessdate=2018-12-16 }}{{Citation needed|date=2018-12|title=Sources claiming Shiwa Inari Shrine as one of the Five Great Inari Shrines of Japan}}. Nearby, there is another shrine called [[Shiwa Koinari Shrine]]. == Enshrined Deities == The enshrined deities are [[Ukanomitama|Ukanomitama no Okami]] (Inari Okami), [[Sarutahiko|Sarutahiko no Okami]], and [[Oomiyanome no Okami]]. The shrine considers Oomiyanome no Okami to be another name for [[Ame-no-Uzume|Ame-no-Uzume no Mikoto]]. During the [[Edo period]], the shrine also worshipped [[Juichimen Kannon]] as its principal Buddha.『Goryobun Shato』. This document, compiled in the mid-18th century, lists temples and shrines within the Morioka domain. == History == According to tradition, the shrine was founded in [[Tenki]] 5 (1057) when [[Minamoto no Yoriyoshi]] enshrined a divided spirit from [[Fushimi Inari Taisha]] in Kyoto to pray for victory in his campaign against the Abe clan during the [[Former Nine Years' War]]. Later, the Fujiwara clan members who ruled Shiwa District, such as Hiramune Takanari, are said to have rebuilt the shrine.『Kadokawa Japan Place name Dictionary』. Another tradition claims that [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] rebuilt the shrine during the [[1189|Oshu War]] in 1189.『Iwate Place names』. There is also a tradition stating that the shrine was founded during the [[Daido]] period (806-810), and another stating that [[Shiba Ienaga]] enshrined the deity from Fushimi Inari when he came to Shiwa District in [[Kenmu]] 2 (1335) as the commander of Oshu.『Shinto Dictionary』 Vol. 2, Heibonsha, 1939. The shrine received patronage from the Shiba clan, who ruled Shiwa District throughout the [[Muromachi period]]. In [[Tensho]] 16 (1588), [[Shiba Tsunenao]] built the shrine building,The shrine possesses a building plaque dated May 28, 1588. but after Tsunenao was ousted by [[Nanbu Nobunao]] that same year, the Nanbu clan became the shrine's patrons. After being attacked by Nobunao, Tsunenao hid in the shrine's affiliated temple, Joju-in, and then fled to Sanno-kai (a small basin in the upper reaches of the Takinakawa River, in present-day Tsuchidate, Shiwa Town). The Nanbu clan's patronage continued, with records showing that Nanbu Nobunao donated over 24 koku of rice fields in [[Keicho]] 4 (1599). Additional donations were made, including 20 koku from Hirasawa Village (present-day Hirasawa, Shiwa Town) in 1612, and another 10 koku in 1614. In [[Shoo]] 1 (1652), Nanbu Nobuaki donated 7 koku of shrine land. By [[Kaei]] 2 (1849), the total donations from the Nanbu domain amounted to 70 koku. festivals and other events were frequently attended by representatives from the Nanbu clan, confirming the shrine's status as a place of worship for the Nanbu family. In [[Tenpo]] 5 (1834), Nanbu Toshiharu constructed a pilgrimage road from [[Morioka]] to the shrine, modeled after the [[Nikko Kaido]], a distance of about 15 km (5 ri). The shrine was Ranked as a [[Village Shrine]] in the early [[Meiji]] period and was elevated to a [[Prefectural Shrine]] in [[Taisho]] 7 (1918). After World War II, it joined the Association of Shinto Shrines and was designated as a Beppyo Shrine in [[Showa]] 32 (1957). == Rituals == Until the early Meiji period, the shrine was influenced by the syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism and was managed by the Shugen-do temple Joju-in. == Grounds == The shrine grounds feature a cedar tree over 1,000 years old, known as the "Inariyama Great Cedar," which is considered sacred. There is a custom of searching for white fox hair at the base of the cedar tree to pray for longevity. == References == {{Reflist}} == External Links == *[http://www.shiwa-oinarisan.jp Shiwa Inari Shrine] (official site) *[http://www.ganshinsei.jp/shiwachou/118shiwainarijinja.html Shiwa Inari Shrine] (Iwate Prefecture Shinto Youth Association) {{Shinto-stub}} == References == {{reflist}} {{Inari Faith}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗}} {{Source Wikipedia}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Shiwa Inari Shrine}} {{translated page|ja|志和稲荷神社|version=101985462|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Inari Shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Iwate Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Shiwa, Iwate]] [[Category:History of Shiwa, Iwate]] [[Category:Inari shrines]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]] [[Category: Completion tier 1]] [[Category:Shiwa Inari Shrine]] [[Category:Pages from everybodywiki under right title]] [[Category:New]] }} [[every:Shiwa Inari Shrine]] [[en:draft:Shiwa Inari Shrine]] [[sn:Shiwa Inari Shrine]] [[en:Shiwa Inari Shrine]] [[ja:志和稲荷神社]]

[[Sarutahiko|Sarutahiko no Okami]]
[[Oomiyanome no Okami]] | type = Former Prefectural Shrine, Beppyo Shrine | founded = Traditionally [[Tenki]] 5 (1057) | main hall= [[Irimoya-zukuri]] | alternate names = | Pilgrimage Sites = | festival = May 5 | Rites = [[Hatsuuma|Hatsuuma festival]] (First Horse Day of the Second Lunar Month) | map = {{location map|Japan Iwate|lat_deg=39|lat_min=33|lat_sec=32.1|lon_deg=141|lon_min=04|lon_sec=40.2|label=Shiwa Inari Shrine|position=center|width=200|float=center}} }} '''Shiwa Inari Shrine''' (志和稲荷神社) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located on the western foothills of the Masuzawa settlement in [[Shiwa Town]], [[Shiwa District, Iwate|Shiwa District]], [[Iwate Prefecture]], on the left bank of the [[Takinakawa River]]. It is a former prefectural shrine and currently a Beppyo Shrine under the Association of Shinto Shrines. Locally, it is sometimes considered one of the [[Inari shrine#Three Great Inari Shrines of Japan|Three Great Inari Shrines of Japan]]{{Cite web|url=https://www.lafrance.co.jp/company.htm |title=La France Onsen Kaisha|publisher=[[Shiwa Town]] [[Third Sector]] Shiwa Machizukuri Kikaku |accessdate=2018-11-16 }}{{Cite web|url=http://www.tsukinowa-iwate.com/press/index8p40.html |title=Featured Information on Meijo Kigan |publisher=[[Tsukinowa Sake Brewery]] |accessdate=2018-11-16 }} or one of the [[Inari shrine#Five Great Inari Shrines of Japan|Five Great Inari Shrines of Japan]]{{Cite web|url=http://www.iwatekeizai.org/watching/watching_201801/8066/ |title=Shiwa Inari Shrine in Shiwa Town |publisher=[[Iwate Bank]] Iwate Economic Research Institute |date=2018-01 |accessdate=2018-12-16 }}{{Citation needed|date=2018-12|title=Sources claiming Shiwa Inari Shrine as one of the Five Great Inari Shrines of Japan}}. Nearby, there is another shrine called [[Shiwa Koinari Shrine]]. == Enshrined Deities == The enshrined deities are [[Ukanomitama|Ukanomitama no Okami]] (Inari Okami), [[Sarutahiko|Sarutahiko no Okami]], and [[Oomiyanome no Okami]]. Later, the Fujiwara clan members who ruled Shiwa District, such as Hiramune Takanari, are said to have rebuilt the shrine.『Kadokawa Japan Place name Dictionary』. Another tradition claims that [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] rebuilt the shrine during the [[1189|Oshu War]] in 1189.『Iwate Place names』. There is also a tradition stating that the shrine was founded during the [[Daido]] period (806-810), and another stating that [[Shiba Ienaga]] enshrined the deity from Fushimi Inari when he came to Shiwa District in [[Kenmu]] 2 (1335) as the commander of Oshu.『Shinto Dictionary』 Vol. 2, Heibonsha, 1939. The shrine received patronage from the Shiba clan, who ruled Shiwa District throughout the [[Muromachi period]]. In [[Tensho]] 16 (1588), [[Shiba Tsunenao]] built the shrine building,The shrine possesses a building plaque dated May 28, 1588. but after Tsunenao was ousted by [[Nanbu Nobunao]] that same year, the Nanbu clan became the shrine's patrons. After being attacked by Nobunao, Tsunenao hid in the shrine's affiliated temple, Joju-in, and then fled to Sanno-kai (a small basin in the upper reaches of the Takinakawa River, in present-day Tsuchidate, Shiwa Town). The Nanbu clan's patronage continued, with records showing that Nanbu Nobunao donated over 24 koku of rice fields in [[Keicho]] 4 (1599). Additional donations were made, including 20 koku from Hirasawa Village (present-day Hirasawa, Shiwa Town) in 1612, and another 10 koku in 1614. In [[Shoo]] 1 (1652), Nanbu Nobuaki donated 7 koku of shrine land. By [[Kaei]] 2 (1849), the total donations from the Nanbu domain amounted to 70 koku. festivals and other events were frequently attended by representatives from the Nanbu clan, confirming the shrine's status as a place of worship for the Nanbu family. In [[Tenpo]] 5 (1834), Nanbu Toshiharu constructed a pilgrimage road from [[Morioka]] to the shrine, modeled after the [[Nikko Kaido]], a distance of about 15 km (5 ri). The shrine was Ranked as a [[Village Shrine]] in the early [[Meiji]] period and was elevated to a [[Prefectural Shrine]] in [[Taisho]] 7 (1918). There is a custom of searching for white fox hair at the base of the cedar tree to pray for longevity. == References == {{Reflist}} == External Links == *[http://www.shiwa-oinarisan.jp Shiwa Inari Shrine] (official site) *[http://www.ganshinsei.jp/shiwachou/118shiwainarijinja.html Shiwa Inari Shrine] (Iwate Prefecture Shinto Youth Association) {{Shinto-stub}} == References == {{reflist}} {{Inari Faith}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗}} {{Source Wikipedia}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Shiwa Inari Shrine}} {{translated page|ja|志和稲荷神社|version=101985462|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Inari Shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Iwate Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Shiwa, Iwate]] [[Category:History of Shiwa, Iwate]] [[Category:Inari shrines]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]] [[Category: Completion tier 1]] [[Category:Shiwa Inari Shrine]] [[Category:Pages from everybodywiki under right title]] [[Category:New]] }} [[every:Shiwa Inari Shrine]] [[en:draft:Shiwa Inari Shrine]] [[sn:Shiwa Inari Shrine]] [[en:Shiwa Inari Shrine]] [[ja:志和稲荷神社]]

The shrine received patronage from the Shiba clan, who ruled Shiwa District throughout the [[Muromachi period]]. After being attacked by Nobunao, Tsunenao hid in the shrine's affiliated temple, Joju-in, and then fled to Sanno-kai (a small basin in the upper reaches of the Takinakawa River, in present-day Tsuchidate, Shiwa Town). The Nanbu clan's patronage continued, with records showing that Nanbu Nobunao donated over 24 koku of rice fields in [[Keicho]] 4 (1599). Additional donations were made, including 20 koku from Hirasawa Village (present-day Hirasawa, Shiwa Town) in 1612, and another 10 koku in 1614. In [[Shoo]] 1 (1652), Nanbu Nobuaki donated 7 koku of shrine land. By [[Kaei]] 2 (1849), the total donations from the Nanbu domain amounted to 70 koku. festivals and other events were frequently attended by representatives from the Nanbu clan, confirming the shrine's status as a place of worship for the Nanbu family. In [[Tenpo]] 5 (1834), Nanbu Toshiharu constructed a pilgrimage road from [[Morioka]] to the shrine, modeled after the [[Nikko Kaido]], a distance of about 15 km (5 ri). The shrine was Ranked as a [[Village Shrine]] in the early [[Meiji]] period and was elevated to a [[Prefectural Shrine]] in [[Taisho]] 7 (1918). After World War II, it joined the Association of Shinto Shrines and was designated as a Beppyo Shrine in [[Showa]] 32 (1957). == Rituals == Until the early Meiji period, the shrine was influenced by the syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism and was managed by the Shugen-do temple Joju-in. == Grounds == The shrine grounds feature a cedar tree over 1,000 years old, known as the "Inariyama Great Cedar," which is considered sacred. There is a custom of searching for white fox hair at the base of the cedar tree to pray for longevity. == References == {{Reflist}} == External Links == *[http://www.shiwa-oinarisan.jp Shiwa Inari Shrine] (official site) *[http://www.ganshinsei.jp/shiwachou/118shiwainarijinja.html Shiwa Inari Shrine] (Iwate Prefecture Shinto Youth Association) {{Shinto-stub}} == References == {{reflist}} {{Inari Faith}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗}} {{Source Wikipedia}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Shiwa Inari Shrine}} {{translated page|ja|志和稲荷神社|version=101985462|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Inari Shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Iwate Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Shiwa, Iwate]] [[Category:History of Shiwa, Iwate]] [[Category:Inari shrines]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]] [[Category: Completion tier 1]] [[Category:Shiwa Inari Shrine]] [[Category:Pages from everybodywiki under right title]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:everybodywiki import successful]][[sn:Shiwa Inari Shrine]][[Category:everybodywiki import successful]] }} [[every:Shiwa Inari Shrine]] [[en:draft:Shiwa Inari Shrine]] [[sn:Shiwa Inari Shrine]] [[en:Shiwa Inari Shrine]] [[ja:志和稲荷神社]]

{{afc comment|1=This page was originally created in the draft namespace on english wikipedia. It was then deleted but preserved on everybodywiki. The imported history from everybodywiki is patchwork and contains all the contributing users, but not all the revisions are actually present. Lets hope this is sufficient attribution though especially since most of them are mine.[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:28, 2 May 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=see for the history[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 02:25, 31 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=Page originally made on english wikipedia in draftspace. g13 deleted and archived on everybodywiki. Go to [[:en:Wikipedia:Requests for undeletion]] to request undeletion of original page [[:en:draft:Shiwa Inari Shrine]] and import the revisions to here for attribution. View on everybodywiki here [[:every:Shiwa Inari Shrine|Shiwa Inari Shrine]]. [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 21:01, 16 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created by Immanuelle as a wikipedia draft. It was then automatically transferred to everybodywiki and recovered from there. In the future it will be good to import the revisions. Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{draft}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Shiwa Inari Shrine | image = [[File:志和稲荷神社 拝殿.jpg|thumb|Shiwa Inari Shrine Worship Hall]] | location = [[Iwate Prefecture]][[Shiwa District, Iwate|Shiwa District]][[Shiwa, Iwate|Shiwa Town]] Masuzawa Aza Maehira 17-1 | coordinates = {{Coord|39|33|32.1|N|141|04|40.2|E|scale:25000_region:JP-03_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deity = [[Ukanomitama|Ukanomitama no Okami]]
[[Sarutahiko|Sarutahiko no Okami]]
[[Oomiyanome no Okami]] | type = Former Prefectural Shrine, Beppyo Shrine | founded = Traditionally [[Tenki]] 5 (1057) | main hall= [[Irimoya-zukuri]] | alternate names = | Pilgrimage Sites = | festival = May 5 | Rites = [[Hatsuuma|Hatsuuma festival]] (First Horse Day of the Second Lunar Month) | map = {{location map|Japan Iwate|lat_deg=39|lat_min=33|lat_sec=32.1|lon_deg=141|lon_min=04|lon_sec=40.2|label=Shiwa Inari Shrine|position=center|width=200|float=center}} }} '''Shiwa Inari Shrine''' (志和稲荷神社) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located on the western foothills of the Masuzawa settlement in [[Shiwa Town]], [[Shiwa District, Iwate|Shiwa District]], [[Iwate Prefecture]], on the left bank of the [[Takinakawa River]]. It is a former prefectural shrine and currently a Beppyo Shrine under the Association of Shinto Shrines. Locally, it is sometimes considered one of the [[Inari shrine#Three Great Inari Shrines of Japan|Three Great Inari Shrines of Japan]]{{Cite web|url=https://www.lafrance.co.jp/company.htm |title=La France Onsen Kaisha|publisher=[[Shiwa Town]] [[Third Sector]] Shiwa Machizukuri Kikaku |accessdate=2018-11-16 }}{{Cite web|url=http://www.tsukinowa-iwate.com/press/index8p40.html |title=Featured Information on Meijo Kigan |publisher=[[Tsukinowa Sake Brewery]] |accessdate=2018-11-16 }} or one of the [[Inari shrine#Five Great Inari Shrines of Japan|Five Great Inari Shrines of Japan]]{{Cite web|url=http://www.iwatekeizai.org/watching/watching_201801/8066/ |title=Shiwa Inari Shrine in Shiwa Town |publisher=[[Iwate Bank]] Iwate Economic Research Institute |date=2018-01 |accessdate=2018-12-16 }}{{Citation needed|date=2018-12|title=Sources claiming Shiwa Inari Shrine as one of the Five Great Inari Shrines of Japan}}. Nearby, there is another shrine called [[Shiwa Koinari Shrine]]. == Enshrined Deities == The enshrined deities are [[Ukanomitama|Ukanomitama no Okami]] (Inari Okami), [[Sarutahiko|Sarutahiko no Okami]], and [[Oomiyanome no Okami]]. The shrine considers Oomiyanome no Okami to be another name for [[Ame-no-Uzume|Ame-no-Uzume no Mikoto]]. During the [[Edo period]], the shrine also worshipped [[Juichimen Kannon]] as its principal Buddha.『Goryobun Shato』. This document, compiled in the mid-18th century, lists temples and shrines within the Morioka domain. == History == According to tradition, the shrine was founded in [[Tenki]] 5 (1057) when [[Minamoto no Yoriyoshi]] enshrined a divided spirit from [[Fushimi Inari Taisha]] in Kyoto to pray for victory in his campaign against the Abe clan during the [[Former Nine Years' War]]. Later, the Fujiwara clan members who ruled Shiwa District, such as Hiramune Takanari, are said to have rebuilt the shrine.『Kadokawa Japan Place name Dictionary』. Another tradition claims that [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] rebuilt the shrine during the [[1189|Oshu War]] in 1189.『Iwate Place names』. There is also a tradition stating that the shrine was founded during the [[Daido]] period (806-810), and another stating that [[Shiba Ienaga]] enshrined the deity from Fushimi Inari when he came to Shiwa District in [[Kenmu]] 2 (1335) as the commander of Oshu.『Shinto Dictionary』 Vol. 2, Heibonsha, 1939. The shrine received patronage from the Shiba clan, who ruled Shiwa District throughout the [[Muromachi period]]. In [[Tensho]] 16 (1588), [[Shiba Tsunenao]] built the shrine building,The shrine possesses a building plaque dated May 28, 1588. but after Tsunenao was ousted by [[Nanbu Nobunao]] that same year, the Nanbu clan became the shrine's patrons. After being attacked by Nobunao, Tsunenao hid in the shrine's affiliated temple, Joju-in, and then fled to Sanno-kai (a small basin in the upper reaches of the Takinakawa River, in present-day Tsuchidate, Shiwa Town). The Nanbu clan's patronage continued, with records showing that Nanbu Nobunao donated over 24 koku of rice fields in [[Keicho]] 4 (1599). Additional donations were made, including 20 koku from Hirasawa Village (present-day Hirasawa, Shiwa Town) in 1612, and another 10 koku in 1614. In [[Shoo]] 1 (1652), Nanbu Nobuaki donated 7 koku of shrine land. By [[Kaei]] 2 (1849), the total donations from the Nanbu domain amounted to 70 koku. festivals and other events were frequently attended by representatives from the Nanbu clan, confirming the shrine's status as a place of worship for the Nanbu family. In [[Tenpo]] 5 (1834), Nanbu Toshiharu constructed a pilgrimage road from [[Morioka]] to the shrine, modeled after the [[Nikko Kaido]], a distance of about 15 km (5 ri). The shrine was Ranked as a [[Village Shrine]] in the early [[Meiji]] period and was elevated to a [[Prefectural Shrine]] in [[Taisho]] 7 (1918). After World War II, it joined the Association of Shinto Shrines and was designated as a Beppyo Shrine in [[Showa]] 32 (1957). == Rituals == Until the early Meiji period, the shrine was influenced by the syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism and was managed by the Shugen-do temple Joju-in. == Grounds == The shrine grounds feature a cedar tree over 1,000 years old, known as the "Inariyama Great Cedar," which is considered sacred. There is a custom of searching for white fox hair at the base of the cedar tree to pray for longevity. == References == {{Reflist}} == External Links == *[http://www.shiwa-oinarisan.jp Shiwa Inari Shrine] (official site) *[http://www.ganshinsei.jp/shiwachou/118shiwainarijinja.html Shiwa Inari Shrine] (Iwate Prefecture Shinto Youth Association) {{Shinto-stub}} == References == {{reflist}} {{Inari Faith}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗}} {{Source Wikipedia}} {{translated page|ja|志和稲荷神社|version=101985462|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Inari Shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Iwate Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Shiwa, Iwate]] [[Category:History of Shiwa, Iwate]] [[Category:Inari shrines]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]] [[Category: Completion tier 1]] [[Category:Shiwa Inari Shrine]] [[Category:Pages from everybodywiki under right title]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:everybodywiki import successful]][[sn:Shiwa Inari Shrine]][[Category:everybodywiki import successful]] }} [[every:Shiwa Inari Shrine]] [[en:draft:Shiwa Inari Shrine]] [[sn:Shiwa Inari Shrine]] [[en:Shiwa Inari Shrine]] [[ja:志和稲荷神社]]

Additional donations were made, including 20 koku from Hirasawa Village (present-day Hirasawa, Shiwa Town) in 1612, and another 10 koku in 1614. In [[Shoo]] 1 (1652), Nanbu Nobuaki donated 7 koku of shrine land. By [[Kaei]] 2 (1849), the total donations from the Nanbu domain amounted to 70 koku. festivals and other events were frequently attended by representatives from the Nanbu clan, confirming the shrine's status as a place of worship for the Nanbu family. In [[Tenpo]] 5 (1834), Nanbu Toshiharu constructed a pilgrimage road from [[Morioka]] to the shrine, modeled after the [[Nikko Kaido]], a distance of about 15 km (5 ri). The shrine was Ranked as a [[Village Shrine]] in the early [[Meiji]] period and was elevated to a [[Prefectural Shrine]] in [[Taisho]] 7 (1918). After World War II, it joined the Association of Shinto Shrines and was designated as a Beppyo Shrine in [[Showa]] 32 (1957). == Rituals == Until the early Meiji period, the shrine was influenced by the syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism and was managed by the Shugen-do temple Joju-in. == Grounds == The shrine grounds feature a cedar tree over 1,000 years old, known as the "Inariyama Great Cedar," which is considered sacred. There is a custom of searching for white fox hair at the base of the cedar tree to pray for longevity. == References == {{Reflist}} == External Links == *[http://www.shiwa-oinarisan.jp Shiwa Inari Shrine] (official site) *[http://www.ganshinsei.jp/shiwachou/118shiwainarijinja.html Shiwa Inari Shrine] (Iwate Prefecture Shinto Youth Association) {{Shinto-stub}} == References == {{reflist}} {{Inari Faith}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗}} {{Source Wikipedia}} {{translated page|ja|志和稲荷神社|version=101985462|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Inari Shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Iwate Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Shiwa, Iwate]] [[Category:History of Shiwa, Iwate]] [[Category:Inari shrines]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]] [[Category: Completion tier 1]] [[Category:Shiwa Inari Shrine]] [[Category:Pages from everybodywiki under right title]] [[Category:everybodywiki import successful]][[sn:Shiwa Inari Shrine]][[Category:everybodywiki import successful]] }} [[every:Shiwa Inari Shrine]] [[en:draft:Shiwa Inari Shrine]] [[sn:Shiwa Inari Shrine]] [[en:Shiwa Inari Shrine]] [[ja:志和稲荷神社]]

{{afc comment|1=This page was originally created in the draft namespace on english wikipedia. It was then deleted but preserved on everybodywiki. The imported history from everybodywiki is patchwork and contains all the contributing users, but not all the revisions are actually present. Lets hope this is sufficient attribution though especially since most of them are mine.[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:28, 2 May 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=see for the history[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 02:25, 31 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=Page originally made on english wikipedia in draftspace. g13 deleted and archived on everybodywiki. Go to [[:en:Wikipedia:Requests for undeletion]] to request undeletion of original page [[:en:draft:Shiwa Inari Shrine]] and import the revisions to here for attribution. View on everybodywiki here [[:every:Shiwa Inari Shrine|Shiwa Inari Shrine]]. [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 21:01, 16 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created by Immanuelle as a wikipedia draft. It was then automatically transferred to everybodywiki and recovered from there. In the future it will be good to import the revisions. Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Shiwa Inari Shrine | image = [[File:志和稲荷神社 拝殿.jpg|thumb|Shiwa Inari Shrine Worship Hall]] | location = [[Iwate Prefecture]][[Shiwa District, Iwate|Shiwa District]][[Shiwa, Iwate|Shiwa Town]] Masuzawa Aza Maehira 17-1 | coordinates = {{Coord|39|33|32.1|N|141|04|40.2|E|scale:25000_region:JP-03_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deity = [[Ukanomitama|Ukanomitama no Okami]]
[[Sarutahiko|Sarutahiko no Okami]]
[[Oomiyanome no Okami]] | type = Former Prefectural Shrine, Beppyo Shrine | founded = Traditionally [[Tenki]] 5 (1057) | main hall= [[Irimoya-zukuri]] | alternate names = | Pilgrimage Sites = | festival = May 5 | Rites = [[Hatsuuma|Hatsuuma festival]] (First Horse Day of the Second Lunar Month) | map = {{location map|Japan Iwate|lat_deg=39|lat_min=33|lat_sec=32.1|lon_deg=141|lon_min=04|lon_sec=40.2|label=Shiwa Inari Shrine|position=center|width=200|float=center}} }} '''Shiwa Inari Shrine''' (志和稲荷神社) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located on the western foothills of the Masuzawa settlement in [[Shiwa Town]], [[Shiwa District, Iwate|Shiwa District]], [[Iwate Prefecture]], on the left bank of the [[Takinakawa River]]. It is a former prefectural shrine and currently a Beppyo Shrine under the Association of Shinto Shrines. Locally, it is sometimes considered one of the [[Inari shrine#Three Great Inari Shrines of Japan|Three Great Inari Shrines of Japan]]{{Cite web|url=https://www.lafrance.co.jp/company.htm |title=La France Onsen Kaisha|publisher=[[Shiwa Town]] [[Third Sector]] Shiwa Machizukuri Kikaku |accessdate=2018-11-16 }}{{Cite web|url=http://www.tsukinowa-iwate.com/press/index8p40.html |title=Featured Information on Meijo Kigan |publisher=[[Tsukinowa Sake Brewery]] |accessdate=2018-11-16 }} or one of the [[Inari shrine#Five Great Inari Shrines of Japan|Five Great Inari Shrines of Japan]]{{Cite web|url=http://www.iwatekeizai.org/watching/watching_201801/8066/ |title=Shiwa Inari Shrine in Shiwa Town |publisher=[[Iwate Bank]] Iwate Economic Research Institute |date=2018-01 |accessdate=2018-12-16 }}{{Citation needed|date=2018-12|title=Sources claiming Shiwa Inari Shrine as one of the Five Great Inari Shrines of Japan}}. Nearby, there is another shrine called [[Shiwa Koinari Shrine]]. == Enshrined Deities == The enshrined deities are [[Ukanomitama|Ukanomitama no Okami]] (Inari Okami), [[Sarutahiko|Sarutahiko no Okami]], and [[Oomiyanome no Okami]]. The shrine considers Oomiyanome no Okami to be another name for [[Ame-no-Uzume|Ame-no-Uzume no Mikoto]]. During the [[Edo period]], the shrine also worshipped [[Juichimen Kannon]] as its principal Buddha.『Goryobun Shato』. This document, compiled in the mid-18th century, lists temples and shrines within the Morioka domain. == History == According to tradition, the shrine was founded in [[Tenki]] 5 (1057) when [[Minamoto no Yoriyoshi]] enshrined a divided spirit from [[Fushimi Inari Taisha]] in Kyoto to pray for victory in his campaign against the Abe clan during the [[Former Nine Years' War]]. Later, the Fujiwara clan members who ruled Shiwa District, such as Hiramune Takanari, are said to have rebuilt the shrine.『Kadokawa Japan Place name Dictionary』. Another tradition claims that [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] rebuilt the shrine during the [[1189|Oshu War]] in 1189.『Iwate Place names』. There is also a tradition stating that the shrine was founded during the [[Daido]] period (806-810), and another stating that [[Shiba Ienaga]] enshrined the deity from Fushimi Inari when he came to Shiwa District in [[Kenmu]] 2 (1335) as the commander of Oshu.『Shinto Dictionary』 Vol. 2, Heibonsha, 1939. The shrine received patronage from the Shiba clan, who ruled Shiwa District throughout the [[Muromachi period]]. In [[Tensho]] 16 (1588), [[Shiba Tsunenao]] built the shrine building,The shrine possesses a building plaque dated May 28, 1588. but after Tsunenao was ousted by [[Nanbu Nobunao]] that same year, the Nanbu clan became the shrine's patrons. After being attacked by Nobunao, Tsunenao hid in the shrine's affiliated temple, Joju-in, and then fled to Sanno-kai (a small basin in the upper reaches of the Takinakawa River, in present-day Tsuchidate, Shiwa Town). The Nanbu clan's patronage continued, with records showing that Nanbu Nobunao donated over 24 koku of rice fields in [[Keicho]] 4 (1599). Additional donations were made, including 20 koku from Hirasawa Village (present-day Hirasawa, Shiwa Town) in 1612, and another 10 koku in 1614. In [[Shoo]] 1 (1652), Nanbu Nobuaki donated 7 koku of shrine land. By [[Kaei]] 2 (1849), the total donations from the Nanbu domain amounted to 70 koku. festivals and other events were frequently attended by representatives from the Nanbu clan, confirming the shrine's status as a place of worship for the Nanbu family. In [[Tenpo]] 5 (1834), Nanbu Toshiharu constructed a pilgrimage road from [[Morioka]] to the shrine, modeled after the [[Nikko Kaido]], a distance of about 15 km (5 ri). The shrine was Ranked as a [[Village Shrine]] in the early [[Meiji]] period and was elevated to a [[Prefectural Shrine]] in [[Taisho]] 7 (1918). After World War II, it joined the Association of Shinto Shrines and was designated as a Beppyo Shrine in [[Showa]] 32 (1957). == Rituals == Until the early Meiji period, the shrine was influenced by the syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism and was managed by the Shugen-do temple Joju-in. == Grounds == The shrine grounds feature a cedar tree over 1,000 years old, known as the "Inariyama Great Cedar," which is considered sacred. There is a custom of searching for white fox hair at the base of the cedar tree to pray for longevity. == References == {{Reflist}} == External Links == *[http://www.shiwa-oinarisan.jp Shiwa Inari Shrine] (official site) *[http://www.ganshinsei.jp/shiwachou/118shiwainarijinja.html Shiwa Inari Shrine] (Iwate Prefecture Shinto Youth Association) {{Shinto-stub}} == References == {{reflist}} {{Inari Faith}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗}} {{Source Wikipedia}} {{translated page|ja|志和稲荷神社|version=101985462|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Inari Shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Iwate Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Shiwa, Iwate]] [[Category:History of Shiwa, Iwate]] [[Category:Inari shrines]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]] [[Category: Completion tier 1]] [[Category:Shiwa Inari Shrine]] [[Category:Pages from everybodywiki under right title]] [[Category:everybodywiki import successful]][[sn:Shiwa Inari Shrine]][[Category:everybodywiki import successful]] }} [[every:Shiwa Inari Shrine]] [[en:draft:Shiwa Inari Shrine]] [[sn:Shiwa Inari Shrine]] [[en:Shiwa Inari Shrine]] [[ja:志和稲荷神社]]

In [[Tenpo]] 5 (1834), Nanbu Toshiharu constructed a pilgrimage road from [[Morioka]] to the shrine, modeled after the [[Nikko Kaido]], a distance of about 15 km (5 ri). The shrine was Ranked as a [[Village Shrine]] in the early [[Meiji]] period and was elevated to a [[Prefectural Shrine]] in [[Taisho]] 7 (1918). After World War II, it joined the Association of Shinto Shrines and was designated as a Beppyo Shrine in [[Showa]] 32 (1957). == Rituals == Until the early Meiji period, the shrine was influenced by the syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism and was managed by the Shugen-do temple Joju-in. == Grounds == The shrine grounds feature a cedar tree over 1,000 years old, known as the "Inariyama Great Cedar," which is considered sacred. There is a custom of searching for white fox hair at the base of the cedar tree to pray for longevity. == References == {{Reflist}} == External Links == *[http://www.shiwa-oinarisan.jp Shiwa Inari Shrine] (official site) *[http://www.ganshinsei.jp/shiwachou/118shiwainarijinja.html Shiwa Inari Shrine] (Iwate Prefecture Shinto Youth Association) {{Shinto-stub}} == References == {{reflist}} {{Inari Faith}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗}} {{Source Wikipedia}} {{translated page|ja|志和稲荷神社|version=101985462|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Inari Shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Iwate Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Shiwa, Iwate]] [[Category:History of Shiwa, Iwate]] [[Category:Inari shrines]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]] [[Category: Completion tier 1]] [[Category:Pages from everybodywiki under right title]] [[Category:everybodywiki import successful]][[sn:Shiwa Inari Shrine]][[Category:everybodywiki import successful]] }} [[every:Shiwa Inari Shrine]] [[en:draft:Shiwa Inari Shrine]] [[sn:Shiwa Inari Shrine]] [[en:Shiwa Inari Shrine]] [[ja:志和稲荷神社]]

revision.text:
{{afc comment|1=This page was originally created in the draft namespace on english wikipedia. It was then deleted but preserved on everybodywiki. The imported history from everybodywiki is patchwork and contains all the contributing users, but not all the revisions are actually present. Lets hope this is sufficient attribution though especially since most of them are mine.[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:28, 2 May 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=see for the history[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 02:25, 31 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=Page originally made on english wikipedia in draftspace. g13 deleted and archived on everybodywiki. Go to [[:en:Wikipedia:Requests for undeletion]] to request undeletion of original page [[:en:draft:Shiwa Inari Shrine]] and import the revisions to here for attribution. View on everybodywiki here [[:every:Shiwa Inari Shrine|Shiwa Inari Shrine]]. [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 21:01, 16 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created by Immanuelle as a wikipedia draft. It was then automatically transferred to everybodywiki and recovered from there. In the future it will be good to import the revisions. Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Shiwa Inari Shrine | image = [[File:志和稲荷神社 拝殿.jpg|thumb|Shiwa Inari Shrine Worship Hall]] | location = [[Iwate Prefecture]][[Shiwa District, Iwate|Shiwa District]][[Shiwa, Iwate|Shiwa Town]] Masuzawa Aza Maehira 17-1 | coordinates = {{Coord|39|33|32.1|N|141|04|40.2|E|scale:25000_region:JP-03_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deity = [[Ukanomitama|Ukanomitama no Okami]]
[[Sarutahiko Ōkami|Sarutahiko no Okami]]
[[Oomiyanome no Okami]] | type = Former Prefectural Shrine, Beppyo Shrine | founded = Traditionally [[Tenki]] 5 (1057) | main hall= [[Irimoya-zukuri]] | alternate names = | Pilgrimage Sites = | festival = May 5 | Rites = [[Hatsuuma|Hatsuuma festival]] (First Horse Day of the Second Lunar Month) | map = {{location map|Japan Iwate|lat_deg=39|lat_min=33|lat_sec=32.1|lon_deg=141|lon_min=04|lon_sec=40.2|label=Shiwa Inari Shrine|position=center|width=200|float=center}} }} '''Shiwa Inari Shrine''' (志和稲荷神社) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located on the western foothills of the Masuzawa settlement in [[Shiwa Town]], [[Shiwa District, Iwate|Shiwa District]], [[Iwate Prefecture]], on the left bank of the [[Takinakawa River]]. It is a former prefectural shrine and currently a Beppyo Shrine under the Association of Shinto Shrines. Locally, it is sometimes considered one of the [[Inari shrine#Three Great Inari Shrines of Japan|Three Great Inari Shrines of Japan]]{{Cite web|url=https://www.lafrance.co.jp/company.htm |title=La France Onsen Kaisha|publisher=[[Shiwa Town]] [[Third Sector]] Shiwa Machizukuri Kikaku |accessdate=2018-11-16 }}{{Cite web|url=http://www.tsukinowa-iwate.com/press/index8p40.html |title=Featured Information on Meijo Kigan |publisher=[[Tsukinowa Sake Brewery]] |accessdate=2018-11-16 }} or one of the [[Inari shrine#Five Great Inari Shrines of Japan|Five Great Inari Shrines of Japan]]{{Cite web|url=http://www.iwatekeizai.org/watching/watching_201801/8066/ |title=Shiwa Inari Shrine in Shiwa Town |publisher=[[Iwate Bank]] Iwate Economic Research Institute |date=2018-01 |accessdate=2018-12-16 }}{{Citation needed|date=2018-12|title=Sources claiming Shiwa Inari Shrine as one of the Five Great Inari Shrines of Japan}}. Nearby, there is another shrine called [[Shiwa Koinari Shrine]]. == Enshrined Deities == The enshrined deities are [[Ukanomitama|Ukanomitama no Okami]] (Inari Okami), [[Sarutahiko Ōkami|Sarutahiko no Okami]], and [[Oomiyanome no Okami]]. The shrine considers Oomiyanome no Okami to be another name for [[Ame-no-Uzume|Ame-no-Uzume no Mikoto]]. During the [[Edo period]], the shrine also worshipped [[Juichimen Kannon]] as its principal Buddha.『Goryobun Shato』. This document, compiled in the mid-18th century, lists temples and shrines within the Morioka domain. == History == According to tradition, the shrine was founded in [[Tenki]] 5 (1057) when [[Minamoto no Yoriyoshi]] enshrined a divided spirit from [[Fushimi Inari Taisha]] in Kyoto to pray for victory in his campaign against the Abe clan during the [[Former Nine Years' War]]. Later, the Fujiwara clan members who ruled Shiwa District, such as Hiramune Takanari, are said to have rebuilt the shrine.『Kadokawa Japan Place name Dictionary』. Another tradition claims that [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] rebuilt the shrine during the [[1189|Oshu War]] in 1189.『Iwate Place names』. There is also a tradition stating that the shrine was founded during the [[Daido]] period (806-810), and another stating that [[Shiba Ienaga]] enshrined the deity from Fushimi Inari when he came to Shiwa District in [[Kenmu]] 2 (1335) as the commander of Oshu.『Shinto Dictionary』 Vol. 2, Heibonsha, 1939. The shrine received patronage from the Shiba clan, who ruled Shiwa District throughout the [[Muromachi period]]. In [[Tensho]] 16 (1588), [[Shiba Tsunenao]] built the shrine building,The shrine possesses a building plaque dated May 28, 1588. but after Tsunenao was ousted by [[Nanbu Nobunao]] that same year, the Nanbu clan became the shrine's patrons. After being attacked by Nobunao, Tsunenao hid in the shrine's affiliated temple, Joju-in, and then fled to Sanno-kai (a small basin in the upper reaches of the Takinakawa River, in present-day Tsuchidate, Shiwa Town). The Nanbu clan's patronage continued, with records showing that Nanbu Nobunao donated over 24 koku of rice fields in [[Keicho]] 4 (1599). Additional donations were made, including 20 koku from Hirasawa Village (present-day Hirasawa, Shiwa Town) in 1612, and another 10 koku in 1614. In [[Shoo]] 1 (1652), Nanbu Nobuaki donated 7 koku of shrine land. By [[Kaei]] 2 (1849), the total donations from the Nanbu domain amounted to 70 koku. festivals and other events were frequently attended by representatives from the Nanbu clan, confirming the shrine's status as a place of worship for the Nanbu family. In [[Tenpo]] 5 (1834), Nanbu Toshiharu constructed a pilgrimage road from [[Morioka]] to the shrine, modeled after the [[Nikko Kaido]], a distance of about 15 km (5 ri). The shrine was Ranked as a [[Village Shrine]] in the early [[Meiji]] period and was elevated to a [[Prefectural Shrine]] in [[Taisho]] 7 (1918). After World War II, it joined the Association of Shinto Shrines and was designated as a Beppyo Shrine in [[Showa]] 32 (1957). == Rituals == Until the early Meiji period, the shrine was influenced by the syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism and was managed by the Shugen-do temple Joju-in. == Grounds == The shrine grounds feature a cedar tree over 1,000 years old, known as the "Inariyama Great Cedar," which is considered sacred. There is a custom of searching for white fox hair at the base of the cedar tree to pray for longevity. == References == {{Reflist}} == External Links == *[http://www.shiwa-oinarisan.jp Shiwa Inari Shrine] (official site) *[http://www.ganshinsei.jp/shiwachou/118shiwainarijinja.html Shiwa Inari Shrine] (Iwate Prefecture Shinto Youth Association) {{Shinto-stub}} == References == {{reflist}} {{Inari Faith}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗}} {{Source Wikipedia}} {{translated page|ja|志和稲荷神社|version=101985462|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Inari Shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Iwate Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Shiwa, Iwate]] [[Category:History of Shiwa, Iwate]] [[Category:Inari shrines]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]] [[Category: Completion tier 1]] [[Category:Pages from everybodywiki under right title]] [[Category:everybodywiki import successful]][[sn:Shiwa Inari Shrine]][[Category:everybodywiki import successful]] }} [[every:Shiwa Inari Shrine]] [[en:draft:Shiwa Inari Shrine]] [[sn:Shiwa Inari Shrine]] [[en:Shiwa Inari Shrine]] [[ja:志和稲荷神社]] [[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines in Iwate Prefecture]]

After World War II, it joined the Association of Shinto Shrines and was designated as a Beppyo Shrine in [[Showa]] 32 (1957). == Rituals == Until the early Meiji period, the shrine was influenced by the syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism and was managed by the Shugen-do temple Joju-in. == Grounds == The shrine grounds feature a cedar tree over 1,000 years old, known as the "Inariyama Great Cedar," which is considered sacred. There is a custom of searching for white fox hair at the base of the cedar tree to pray for longevity. == References == {{Reflist}} == External Links == *[http://www.shiwa-oinarisan.jp Shiwa Inari Shrine] (official site) *[http://www.ganshinsei.jp/shiwachou/118shiwainarijinja.html Shiwa Inari Shrine] (Iwate Prefecture Shinto Youth Association) {{Shinto-stub}} == References == {{reflist}} {{Inari Faith}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗}} {{Source Wikipedia}} {{translated page|ja|志和稲荷神社|version=101985462|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Inari Shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Iwate Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Shiwa, Iwate]] [[Category:History of Shiwa, Iwate]] [[Category:Inari shrines]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]] [[Category: Completion tier 1]] [[Category:Pages from everybodywiki under right title]] [[Category:everybodywiki import successful]][[sn:Shiwa Inari Shrine]][[Category:everybodywiki import successful]] }} [[every:Shiwa Inari Shrine]] [[en:draft:Shiwa Inari Shrine]] [[sn:Shiwa Inari Shrine]] [[en:Shiwa Inari Shrine]] [[ja:志和稲荷神社]] [[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines in Iwate Prefecture]] [[Category:紫波町の建築物]] [[Category:紫波町の歴史]]

revision.text:
{{afc comment|1=This page was originally created in the draft namespace on english wikipedia. It was then deleted but preserved on everybodywiki. The imported history from everybodywiki is patchwork and contains all the contributing users, but not all the revisions are actually present. Lets hope this is sufficient attribution though especially since most of them are mine.[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:28, 2 May 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=see for the history[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 02:25, 31 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=Page originally made on english wikipedia in draftspace. g13 deleted and archived on everybodywiki. Go to [[:en:Wikipedia:Requests for undeletion]] to request undeletion of original page [[:en:draft:Shiwa Inari Shrine]] and import the revisions to here for attribution. View on everybodywiki here [[:every:Shiwa Inari Shrine|Shiwa Inari Shrine]]. [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 21:01, 16 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created by Immanuelle as a wikipedia draft. It was then automatically transferred to everybodywiki and recovered from there. In the future it will be good to import the revisions. Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Shiwa Inari Shrine | image = [[File:志和稲荷神社 拝殿.jpg|thumb|Shiwa Inari Shrine Worship Hall]] | location = [[Iwate Prefecture]][[Shiwa District, Iwate|Shiwa District]][[Shiwa, Iwate|Shiwa Town]] Masuzawa Aza Maehira 17-1 | coordinates = {{Coord|39|33|32.1|N|141|04|40.2|E|scale:25000_region:JP-03_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deity = [[Ukanomitama|Ukanomitama no Okami]]
[[Sarutahiko Ōkami|Sarutahiko no Okami]]
[[Oomiyanome no Okami]] | type = Former Prefectural Shrine, Beppyo Shrine | founded = Traditionally [[Tenki]] 5 (1057) | main hall= [[Irimoya-zukuri]] | alternate names = | Pilgrimage Sites = | festival = May 5 | Rites = [[Hatsuuma|Hatsuuma festival]] (First Horse Day of the Second Lunar Month) | map = {{location map|Japan Iwate|lat_deg=39|lat_min=33|lat_sec=32.1|lon_deg=141|lon_min=04|lon_sec=40.2|label=Shiwa Inari Shrine|position=center|width=200|float=center}} }} '''Shiwa Inari Shrine''' (志和稲荷神社) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located on the western foothills of the Masuzawa settlement in [[Shiwa Town]], [[Shiwa District, Iwate|Shiwa District]], [[Iwate Prefecture]], on the left bank of the [[Takinakawa River]]. It is a former prefectural shrine and currently a Beppyo Shrine under the Association of Shinto Shrines. Locally, it is sometimes considered one of the [[Inari shrine#Three Great Inari Shrines of Japan|Three Great Inari Shrines of Japan]]{{Cite web|url=https://www.lafrance.co.jp/company.htm |title=La France Onsen Kaisha|publisher=[[Shiwa Town]] [[Third Sector]] Shiwa Machizukuri Kikaku |accessdate=2018-11-16 }}{{Cite web|url=http://www.tsukinowa-iwate.com/press/index8p40.html |title=Featured Information on Meijo Kigan |publisher=[[Tsukinowa Sake Brewery]] |accessdate=2018-11-16 }} or one of the [[Inari shrine#Five Great Inari Shrines of Japan|Five Great Inari Shrines of Japan]]{{Cite web|url=http://www.iwatekeizai.org/watching/watching_201801/8066/ |title=Shiwa Inari Shrine in Shiwa Town |publisher=[[Iwate Bank]] Iwate Economic Research Institute |date=2018-01 |accessdate=2018-12-16 }}{{Citation needed|date=2018-12|title=Sources claiming Shiwa Inari Shrine as one of the Five Great Inari Shrines of Japan}}. Nearby, there is another shrine called [[Shiwa Koinari Shrine]]. == Enshrined Deities == The enshrined deities are [[Ukanomitama|Ukanomitama no Okami]] (Inari Okami), [[Sarutahiko Ōkami|Sarutahiko no Okami]], and [[Oomiyanome no Okami]]. The shrine considers Oomiyanome no Okami to be another name for [[Ame-no-Uzume|Ame-no-Uzume no Mikoto]]. During the [[Edo period]], the shrine also worshipped [[Juichimen Kannon]] as its principal Buddha.『Goryobun Shato』. This document, compiled in the mid-18th century, lists temples and shrines within the Morioka domain. == History == According to tradition, the shrine was founded in [[Tenki]] 5 (1057) when [[Minamoto no Yoriyoshi]] enshrined a divided spirit from [[Fushimi Inari Taisha]] in Kyoto to pray for victory in his campaign against the Abe clan during the [[Former Nine Years' War]]. Later, the Fujiwara clan members who ruled Shiwa District, such as Hiramune Takanari, are said to have rebuilt the shrine.『Kadokawa Japan Place name Dictionary』. Another tradition claims that [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] rebuilt the shrine during the [[1189|Oshu War]] in 1189.『Iwate Place names』. There is also a tradition stating that the shrine was founded during the [[Daido]] period (806-810), and another stating that [[Shiba Ienaga]] enshrined the deity from Fushimi Inari when he came to Shiwa District in [[Kenmu]] 2 (1335) as the commander of Oshu.『Shinto Dictionary』 Vol. 2, Heibonsha, 1939. The shrine received patronage from the Shiba clan, who ruled Shiwa District throughout the [[Muromachi period]]. In [[Tensho]] 16 (1588), [[Shiba Tsunenao]] built the shrine building,The shrine possesses a building plaque dated May 28, 1588. but after Tsunenao was ousted by [[Nanbu Nobunao]] that same year, the Nanbu clan became the shrine's patrons. After being attacked by Nobunao, Tsunenao hid in the shrine's affiliated temple, Joju-in, and then fled to Sanno-kai (a small basin in the upper reaches of the Takinakawa River, in present-day Tsuchidate, Shiwa Town). The Nanbu clan's patronage continued, with records showing that Nanbu Nobunao donated over 24 koku of rice fields in [[Keicho]] 4 (1599). Additional donations were made, including 20 koku from Hirasawa Village (present-day Hirasawa, Shiwa Town) in 1612, and another 10 koku in 1614. In [[Shoo]] 1 (1652), Nanbu Nobuaki donated 7 koku of shrine land. By [[Kaei]] 2 (1849), the total donations from the Nanbu domain amounted to 70 koku. festivals and other events were frequently attended by representatives from the Nanbu clan, confirming the shrine's status as a place of worship for the Nanbu family. In [[Tenpo]] 5 (1834), Nanbu Toshiharu constructed a pilgrimage road from [[Morioka]] to the shrine, modeled after the [[Nikko Kaido]], a distance of about 15 km (5 ri). The shrine was Ranked as a [[Village Shrine]] in the early [[Meiji]] period and was elevated to a [[Prefectural Shrine]] in [[Taisho]] 7 (1918). After World War II, it joined the Association of Shinto Shrines and was designated as a Beppyo Shrine in [[Showa]] 32 (1957). == Rituals == Until the early Meiji period, the shrine was influenced by the syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism and was managed by the Shugen-do temple Joju-in. == Grounds == The shrine grounds feature a cedar tree over 1,000 years old, known as the "Inariyama Great Cedar," which is considered sacred. There is a custom of searching for white fox hair at the base of the cedar tree to pray for longevity. == References == {{Reflist}} == External Links == *[http://www.shiwa-oinarisan.jp Shiwa Inari Shrine] (official site) *[http://www.ganshinsei.jp/shiwachou/118shiwainarijinja.html Shiwa Inari Shrine] (Iwate Prefecture Shinto Youth Association) {{Shinto-stub}} == References == {{reflist}} {{Inari Faith}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗}} {{Source Wikipedia}} {{translated page|ja|志和稲荷神社|version=101985462|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Inari Shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Iwate Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Shiwa, Iwate]] [[Category:History of Shiwa, Iwate]] [[Category:Inari shrines]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]] [[Category: Completion tier 1]] [[Category:Pages from everybodywiki under right title]] [[Category:everybodywiki import successful]][[sn:Shiwa Inari Shrine]][[Category:everybodywiki import successful]] }} [[every:Shiwa Inari Shrine]] [[sn:Shiwa Inari Shrine]] [[ja:志和稲荷神社]] [[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines in Iwate Prefecture]] [[Category:紫波町の建築物]] [[Category:紫波町の歴史]]
