'''Shiwa Inari Shrine''' (志和稲荷神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located on the western foothills of the Masuzawa settlement in [[Shiwa Town]], [[:en:Shiwa District, Iwate|Shiwa District]], [[:en:Iwate Prefecture|Iwate Prefecture]], on the left bank of the [[Takinakawa River]]. It is a former prefectural shrine and currently a Beppyo Shrine under the Association of Shinto Shrines.

Locally, it is sometimes considered one of the [[Inari shrine#Three Great Inari Shrines of Japan|Three Great Inari Shrines of Japan]]<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.lafrance.co.jp/company.htm |title=La France Onsen Kaisha|publisher=[[Shiwa Town]] [[Third Sector]] Shiwa Machizukuri Kikaku |accessdate=2018-11-16 }}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.tsukinowa-iwate.com/press/index8p40.html |title=Featured Information on Meijo Kigan |publisher=[[Tsukinowa Sake Brewery]] |accessdate=2018-11-16 }}</ref> or one of the [[Inari shrine#Five Great Inari Shrines of Japan|Five Great Inari Shrines of Japan]]<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.iwatekeizai.org/watching/watching_201801/8066/ |title=Shiwa Inari Shrine in Shiwa Town |publisher=[[Iwate Bank]] Iwate Economic Research Institute |date=2018-01 |accessdate=2018-12-16 }}</ref>{{Citation needed|date=2018-12|title=Sources claiming Shiwa Inari Shrine as one of the Five Great Inari Shrines of Japan}}.

Nearby, there is another shrine called [[Shiwa Koinari Shrine]].

== Enshrined Deities ==
The enshrined deities are [[Ukanomitama|Ukanomitama no Okami]] (Inari Okami), [[Sarutahiko Ōkami|Sarutahiko no Okami]], and [[Oomiyanome no Okami]]. The shrine considers Oomiyanome no Okami to be another name for [[Ame-no-Uzume|Ame-no-Uzume no Mikoto]].

During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], the shrine also worshipped [[:en:Ekādaśamukha|Ekādaśamukha]] as its principal Buddha.<ref>『Goryobun Shato』. This document, compiled in the mid-18th century, lists temples and shrines within the Morioka domain.</ref>

== History ==
According to tradition, the shrine was founded in [[:en:Tenki|Tenki]] 5 (1057) when [[Minamoto no Yoriyoshi]] enshrined a divided spirit from [[:en:Fushimi Inari Taisha|Fushimi Inari Taisha]] in Kyoto to pray for victory in his campaign against the Abe clan during the [[:en:Former Nine Years' War|Former Nine Years' War]]. Later, the Fujiwara clan members who ruled Shiwa District, such as Hiramune Takanari, are said to have rebuilt the shrine.<ref name="Kadokawa">『Kadokawa Japan Place name Dictionary』.</ref> Another tradition claims that [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] rebuilt the shrine during the [[:en:1189|Oshu War]] in 1189.<ref name="Heibonsha">『Iwate Place names』.</ref> There is also a tradition stating that the shrine was founded during the [[:en:Daido|Daido]] period (806-810), and another stating that [[Shiba Ienaga]] enshrined the deity from Fushimi Inari when he came to Shiwa District in [[:en:Kenmu|Kenmu]] 2 (1335) as the commander of Oshu.<ref name="Heibonsha"/><ref>『Shinto Dictionary』 Vol. 2, Heibonsha, 1939.</ref>

The shrine received patronage from the Shiba clan, who ruled Shiwa District throughout the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]]. In [[:en:Tensho|Tensho]] 16 (1588), [[Shiba Tsunenao]] built the shrine building,<ref>The shrine possesses a building plaque dated May 28, 1588.</ref> but after Tsunenao was ousted by [[Nanbu Nobunao]] that same year, the Nanbu clan became the shrine's patrons. After being attacked by Nobunao, Tsunenao hid in the shrine's affiliated temple, Joju-in, and then fled to Sanno-kai (a small basin in the upper reaches of the Takinakawa River, in present-day Tsuchidate, Shiwa Town).<ref name="Heibonsha"/>

The Nanbu clan's patronage continued, with records showing that Nanbu Nobunao donated over 24 koku of rice fields in [[:en:Keicho|Keicho]] 4 (1599). Additional donations were made, including 20 koku from Hirasawa Village (present-day Hirasawa, Shiwa Town) in 1612, and another 10 koku in 1614. In [[:en:Shoo|Shoo]] 1 (1652), Nanbu Nobuaki donated 7 koku of shrine land. By [[:en:Kaei|Kaei]] 2 (1849), the total donations from the Nanbu domain amounted to 70 koku.<ref name="Heibonsha"/> festivals and other events were frequently attended by representatives from the Nanbu clan, confirming the shrine's status as a place of worship for the Nanbu family.<ref name="Kadokawa"/> In [[:en:Tenpo|Tenpo]] 5 (1834), Nanbu Toshiharu constructed a pilgrimage road from [[:en:Morioka|Morioka]] to the shrine, modeled after the [[:en:Nikko Kaido|Nikko Kaido]], a distance of about 15 km (5 ri).

The shrine was Ranked as a [[Village Shrine]] in the early [[Meiji]] period and was elevated to a [[Prefectural Shrine]] in [[:en:Taishō era|Taishō era]] 7 (1918). After World War II, it joined the Association of Shinto Shrines and was designated as a Beppyo Shrine in [[:en:Shōwa|Shōwa]] 32 (1957).

== Rituals ==
Until the early Meiji period, the shrine was influenced by the syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism and was managed by the Shugen-do temple Joju-in.

== Grounds ==
The shrine grounds feature a cedar tree over 1,000 years old, known as the "Inariyama Great Cedar," which is considered sacred. There is a custom of searching for white fox hair at the base of the cedar tree to pray for longevity.

== Grounds ==
The shrine grounds feature a cedar tree over 1,000 years old, known as the "Inariyama Great Cedar," which is considered sacred. There is a custom of searching for white fox hair at the base of the cedar tree to pray for longevity.

'''Shiwa Inari Shrine''' (志和稲荷神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located on the western foothills of the Masuzawa settlement in [[Shiwa Town]], [[:en:Shiwa District, Iwate|Shiwa District]], [[:en:Iwate Prefecture|Iwate Prefecture]], on the left bank of the [[Takinakawa River]]. It is a former prefectural shrine and currently a Beppyo Shrine under the Association of Shinto Shrines.

Locally, it is sometimes considered one of the [[Inari shrine#Three Great Inari Shrines of Japan|Three Great Inari Shrines of Japan]]<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.lafrance.co.jp/company.htm |title=La France Onsen Kaisha|publisher=[[Shiwa Town]] [[Third Sector]] Shiwa Machizukuri Kikaku |accessdate=2018-11-16 }}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.tsukinowa-iwate.com/press/index8p40.html |title=Featured Information on Meijo Kigan |publisher=[[Tsukinowa Sake Brewery]] |accessdate=2018-11-16 }}</ref> or one of the [[Inari shrine#Five Great Inari Shrines of Japan|Five Great Inari Shrines of Japan]]<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.iwatekeizai.org/watching/watching_201801/8066/ |title=Shiwa Inari Shrine in Shiwa Town |publisher=[[Iwate Bank]] Iwate Economic Research Institute |date=2018-01 |accessdate=2018-12-16 }}</ref>{{Citation needed|date=2018-12|title=Sources claiming Shiwa Inari Shrine as one of the Five Great Inari Shrines of Japan}}.

Nearby, there is another shrine called [[Shiwa Koinari Shrine]].

== Enshrined Deities ==
The enshrined deities are [[Ukanomitama|Ukanomitama no Okami]] (Inari Okami), [[Sarutahiko Ōkami|Sarutahiko no Okami]], and [[Oomiyanome no Okami]]. The shrine considers Oomiyanome no Okami to be another name for [[Ame-no-Uzume|Ame-no-Uzume no Mikoto]].

During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], the shrine also worshipped [[:en:Ekādaśamukha|Ekādaśamukha]] as its principal Buddha.<ref>『Goryobun Shato』. This document, compiled in the mid-18th century, lists temples and shrines within the Morioka domain.</ref>

== History ==
According to tradition, the shrine was founded in [[:en:Tenki|Tenki]] 5 (1057) when [[Minamoto no Yoriyoshi]] enshrined a divided spirit from [[:en:Fushimi Inari Taisha|Fushimi Inari Taisha]] in Kyoto to pray for victory in his campaign against the Abe clan during the [[:en:Former Nine Years' War|Former Nine Years' War]]. Later, the Fujiwara clan members who ruled Shiwa District, such as Hiramune Takanari, are said to have rebuilt the shrine.<ref name="Kadokawa">『Kadokawa Japan Place name Dictionary』.</ref> Another tradition claims that [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] rebuilt the shrine during the [[:en:1189|Oshu War]] in 1189.<ref name="Heibonsha">『Iwate Place names』.</ref> There is also a tradition stating that the shrine was founded during the [[:en:Daido|Daido]] period (806-810), and another stating that [[Shiba Ienaga]] enshrined the deity from Fushimi Inari when he came to Shiwa District in [[:en:Kenmu|Kenmu]] 2 (1335) as the commander of Oshu.<ref name="Heibonsha"/><ref>『Shinto Dictionary』 Vol. 2, Heibonsha, 1939.</ref>

The shrine received patronage from the Shiba clan, who ruled Shiwa District throughout the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]]. In [[:en:Tensho|Tensho]] 16 (1588), [[Shiba Tsunenao]] built the shrine building,<ref>The shrine possesses a building plaque dated May 28, 1588.</ref> but after Tsunenao was ousted by [[Nanbu Nobunao]] that same year, the Nanbu clan became the shrine's patrons. After being attacked by Nobunao, Tsunenao hid in the shrine's affiliated temple, Joju-in, and then fled to Sanno-kai (a small basin in the upper reaches of the Takinakawa River, in present-day Tsuchidate, Shiwa Town).<ref name="Heibonsha"/>

The Nanbu clan's patronage continued, with records showing that Nanbu Nobunao donated over 24 koku of rice fields in [[:en:Keicho|Keicho]] 4 (1599). Additional donations were made, including 20 koku from Hirasawa Village (present-day Hirasawa, Shiwa Town) in 1612, and another 10 koku in 1614. In [[:en:Shoo|Shoo]] 1 (1652), Nanbu Nobuaki donated 7 koku of shrine land. By [[:en:Kaei|Kaei]] 2 (1849), the total donations from the Nanbu domain amounted to 70 koku.<ref name="Heibonsha"/> festivals and other events were frequently attended by representatives from the Nanbu clan, confirming the shrine's status as a place of worship for the Nanbu family.<ref name="Kadokawa"/> In [[:en:Tenpo|Tenpo]] 5 (1834), Nanbu Toshiharu constructed a pilgrimage road from [[:en:Morioka|Morioka]] to the shrine, modeled after the [[:en:Nikko Kaido|Nikko Kaido]], a distance of about 15 km (5 ri).

The shrine was Ranked as a [[Village Shrine]] in the early [[Meiji]] period and was elevated to a [[Prefectural Shrine]] in [[:en:Taishō era|Taishō era]] 7 (1918). After World War II, it joined the Association of Shinto Shrines and was designated as a Beppyo Shrine in [[:en:Shōwa|Shōwa]] 32 (1957).

== Rituals ==
Until the early Meiji period, the shrine was influenced by the syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism and was managed by the Shugen-do temple Joju-in.

== Grounds ==
The shrine grounds feature a cedar tree over 1,000 years old, known as the "Inariyama Great Cedar," which is considered sacred. There is a custom of searching for white fox hair at the base of the cedar tree to pray for longevity.

'''Shiwa Inari Shrine''' (志和稲荷神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located on the western foothills of the Masuzawa settlement in [[Shiwa Town]], [[:en:Shiwa District, Iwate|Shiwa District]], [[:en:Iwate Prefecture|Iwate Prefecture]], on the left bank of the [[Takinakawa River]]. It is a former prefectural shrine and currently a Beppyo Shrine under the Association of Shinto Shrines.

Locally, it is sometimes considered one of the [[Inari shrine#Three Great Inari Shrines of Japan|Three Great Inari Shrines of Japan]]<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.lafrance.co.jp/company.htm |title=La France Onsen Kaisha|publisher=[[Shiwa Town]] [[Third Sector]] Shiwa Machizukuri Kikaku |accessdate=2018-11-16 }}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.tsukinowa-iwate.com/press/index8p40.html |title=Featured Information on Meijo Kigan |publisher=[[Tsukinowa Sake Brewery]] |accessdate=2018-11-16 }}</ref> or one of the [[Inari shrine#Five Great Inari Shrines of Japan|Five Great Inari Shrines of Japan]]<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.iwatekeizai.org/watching/watching_201801/8066/ |title=Shiwa Inari Shrine in Shiwa Town |publisher=[[Iwate Bank]] Iwate Economic Research Institute |date=2018-01 |accessdate=2018-12-16 }}</ref>{{Citation needed|date=2018-12|title=Sources claiming Shiwa Inari Shrine as one of the Five Great Inari Shrines of Japan}}.

Nearby, there is another shrine called [[Shiwa Koinari Shrine]].

== Enshrined Deities ==
The enshrined deities are [[Ukanomitama|Ukanomitama no Okami]] (Inari Okami), [[Sarutahiko Ōkami|Sarutahiko no Okami]], and [[Oomiyanome no Okami]]. The shrine considers Oomiyanome no Okami to be another name for [[Ame-no-Uzume|Ame-no-Uzume no Mikoto]].

During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], the shrine also worshipped [[:en:Ekādaśamukha|Ekādaśamukha]] as its principal Buddha.<ref>『Goryobun Shato』. This document, compiled in the mid-18th century, lists temples and shrines within the Morioka domain.</ref>

== History ==
According to tradition, the shrine was founded in [[:en:Tenki|Tenki]] 5 (1057) when [[Minamoto no Yoriyoshi]] enshrined a divided spirit from [[:en:Fushimi Inari Taisha|Fushimi Inari Taisha]] in Kyoto to pray for victory in his campaign against the Abe clan during the [[:en:Former Nine Years' War|Former Nine Years' War]]. Later, the Fujiwara clan members who ruled Shiwa District, such as Hiramune Takanari, are said to have rebuilt the shrine.<ref name="Kadokawa">『Kadokawa Japan Place name Dictionary』.</ref> Another tradition claims that [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] rebuilt the shrine during the [[:en:1189|Oshu War]] in 1189.<ref name="Heibonsha">『Iwate Place names』.</ref> There is also a tradition stating that the shrine was founded during the [[:en:Daido|Daido]] period (806-810), and another stating that [[Shiba Ienaga]] enshrined the deity from Fushimi Inari when he came to Shiwa District in [[:en:Kenmu|Kenmu]] 2 (1335) as the commander of Oshu.<ref name="Heibonsha"/><ref>『Shinto Dictionary』 Vol. 2, Heibonsha, 1939.</ref>

The shrine received patronage from the Shiba clan, who ruled Shiwa District throughout the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]]. In [[:en:Tensho|Tensho]] 16 (1588), [[Shiba Tsunenao]] built the shrine building,<ref>The shrine possesses a building plaque dated May 28, 1588.</ref> but after Tsunenao was ousted by [[Nanbu Nobunao]] that same year, the Nanbu clan became the shrine's patrons. After being attacked by Nobunao, Tsunenao hid in the shrine's affiliated temple, Joju-in, and then fled to Sanno-kai (a small basin in the upper reaches of the Takinakawa River, in present-day Tsuchidate, Shiwa Town).<ref name="Heibonsha"/>

The Nanbu clan's patronage continued, with records showing that Nanbu Nobunao donated over 24 koku of rice fields in [[:en:Keicho|Keicho]] 4 (1599). Additional donations were made, including 20 koku from Hirasawa Village (present-day Hirasawa, Shiwa Town) in 1612, and another 10 koku in 1614. In [[:en:Shoo|Shoo]] 1 (1652), Nanbu Nobuaki donated 7 koku of shrine land. By [[:en:Kaei|Kaei]] 2 (1849), the total donations from the Nanbu domain amounted to 70 koku.<ref name="Heibonsha"/> festivals and other events were frequently attended by representatives from the Nanbu clan, confirming the shrine's status as a place of worship for the Nanbu family.<ref name="Kadokawa"/> In [[:en:Tenpo|Tenpo]] 5 (1834), Nanbu Toshiharu constructed a pilgrimage road from [[:en:Morioka|Morioka]] to the shrine, modeled after the [[:en:Nikko Kaido|Nikko Kaido]], a distance of about 15 km (5 ri).

The shrine was Ranked as a [[Village Shrine]] in the early [[Meiji]] period and was elevated to a [[Prefectural Shrine]] in [[:en:Taishō era|Taishō era]] 7 (1918). After World War II, it joined the Association of Shinto Shrines and was designated as a Beppyo Shrine in [[:en:Shōwa|Shōwa]] 32 (1957).

== Rituals ==
Until the early Meiji period, the shrine was influenced by the syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism and was managed by the Shugen-do temple Joju-in.

== Grounds ==
The shrine grounds feature a cedar tree over 1,000 years old, known as the "Inariyama Great Cedar," which is considered sacred. There is a custom of searching for white fox hair at the base of the cedar tree to pray for longevity.

Additional donations were made, including 20 koku from Hirasawa Village (present-day Hirasawa, Shiwa Town) in 1612, and another 10 koku in 1614. In [[:en:Shoo|Shoo]] 1 (1652), Nanbu Nobuaki donated 7 koku of shrine land. By [[:en:Kaei|Kaei]] 2 (1849), the total donations from the Nanbu domain amounted to 70 koku.<ref name="Heibonsha"/> festivals and other events were frequently attended by representatives from the Nanbu clan, confirming the shrine's status as a place of worship for the Nanbu family.<ref name="Kadokawa"/> In [[:en:Tenpo|Tenpo]] 5 (1834), Nanbu Toshiharu constructed a pilgrimage road from [[:en:Morioka|Morioka]] to the shrine, modeled after the [[:en:Nikko Kaido|Nikko Kaido]], a distance of about 15 km (5 ri).

The shrine was Ranked as a [[Village Shrine]] in the early [[Meiji]] period and was elevated to a [[Prefectural Shrine]] in [[:en:Taishō era|Taishō era]] 7 (1918). After World War II, it joined the Association of Shinto Shrines and was designated as a Beppyo Shrine in [[:en:Shōwa|Shōwa]] 32 (1957).

== Rituals ==
Until the early Meiji period, the shrine was influenced by the syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism and was managed by the Shugen-do temple Joju-in.

== Grounds ==
The shrine grounds feature a cedar tree over 1,000 years old, known as the "Inariyama Great Cedar," which is considered sacred. There is a custom of searching for white fox hair at the base of the cedar tree to pray for longevity.

'''Shiwa Inari Shrine''' (志和稲荷神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located on the western foothills of the Masuzawa settlement in [[Shiwa Town]], [[:en:Shiwa District, Iwate|Shiwa District]], [[:en:Iwate Prefecture|Iwate Prefecture]], on the left bank of the [[Takinakawa River]]. It is a former prefectural shrine and currently a Beppyo Shrine under the Association of Shinto Shrines.

Locally, it is sometimes considered one of the [[Inari shrine#Three Great Inari Shrines of Japan|Three Great Inari Shrines of Japan]]<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.lafrance.co.jp/company.htm |title=La France Onsen Kaisha|publisher=[[Shiwa Town]] [[Third Sector]] Shiwa Machizukuri Kikaku |accessdate=2018-11-16 }}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.tsukinowa-iwate.com/press/index8p40.html |title=Featured Information on Meijo Kigan |publisher=[[Tsukinowa Sake Brewery]] |accessdate=2018-11-16 }}</ref> or one of the [[Inari shrine#Five Great Inari Shrines of Japan|Five Great Inari Shrines of Japan]]<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.iwatekeizai.org/watching/watching_201801/8066/ |title=Shiwa Inari Shrine in Shiwa Town |publisher=[[Iwate Bank]] Iwate Economic Research Institute |date=2018-01 |accessdate=2018-12-16 }}</ref>{{Citation needed|date=2018-12|title=Sources claiming Shiwa Inari Shrine as one of the Five Great Inari Shrines of Japan}}.

Nearby, there is another shrine called [[Shiwa Koinari Shrine]].

== Enshrined Deities ==
The enshrined deities are [[Ukanomitama|Ukanomitama no Okami]] (Inari Okami), [[Sarutahiko Ōkami|Sarutahiko no Okami]], and [[Oomiyanome no Okami]]. The shrine considers Oomiyanome no Okami to be another name for [[Ame-no-Uzume|Ame-no-Uzume no Mikoto]].

During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], the shrine also worshipped [[:en:Ekādaśamukha|Ekādaśamukha]] as its principal Buddha.<ref>『Goryobun Shato』. This document, compiled in the mid-18th century, lists temples and shrines within the Morioka domain.</ref>

== History ==
According to tradition, the shrine was founded in [[:en:Tenki|Tenki]] 5 (1057) when [[Minamoto no Yoriyoshi]] enshrined a divided spirit from [[:en:Fushimi Inari Taisha|Fushimi Inari Taisha]] in Kyoto to pray for victory in his campaign against the Abe clan during the [[:en:Former Nine Years' War|Former Nine Years' War]]. Later, the Fujiwara clan members who ruled Shiwa District, such as Hiramune Takanari, are said to have rebuilt the shrine.<ref name="Kadokawa">『Kadokawa Japan Place name Dictionary』.</ref> Another tradition claims that [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] rebuilt the shrine during the [[:en:1189|Oshu War]] in 1189.<ref name="Heibonsha">『Iwate Place names』.</ref> There is also a tradition stating that the shrine was founded during the [[:en:Daido|Daido]] period (806-810), and another stating that [[Shiba Ienaga]] enshrined the deity from Fushimi Inari when he came to Shiwa District in [[:en:Kenmu|Kenmu]] 2 (1335) as the commander of Oshu.<ref name="Heibonsha"/><ref>『Shinto Dictionary』 Vol. 2, Heibonsha, 1939.</ref>

The shrine received patronage from the Shiba clan, who ruled Shiwa District throughout the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]]. In [[:en:Tensho|Tensho]] 16 (1588), [[Shiba Tsunenao]] built the shrine building,<ref>The shrine possesses a building plaque dated May 28, 1588.</ref> but after Tsunenao was ousted by [[Nanbu Nobunao]] that same year, the Nanbu clan became the shrine's patrons. After being attacked by Nobunao, Tsunenao hid in the shrine's affiliated temple, Joju-in, and then fled to Sanno-kai (a small basin in the upper reaches of the Takinakawa River, in present-day Tsuchidate, Shiwa Town).<ref name="Heibonsha"/>

The Nanbu clan's patronage continued, with records showing that Nanbu Nobunao donated over 24 koku of rice fields in [[:en:Keicho|Keicho]] 4 (1599). Additional donations were made, including 20 koku from Hirasawa Village (present-day Hirasawa, Shiwa Town) in 1612, and another 10 koku in 1614. In [[:en:Shoo|Shoo]] 1 (1652), Nanbu Nobuaki donated 7 koku of shrine land. By [[:en:Kaei|Kaei]] 2 (1849), the total donations from the Nanbu domain amounted to 70 koku.<ref name="Heibonsha"/> festivals and other events were frequently attended by representatives from the Nanbu clan, confirming the shrine's status as a place of worship for the Nanbu family.<ref name="Kadokawa"/> In [[:en:Tenpo|Tenpo]] 5 (1834), Nanbu Toshiharu constructed a pilgrimage road from [[:en:Morioka|Morioka]] to the shrine, modeled after the [[:en:Nikko Kaido|Nikko Kaido]], a distance of about 15 km (5 ri).

The shrine was Ranked as a [[Village Shrine]] in the early [[Meiji]] period and was elevated to a [[Prefectural Shrine]] in [[:en:Taishō era|Taishō era]] 7 (1918). After World War II, it joined the Association of Shinto Shrines and was designated as a Beppyo Shrine in [[:en:Shōwa|Shōwa]] 32 (1957).

== Rituals ==
Until the early Meiji period, the shrine was influenced by the syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism and was managed by the Shugen-do temple Joju-in.

== Grounds ==
The shrine grounds feature a cedar tree over 1,000 years old, known as the "Inariyama Great Cedar," which is considered sacred. There is a custom of searching for white fox hair at the base of the cedar tree to pray for longevity.

{{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Shiwa Inari Shrine | image = File:志和稲荷神社 拝殿.jpg | location = [[:en:Iwate Prefecture|Iwate Prefecture]][[:en:Shiwa District, Iwate|Shiwa District]][[:en:Shiwa, Iwate|Shiwa Town]] Masuzawa Aza Maehira 17-1 | coordinates = {{Coord|39.558917|N|141.077833|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deity = {{ill|Sarutahiko Ōkami|WD=Q3090037}}; {{ill|Ukanomitama|WD=Q3080728}}; {{ill|Ōmiyanome|WD=Q24865597}} | type = Former Prefectural Shrine, Beppyo Shrine | founded = Traditionally [[:en:Tenki|Tenki]] 5 (1057) | main hall= [[Irimoya-zukuri]] | alternate names = | Pilgrimage Sites = | festival = May 5 | Rites = [[Hatsuuma|Hatsuuma festival]] (First Horse Day of the Second Lunar Month) | map = {{location map|Japan Iwate|lat_deg=39|lat_min=33|lat_sec=32.1|lon_deg=141|lon_min=04|lon_sec=40.2|label=Shiwa Inari Shrine|position=center|width=200|float=center}} | location = {{ill|Shiwa|WD=Q387336}} | architecture_style = {{ill|East Asian hip-and-gable roof|WD=Q855025}} }}
'''Shiwa Inari Shrine''' (志和稲荷神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located on the western foothills of the Masuzawa settlement in [[Shiwa Town]], [[:en:Shiwa District, Iwate|Shiwa District]], [[:en:Iwate Prefecture|Iwate Prefecture]], on the left bank of the [[Takinakawa River]]. It is a former prefectural shrine and currently a Beppyo Shrine under the Association of Shinto Shrines.
Locally, it is sometimes considered one of the [[Inari shrine#Three Great Inari Shrines of Japan|Three Great Inari Shrines of Japan]]<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.lafrance.co.jp/company.htm |title=La France Onsen Kaisha|publisher=[[Shiwa Town]] [[Third Sector]] Shiwa Machizukuri Kikaku |accessdate=2018-11-16 }}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.tsukinowa-iwate.com/press/index8p40.html |title=Featured Information on Meijo Kigan |publisher=[[Tsukinowa Sake Brewery]] |accessdate=2018-11-16 }}</ref> or one of the [[Inari shrine#Five Great Inari Shrines of Japan|Five Great Inari Shrines of Japan]]<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.iwatekeizai.org/watching/watching_201801/8066/ |title=Shiwa Inari Shrine in Shiwa Town |publisher=[[Iwate Bank]] Iwate Economic Research Institute |date=2018-01 |accessdate=2018-12-16 }}</ref>{{Citation needed|date=2018-12|title=Sources claiming Shiwa Inari Shrine as one of the Five Great Inari Shrines of Japan}}.
Nearby, there is another shrine called [[Shiwa Koinari Shrine]].
== Enshrined Deities == The enshrined deities are [[Ukanomitama|Ukanomitama no Okami]] (Inari Okami), [[Sarutahiko Ōkami|Sarutahiko no Okami]], and [[Oomiyanome no Okami]]. The shrine considers Oomiyanome no Okami to be another name for [[Ame-no-Uzume|Ame-no-Uzume no Mikoto]].
During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], the shrine also worshipped [[:en:Ekādaśamukha|Ekādaśamukha]] as its principal Buddha.<ref>『Goryobun Shato』. This document, compiled in the mid-18th century, lists temples and shrines within the Morioka domain.</ref>
== History == According to tradition, the shrine was founded in [[:en:Tenki|Tenki]] 5 (1057) when [[Minamoto no Yoriyoshi]] enshrined a divided spirit from [[:en:Fushimi Inari Taisha|Fushimi Inari Taisha]] in Kyoto to pray for victory in his campaign against the Abe clan during the [[:en:Former Nine Years' War|Former Nine Years' War]]. Later, the Fujiwara clan members who ruled Shiwa District, such as Hiramune Takanari, are said to have rebuilt the shrine.<ref name="Kadokawa">『Kadokawa Japan Place name Dictionary』.</ref> Another tradition claims that [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] rebuilt the shrine during the [[:en:1189|Oshu War]] in 1189.<ref name="Heibonsha">『Iwate Place names』.</ref> There is also a tradition stating that the shrine was founded during the [[:en:Daido|Daido]] period (806-810), and another stating that [[Shiba Ienaga]] enshrined the deity from Fushimi Inari when he came to Shiwa District in [[:en:Kenmu|Kenmu]] 2 (1335) as the commander of Oshu.<ref name="Heibonsha"/><ref>『Shinto Dictionary』 Vol. 2, Heibonsha, 1939.</ref>
The shrine received patronage from the Shiba clan, who ruled Shiwa District throughout the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]]. In [[:en:Tensho|Tensho]] 16 (1588), [[Shiba Tsunenao]] built the shrine building,<ref>The shrine possesses a building plaque dated May 28, 1588.</ref> but after Tsunenao was ousted by [[Nanbu Nobunao]] that same year, the Nanbu clan became the shrine's patrons. After being attacked by Nobunao, Tsunenao hid in the shrine's affiliated temple, Joju-in, and then fled to Sanno-kai (a small basin in the upper reaches of the Takinakawa River, in present-day Tsuchidate, Shiwa Town).<ref name="Heibonsha"/>
The Nanbu clan's patronage continued, with records showing that Nanbu Nobunao donated over 24 koku of rice fields in [[:en:Keicho|Keicho]] 4 (1599). Additional donations were made, including 20 koku from Hirasawa Village (present-day Hirasawa, Shiwa Town) in 1612, and another 10 koku in 1614. In [[:en:Shoo|Shoo]] 1 (1652), Nanbu Nobuaki donated 7 koku of shrine land. By [[:en:Kaei|Kaei]] 2 (1849), the total donations from the Nanbu domain amounted to 70 koku.<ref name="Heibonsha"/> festivals and other events were frequently attended by representatives from the Nanbu clan, confirming the shrine's status as a place of worship for the Nanbu family.<ref name="Kadokawa"/> In [[:en:Tenpo|Tenpo]] 5 (1834), Nanbu Toshiharu constructed a pilgrimage road from [[:en:Morioka|Morioka]] to the shrine, modeled after the [[:en:Nikko Kaido|Nikko Kaido]], a distance of about 15 km (5 ri).
The shrine was Ranked as a [[Village Shrine]] in the early [[Meiji]] period and was elevated to a [[Prefectural Shrine]] in [[:en:Taishō era|Taishō era]] 7 (1918). After World War II, it joined the Association of Shinto Shrines and was designated as a Beppyo Shrine in [[:en:Shōwa|Shōwa]] 32 (1957).
== Rituals == Until the early Meiji period, the shrine was influenced by the syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism and was managed by the Shugen-do temple Joju-in.
== Grounds == The shrine grounds feature a cedar tree over 1,000 years old, known as the "Inariyama Great Cedar," which is considered sacred. There is a custom of searching for white fox hair at the base of the cedar tree to pray for longevity.

In [[:en:Tensho|Tensho]] 16 (1588), [[Shiba Tsunenao]] built the shrine building,<ref>The shrine possesses a building plaque dated May 28, 1588.</ref> but after Tsunenao was ousted by [[Nanbu Nobunao]] that same year, the Nanbu clan became the shrine's patrons. After being attacked by Nobunao, Tsunenao hid in the shrine's affiliated temple, Joju-in, and then fled to Sanno-kai (a small basin in the upper reaches of the Takinakawa River, in present-day Tsuchidate, Shiwa Town).<ref name="Heibonsha"/>
The Nanbu clan's patronage continued, with records showing that Nanbu Nobunao donated over 24 koku of rice fields in [[:en:Keicho|Keicho]] 4 (1599). Additional donations were made, including 20 koku from Hirasawa Village (present-day Hirasawa, Shiwa Town) in 1612, and another 10 koku in 1614. In [[:en:Shoo|Shoo]] 1 (1652), Nanbu Nobuaki donated 7 koku of shrine land. By [[:en:Kaei|Kaei]] 2 (1849), the total donations from the Nanbu domain amounted to 70 koku.<ref name="Heibonsha"/> festivals and other events were frequently attended by representatives from the Nanbu clan, confirming the shrine's status as a place of worship for the Nanbu family.<ref name="Kadokawa"/> In [[:en:Tenpo|Tenpo]] 5 (1834), Nanbu Toshiharu constructed a pilgrimage road from [[:en:Morioka|Morioka]] to the shrine, modeled after the [[:en:Nikko Kaido|Nikko Kaido]], a distance of about 15 km (5 ri).
The shrine was Ranked as a [[Village Shrine]] in the early [[Meiji]] period and was elevated to a [[Prefectural Shrine]] in [[:en:Taishō era|Taishō era]] 7 (1918). After World War II, it joined the Association of Shinto Shrines and was designated as a Beppyo Shrine in [[:en:Shōwa|Shōwa]] 32 (1957).
== Rituals == Until the early Meiji period, the shrine was influenced by the syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism and was managed by the Shugen-do temple Joju-in.
== Grounds == The shrine grounds feature a cedar tree over 1,000 years old, known as the "Inariyama Great Cedar," which is considered sacred. There is a custom of searching for white fox hair at the base of the cedar tree to pray for longevity.

{{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Shiwa Inari Shrine | image = File:志和稲荷神社 拝殿.jpg | location = [[:en:Iwate Prefecture|Iwate Prefecture]][[:en:Shiwa District, Iwate|Shiwa District]][[:en:Shiwa, Iwate|Shiwa Town]] Masuzawa Aza Maehira 17-1 | coordinates = {{Coord|39.558917|N|141.077833|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deity = {{ill|Sarutahiko Ōkami|WD=Q3090037}}; {{ill|Ukanomitama|WD=Q3080728}}; {{ill|Ōmiyanome|WD=Q24865597}} | type = Former Prefectural Shrine, Beppyo Shrine | founded = Traditionally [[:en:Tenki|Tenki]] 5 (1057) | main hall= [[Irimoya-zukuri]] | alternate names = | Pilgrimage Sites = | festival = May 5 | Rites = [[Hatsuuma|Hatsuuma festival]] (First Horse Day of the Second Lunar Month) | map = {{location map|Japan Iwate|lat_deg=39|lat_min=33|lat_sec=32.1|lon_deg=141|lon_min=04|lon_sec=40.2|label=Shiwa Inari Shrine|position=center|width=200|float=center}} | location = {{ill|Shiwa|WD=Q387336}} | architecture_style = {{ill|East Asian hip-and-gable roof|WD=Q855025}} }}
'''Shiwa Inari Shrine''' (志和稲荷神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located on the western foothills of the Masuzawa settlement in [[Shiwa Town]], [[:en:Shiwa District, Iwate|Shiwa District]], [[:en:Iwate Prefecture|Iwate Prefecture]], on the left bank of the [[Takinakawa River]]. It is a former prefectural shrine and currently a Beppyo Shrine under the Association of Shinto Shrines.
Locally, it is sometimes considered one of the [[Inari shrine#Three Great Inari Shrines of Japan|Three Great Inari Shrines of Japan]]<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.lafrance.co.jp/company.htm |title=La France Onsen Kaisha|publisher=[[Shiwa Town]] [[Third Sector]] Shiwa Machizukuri Kikaku |accessdate=2018-11-16 }}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.tsukinowa-iwate.com/press/index8p40.html |title=Featured Information on Meijo Kigan |publisher=[[Tsukinowa Sake Brewery]] |accessdate=2018-11-16 }}</ref> or one of the [[Inari shrine#Five Great Inari Shrines of Japan|Five Great Inari Shrines of Japan]]<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.iwatekeizai.org/watching/watching_201801/8066/ |title=Shiwa Inari Shrine in Shiwa Town |publisher=[[Iwate Bank]] Iwate Economic Research Institute |date=2018-01 |accessdate=2018-12-16 }}</ref>{{Citation needed|date=2018-12|title=Sources claiming Shiwa Inari Shrine as one of the Five Great Inari Shrines of Japan}}.
Nearby, there is another shrine called [[Shiwa Koinari Shrine]].
== Enshrined Deities == The enshrined deities are [[Ukanomitama|Ukanomitama no Okami]] (Inari Okami), [[Sarutahiko Ōkami|Sarutahiko no Okami]], and [[Oomiyanome no Okami]]. The shrine considers Oomiyanome no Okami to be another name for [[Ame-no-Uzume|Ame-no-Uzume no Mikoto]].
During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], the shrine also worshipped [[:en:Ekādaśamukha|Ekādaśamukha]] as its principal Buddha.<ref>『Goryobun Shato』. This document, compiled in the mid-18th century, lists temples and shrines within the Morioka domain.</ref>
== History == According to tradition, the shrine was founded in [[:en:Tenki|Tenki]] 5 (1057) when [[Minamoto no Yoriyoshi]] enshrined a divided spirit from [[:en:Fushimi Inari Taisha|Fushimi Inari Taisha]] in Kyoto to pray for victory in his campaign against the Abe clan during the [[:en:Former Nine Years' War|Former Nine Years' War]]. Later, the Fujiwara clan members who ruled Shiwa District, such as Hiramune Takanari, are said to have rebuilt the shrine.<ref name="Kadokawa">『Kadokawa Japan Place name Dictionary』.</ref> Another tradition claims that [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] rebuilt the shrine during the [[:en:1189|Oshu War]] in 1189.<ref name="Heibonsha">『Iwate Place names』.</ref> There is also a tradition stating that the shrine was founded during the [[:en:Daido|Daido]] period (806-810), and another stating that [[Shiba Ienaga]] enshrined the deity from Fushimi Inari when he came to Shiwa District in [[:en:Kenmu|Kenmu]] 2 (1335) as the commander of Oshu.<ref name="Heibonsha"/><ref>『Shinto Dictionary』 Vol. 2, Heibonsha, 1939.</ref>
The shrine received patronage from the Shiba clan, who ruled Shiwa District throughout the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]]. In [[:en:Tensho|Tensho]] 16 (1588), [[Shiba Tsunenao]] built the shrine building,<ref>The shrine possesses a building plaque dated May 28, 1588.</ref> but after Tsunenao was ousted by [[Nanbu Nobunao]] that same year, the Nanbu clan became the shrine's patrons. After being attacked by Nobunao, Tsunenao hid in the shrine's affiliated temple, Joju-in, and then fled to Sanno-kai (a small basin in the upper reaches of the Takinakawa River, in present-day Tsuchidate, Shiwa Town).<ref name="Heibonsha"/>
The Nanbu clan's patronage continued, with records showing that Nanbu Nobunao donated over 24 koku of rice fields in [[:en:Keicho|Keicho]] 4 (1599). Additional donations were made, including 20 koku from Hirasawa Village (present-day Hirasawa, Shiwa Town) in 1612, and another 10 koku in 1614. In [[:en:Shoo|Shoo]] 1 (1652), Nanbu Nobuaki donated 7 koku of shrine land. By [[:en:Kaei|Kaei]] 2 (1849), the total donations from the Nanbu domain amounted to 70 koku.<ref name="Heibonsha"/> festivals and other events were frequently attended by representatives from the Nanbu clan, confirming the shrine's status as a place of worship for the Nanbu family.<ref name="Kadokawa"/> In [[:en:Tenpo|Tenpo]] 5 (1834), Nanbu Toshiharu constructed a pilgrimage road from [[:en:Morioka|Morioka]] to the shrine, modeled after the [[:en:Nikko Kaido|Nikko Kaido]], a distance of about 15 km (5 ri).
The shrine was Ranked as a [[Village Shrine]] in the early [[Meiji]] period and was elevated to a [[Prefectural Shrine]] in [[:en:Taishō era|Taishō era]] 7 (1918). After World War II, it joined the Association of Shinto Shrines and was designated as a Beppyo Shrine in [[:en:Shōwa|Shōwa]] 32 (1957).
== Rituals == Until the early Meiji period, the shrine was influenced by the syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism and was managed by the Shugen-do temple Joju-in.
== Grounds == The shrine grounds feature a cedar tree over 1,000 years old, known as the "Inariyama Great Cedar," which is considered sacred. There is a custom of searching for white fox hair at the base of the cedar tree to pray for longevity.

{{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Shiwa Inari Shrine | image = File:志和稲荷神社 拝殿.jpg | location = {{ill|Iwate Prefecture|en|Iwate Prefecture|WD=Q48326|lt=Iwate Prefecture}}{{ill|Shiwa District, Iwate|en|Shiwa District, Iwate|WD=Q1131886|lt=Shiwa District}}{{ill|Shiwa, Iwate|en|Shiwa, Iwate|WD=Q387336|lt=Shiwa Town}} Masuzawa Aza Maehira 17-1 | coordinates = {{Coord|39.558917|N|141.077833|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deity = {{ill|Sarutahiko Ōkami|WD=Q3090037}}; {{ill|Ukanomitama|WD=Q3080728}}; {{ill|Ōmiya-no-Me|WD=Q24865597}} | type = Former Prefectural Shrine, Beppyo Shrine | founded = Traditionally {{ill|Tenki|en|Tenki|WD=Q29536617|lt=Tenki}} 5 (1057) | main hall= [[Irimoya-zukuri]] | alternate names = | Pilgrimage Sites = | festival = May 5 | Rites = [[Hatsuuma|Hatsuuma festival]] (First Horse Day of the Second Lunar Month) | map = {{location map|Japan Iwate|lat_deg=39|lat_min=33|lat_sec=32.1|lon_deg=141|lon_min=04|lon_sec=40.2|label=Shiwa Inari Shrine|position=center|width=200|float=center}} | location = {{ill|Shiwa|WD=Q387336}} | architecture_style = {{ill|East Asian hip-and-gable roof|WD=Q855025}} }}
'''Shiwa Inari Shrine''' (志和稲荷神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} located on the western foothills of the Masuzawa settlement in [[Shiwa Town]], {{ill|Shiwa District, Iwate|en|Shiwa District, Iwate|WD=Q1131886|lt=Shiwa District}}, {{ill|Iwate Prefecture|en|Iwate Prefecture|WD=Q48326|lt=Iwate Prefecture}}, on the left bank of the [[Takinakawa River]]. It is a former prefectural shrine and currently a Beppyo Shrine under the Association of Shinto Shrines.
Locally, it is sometimes considered one of the [[Inari shrine#Three Great Inari Shrines of Japan|Three Great Inari Shrines of Japan]]<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.lafrance.co.jp/company.htm |title=La France Onsen Kaisha|publisher=[[Shiwa Town]] [[Third Sector]] Shiwa Machizukuri Kikaku |accessdate=2018-11-16 }}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.tsukinowa-iwate.com/press/index8p40.html |title=Featured Information on Meijo Kigan |publisher=[[Tsukinowa Sake Brewery]] |accessdate=2018-11-16 }}</ref> or one of the [[Inari shrine#Five Great Inari Shrines of Japan|Five Great Inari Shrines of Japan]]<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.iwatekeizai.org/watching/watching_201801/8066/ |title=Shiwa Inari Shrine in Shiwa Town |publisher=[[Iwate Bank]] Iwate Economic Research Institute |date=2018-01 |accessdate=2018-12-16 }}</ref>{{Citation needed|date=2018-12|title=Sources claiming Shiwa Inari Shrine as one of the Five Great Inari Shrines of Japan}}.
Nearby, there is another shrine called [[Shiwa Koinari Shrine]].
== Enshrined Deities == The enshrined deities are [[Ukanomitama|Ukanomitama no Okami]] (Inari Okami), [[Sarutahiko Ōkami|Sarutahiko no Okami]], and [[Oomiyanome no Okami]]. The shrine considers Oomiyanome no Okami to be another name for [[Ame-no-Uzume|Ame-no-Uzume no Mikoto]].
During the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}, the shrine also worshipped {{ill|Ekādaśamukha|en|Ekādaśamukha|WD=Q11404731|lt=Ekādaśamukha}} as its principal Buddha.<ref>『Goryobun Shato』. This document, compiled in the mid-18th century, lists temples and shrines within the Morioka domain.</ref>
== History == According to tradition, the shrine was founded in {{ill|Tenki|en|Tenki|WD=Q29536617|lt=Tenki}} 5 (1057) when [[Minamoto no Yoriyoshi]] enshrined a divided spirit from [[:en:Fushimi Inari Taisha|Fushimi Inari Taisha]] in Kyoto to pray for victory in his campaign against the Abe clan during the {{ill|Former Nine Years' War|en|Former Nine Years' War|WD=Q1187448|lt=Former Nine Years' War}}. Later, the Fujiwara clan members who ruled Shiwa District, such as Hiramune Takanari, are said to have rebuilt the shrine.<ref name="Kadokawa">『Kadokawa Japan Place name Dictionary』.</ref> Another tradition claims that [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] rebuilt the shrine during the {{ill|1189|en|1189|WD=Q19735|lt=Oshu War}} in 1189.<ref name="Heibonsha">『Iwate Place names』.</ref> There is also a tradition stating that the shrine was founded during the [[:en:Daido|Daido]] period (806-810), and another stating that [[Shiba Ienaga]] enshrined the deity from Fushimi Inari when he came to Shiwa District in {{ill|Kenmu|en|Kenmu|WD=Q1193970|lt=Kenmu}} 2 (1335) as the commander of Oshu.<ref name="Heibonsha"/><ref>『Shinto Dictionary』 Vol. 2, Heibonsha, 1939.</ref>
The shrine received patronage from the Shiba clan, who ruled Shiwa District throughout the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period}}. In {{ill|Tensho|en|Tensho|WD=Q7700654|lt=Tensho}} 16 (1588), [[Shiba Tsunenao]] built the shrine building,<ref>The shrine possesses a building plaque dated May 28, 1588.</ref> but after Tsunenao was ousted by [[Nanbu Nobunao]] that same year, the Nanbu clan became the shrine's patrons. After being attacked by Nobunao, Tsunenao hid in the shrine's affiliated temple, Joju-in, and then fled to Sanno-kai (a small basin in the upper reaches of the Takinakawa River, in present-day Tsuchidate, Shiwa Town).<ref name="Heibonsha"/>
The Nanbu clan's patronage continued, with records showing that Nanbu Nobunao donated over 24 koku of rice fields in [[:en:Keicho|Keicho]] 4 (1599). Additional donations were made, including 20 koku from Hirasawa Village (present-day Hirasawa, Shiwa Town) in 1612, and another 10 koku in 1614. In {{ill|Shoo|en|Shoo|WD=Q7506255|lt=Shoo}} 1 (1652), Nanbu Nobuaki donated 7 koku of shrine land. By {{ill|Kaei|en|Kaei|WD=Q1146125|lt=Kaei}} 2 (1849), the total donations from the Nanbu domain amounted to 70 koku.<ref name="Heibonsha"/> festivals and other events were frequently attended by representatives from the Nanbu clan, confirming the shrine's status as a place of worship for the Nanbu family.<ref name="Kadokawa"/> In [[:en:Tenpo|Tenpo]] 5 (1834), Nanbu Toshiharu constructed a pilgrimage road from {{ill|Morioka|en|Morioka|WD=Q200077|lt=Morioka}} to the shrine, modeled after the [[:en:Nikko Kaido|Nikko Kaido]], a distance of about 15 km (5 ri).
The shrine was Ranked as a [[Village Shrine]] in the early [[Meiji]] period and was elevated to a [[Prefectural Shrine]] in {{ill|Taishō era|en|Taishō era|WD=Q329174|lt=Taishō era}} 7 (1918). After World War II, it joined the Association of Shinto Shrines and was designated as a Beppyo Shrine in {{ill|Shōwa|en|Shōwa|WD=Q398996|lt=Shōwa}} 32 (1957).
== Rituals == Until the early Meiji period, the shrine was influenced by the syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism and was managed by the Shugen-do temple Joju-in.
== Grounds == The shrine grounds feature a cedar tree over 1,000 years old, known as the "Inariyama Great Cedar," which is considered sacred. There is a custom of searching for white fox hair at the base of the cedar tree to pray for longevity.

{{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Shiwa Inari Shrine | image = File:志和稲荷神社 拝殿.jpg | location = {{ill|Iwate Prefecture|en|Iwate Prefecture|WD=Q48326|lt=Iwate Prefecture}}{{ill|Shiwa District, Iwate|en|Shiwa District, Iwate|WD=Q1131886|lt=Shiwa District}}{{ill|Shiwa, Iwate|en|Shiwa, Iwate|WD=Q387336|lt=Shiwa Town}} Masuzawa Aza Maehira 17-1 | coordinates = {{Coord|39.558917|N|141.077833|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deity = {{ill|Sarutahiko Ōkami|WD=Q3090037}}; {{ill|Ukanomitama|WD=Q3080728}}; {{ill|Ōmiya-no-Me|WD=Q24865597}} | type = Former Prefectural Shrine, Beppyo Shrine | founded = Traditionally {{ill|Tenki|en|Tenki|WD=Q29536617|lt=Tenki}} 5 (1057) | main hall= [[Irimoya-zukuri]] | alternate names = | Pilgrimage Sites = | festival = May 5 | Rites = [[Hatsuuma|Hatsuuma festival]] (First Horse Day of the Second Lunar Month) | map = {{location map|Japan Iwate|lat_deg=39|lat_min=33|lat_sec=32.1|lon_deg=141|lon_min=04|lon_sec=40.2|label=Shiwa Inari Shrine|position=center|width=200|float=center}} | location = {{ill|Shiwa|WD=Q387336}} | architecture_style = {{ill|East Asian hip-and-gable roof|WD=Q855025}} }}
'''Shiwa Inari Shrine''' (志和稲荷神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} located on the western foothills of the Masuzawa settlement in [[Shiwa Town]], {{ill|Shiwa District, Iwate|en|Shiwa District, Iwate|WD=Q1131886|lt=Shiwa District}}, {{ill|Iwate Prefecture|en|Iwate Prefecture|WD=Q48326|lt=Iwate Prefecture}}, on the left bank of the [[Takinakawa River]]. It is a former prefectural shrine and currently a Beppyo Shrine under the Association of Shinto Shrines.
Locally, it is sometimes considered one of the [[Inari shrine#Three Great Inari Shrines of Japan|Three Great Inari Shrines of Japan]]<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.lafrance.co.jp/company.htm |title=La France Onsen Kaisha|publisher=[[Shiwa Town]] [[Third Sector]] Shiwa Machizukuri Kikaku |accessdate=2018-11-16 }}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.tsukinowa-iwate.com/press/index8p40.html |title=Featured Information on Meijo Kigan |publisher=[[Tsukinowa Sake Brewery]] |accessdate=2018-11-16 }}</ref> or one of the [[Inari shrine#Five Great Inari Shrines of Japan|Five Great Inari Shrines of Japan]]<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.iwatekeizai.org/watching/watching_201801/8066/ |title=Shiwa Inari Shrine in Shiwa Town |publisher=[[Iwate Bank]] Iwate Economic Research Institute |date=2018-01 |accessdate=2018-12-16 }}</ref>{{Citation needed|date=2018-12|title=Sources claiming Shiwa Inari Shrine as one of the Five Great Inari Shrines of Japan}}.
Nearby, there is another shrine called [[Shiwa Koinari Shrine]].
== Enshrined Deities == The enshrined deities are [[Ukanomitama|Ukanomitama no Okami]] (Inari Okami), [[Sarutahiko Ōkami|Sarutahiko no Okami]], and [[Oomiyanome no Okami]]. The shrine considers Oomiyanome no Okami to be another name for [[Ame-no-Uzume|Ame-no-Uzume no Mikoto]].
During the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}, the shrine also worshipped {{ill|Ekādaśamukha|en|Ekādaśamukha|WD=Q11404731|lt=Ekādaśamukha}} as its principal Buddha.<ref>『Goryobun Shato』. This document, compiled in the mid-18th century, lists temples and shrines within the Morioka domain.</ref>
== History == According to tradition, the shrine was founded in {{ill|Tenki|en|Tenki|WD=Q29536617|lt=Tenki}} 5 (1057) when [[Minamoto no Yoriyoshi]] enshrined a divided spirit from [[:en:Fushimi Inari Taisha|Fushimi Inari Taisha]] in Kyoto to pray for victory in his campaign against the Abe clan during the {{ill|Former Nine Years' War|en|Former Nine Years' War|WD=Q1187448|lt=Former Nine Years' War}}. Later, the Fujiwara clan members who ruled Shiwa District, such as Hiramune Takanari, are said to have rebuilt the shrine.<ref name="Kadokawa">『Kadokawa Japan Place name Dictionary』.</ref> Another tradition claims that [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] rebuilt the shrine during the {{ill|1189|en|1189|WD=Q19735|lt=Oshu War}} in 1189.<ref name="Heibonsha">『Iwate Place names』.</ref> There is also a tradition stating that the shrine was founded during the [[:en:Daido|Daido]] period (806-810), and another stating that [[Shiba Ienaga]] enshrined the deity from Fushimi Inari when he came to Shiwa District in {{ill|Kenmu|en|Kenmu|WD=Q1193970|lt=Kenmu}} 2 (1335) as the commander of Oshu.<ref name="Heibonsha"/><ref>『Shinto Dictionary』 Vol. 2, Heibonsha, 1939.</ref>
The shrine received patronage from the Shiba clan, who ruled Shiwa District throughout the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period}}. In {{ill|Tensho|en|Tensho|WD=Q7700654|lt=Tensho}} 16 (1588), [[Shiba Tsunenao]] built the shrine building,<ref>The shrine possesses a building plaque dated May 28, 1588.</ref> but after Tsunenao was ousted by [[Nanbu Nobunao]] that same year, the Nanbu clan became the shrine's patrons. After being attacked by Nobunao, Tsunenao hid in the shrine's affiliated temple, Joju-in, and then fled to Sanno-kai (a small basin in the upper reaches of the Takinakawa River, in present-day Tsuchidate, Shiwa Town).<ref name="Heibonsha"/>
The Nanbu clan's patronage continued, with records showing that Nanbu Nobunao donated over 24 koku of rice fields in [[:en:Keicho|Keicho]] 4 (1599). Additional donations were made, including 20 koku from Hirasawa Village (present-day Hirasawa, Shiwa Town) in 1612, and another 10 koku in 1614. In {{ill|Shoo|en|Shoo|WD=Q7506255|lt=Shoo}} 1 (1652), Nanbu Nobuaki donated 7 koku of shrine land. By {{ill|Kaei|en|Kaei|WD=Q1146125|lt=Kaei}} 2 (1849), the total donations from the Nanbu domain amounted to 70 koku.<ref name="Heibonsha"/> festivals and other events were frequently attended by representatives from the Nanbu clan, confirming the shrine's status as a place of worship for the Nanbu family.<ref name="Kadokawa"/> In [[:en:Tenpo|Tenpo]] 5 (1834), Nanbu Toshiharu constructed a pilgrimage road from {{ill|Morioka|en|Morioka|WD=Q200077|lt=Morioka}} to the shrine, modeled after the [[:en:Nikko Kaido|Nikko Kaido]], a distance of about 15 km (5 ri).
The shrine was Ranked as a [[Village Shrine]] in the early [[Meiji]] period and was elevated to a [[Prefectural Shrine]] in {{ill|Taishō era|en|Taishō era|WD=Q329174|lt=Taishō era}} 7 (1918). After World War II, it joined the Association of Shinto Shrines and was designated as a Beppyo Shrine in {{ill|Shōwa|en|Shōwa|WD=Q398996|lt=Shōwa}} 32 (1957).
== Rituals == Until the early Meiji period, the shrine was influenced by the syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism and was managed by the Shugen-do temple Joju-in.
== Grounds == The shrine grounds feature a cedar tree over 1,000 years old, known as the "Inariyama Great Cedar," which is considered sacred. There is a custom of searching for white fox hair at the base of the cedar tree to pray for longevity.

This document, compiled in the mid-18th century, lists temples and shrines within the Morioka domain.</ref>

Later, the Fujiwara clan members who ruled Shiwa District, such as Hiramune Takanari, are said to have rebuilt the shrine.<ref name="Kadokawa">『Kadokawa Japan Place name Dictionary』.</ref> Another tradition claims that [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] rebuilt the shrine during the {{ill|1189|en|1189|WD=Q19735|lt=Oshu War}} in 1189.<ref name="Heibonsha">『Iwate Place names』.</ref> There is also a tradition stating that the shrine was founded during the [[:en:Daido|Daido]] period (806-810), and another stating that [[Shiba Ienaga]] enshrined the deity from Fushimi Inari when he came to Shiwa District in {{ill|Kenmu|en|Kenmu|WD=Q1193970|lt=Kenmu}} 2 (1335) as the commander of Oshu.<ref name="Heibonsha"/><ref>『Shinto Dictionary』 Vol. 2, Heibonsha, 1939.</ref>
The shrine received patronage from the Shiba clan, who ruled Shiwa District throughout the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period}}. In {{ill|Tensho|en|Tensho|WD=Q7700654|lt=Tensho}} 16 (1588), [[Shiba Tsunenao]] built the shrine building,<ref>The shrine possesses a building plaque dated May 28, 1588.</ref> but after Tsunenao was ousted by [[Nanbu Nobunao]] that same year, the Nanbu clan became the shrine's patrons. After being attacked by Nobunao, Tsunenao hid in the shrine's affiliated temple, Joju-in, and then fled to Sanno-kai (a small basin in the upper reaches of the Takinakawa River, in present-day Tsuchidate, Shiwa Town).<ref name="Heibonsha"/>
The Nanbu clan's patronage continued, with records showing that Nanbu Nobunao donated over 24 koku of rice fields in [[:en:Keicho|Keicho]] 4 (1599). Additional donations were made, including 20 koku from Hirasawa Village (present-day Hirasawa, Shiwa Town) in 1612, and another 10 koku in 1614. In {{ill|Shoo|en|Shoo|WD=Q7506255|lt=Shoo}} 1 (1652), Nanbu Nobuaki donated 7 koku of shrine land. By {{ill|Kaei|en|Kaei|WD=Q1146125|lt=Kaei}} 2 (1849), the total donations from the Nanbu domain amounted to 70 koku.<ref name="Heibonsha"/> festivals and other events were frequently attended by representatives from the Nanbu clan, confirming the shrine's status as a place of worship for the Nanbu family.<ref name="Kadokawa"/> In [[:en:Tenpo|Tenpo]] 5 (1834), Nanbu Toshiharu constructed a pilgrimage road from {{ill|Morioka|en|Morioka|WD=Q200077|lt=Morioka}} to the shrine, modeled after the [[:en:Nikko Kaido|Nikko Kaido]], a distance of about 15 km (5 ri).
The shrine was Ranked as a [[Village Shrine]] in the early [[Meiji]] period and was elevated to a [[Prefectural Shrine]] in {{ill|Taishō era|en|Taishō era|WD=Q329174|lt=Taishō era}} 7 (1918). After World War II, it joined the Association of Shinto Shrines and was designated as a Beppyo Shrine in {{ill|Shōwa|en|Shōwa|WD=Q398996|lt=Shōwa}} 32 (1957).
== Rituals == Until the early Meiji period, the shrine was influenced by the syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism and was managed by the Shugen-do temple Joju-in.
== Grounds == The shrine grounds feature a cedar tree over 1,000 years old, known as the "Inariyama Great Cedar," which is considered sacred. There is a custom of searching for white fox hair at the base of the cedar tree to pray for longevity.
== References == {{Reflist}}

{{Shinto-stub}}

'''Shiwa Inari Shrine''' (志和稲荷神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} located on the western foothills of the Masuzawa settlement in [[Shiwa Town]], {{ill|Shiwa District, Iwate|en|Shiwa District, Iwate|WD=Q1131886|lt=Shiwa District}}, {{ill|Iwate Prefecture|en|Iwate Prefecture|WD=Q48326|lt=Iwate Prefecture}}, on the left bank of the [[Takinakawa River]]. It is a former prefectural shrine and currently a Beppyo Shrine under the Association of Shinto Shrines.
Locally, it is sometimes considered one of the [[Inari shrine#Three Great Inari Shrines of Japan|Three Great Inari Shrines of Japan]]<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.lafrance.co.jp/company.htm |title=La France Onsen Kaisha|publisher=[[Shiwa Town]] [[Third Sector]] Shiwa Machizukuri Kikaku |accessdate=2018-11-16 }}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.tsukinowa-iwate.com/press/index8p40.html |title=Featured Information on Meijo Kigan |publisher=[[Tsukinowa Sake Brewery]] |accessdate=2018-11-16 }}</ref> or one of the [[Inari shrine#Five Great Inari Shrines of Japan|Five Great Inari Shrines of Japan]]<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.iwatekeizai.org/watching/watching_201801/8066/ |title=Shiwa Inari Shrine in Shiwa Town |publisher=[[Iwate Bank]] Iwate Economic Research Institute |date=2018-01 |accessdate=2018-12-16 }}</ref>{{Citation needed|date=2018-12|title=Sources claiming Shiwa Inari Shrine as one of the Five Great Inari Shrines of Japan}}.
Nearby, there is another shrine called [[Shiwa Koinari Shrine]].

During the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}, the shrine also worshipped {{ill|Ekādaśamukha|en|Ekādaśamukha|WD=Q11404731|lt=Ekādaśamukha}} as its principal Buddha.<ref>『Goryobun Shato』. This document, compiled in the mid-18th century, lists temples and shrines within the Morioka domain.</ref>

The shrine received patronage from the Shiba clan, who ruled Shiwa District throughout the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period}}. In {{ill|Tensho|en|Tensho|WD=Q7700654|lt=Tensho}} 16 (1588), [[Shiba Tsunenao]] built the shrine building,<ref>The shrine possesses a building plaque dated May 28, 1588.</ref> but after Tsunenao was ousted by [[Nanbu Nobunao]] that same year, the Nanbu clan became the shrine's patrons. {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Shiwa Inari Shrine | image = File:志和稲荷神社 拝殿.jpg | location = {{ill|Iwate Prefecture|en|Iwate Prefecture|WD=Q48326|lt=Iwate Prefecture|1=Iwate Prefecture}}{{ill|Shiwa District, Iwate|en|Shiwa District, Iwate|WD=Q1131886|lt=Shiwa District|1=Shiwa District, Iwate}}{{ill|Shiwa, Iwate|en|Shiwa, Iwate|WD=Q387336|lt=Shiwa Town|1=Shiwa, Iwate}} Masuzawa Aza Maehira 17-1 | coordinates = {{Coord|39.558917|N|141.077833|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deity = {{ill|Sarutahiko Ōkami|WD=Q3090037|1=Sarutahiko Ōkami}}; {{ill|Ukanomitama|WD=Q3080728|1=Ukanomitama}}; {{ill|Ōmiya-no-Me|WD=Q24865597|1=Ōmiya-no-Me}} | type = Former Prefectural Shrine, Beppyo Shrine | founded = Traditionally {{ill|Tenki|en|Tenki|WD=Q29536617|lt=Tenki|1=Tenki}} 5 (1057) | main hall= [[Irimoya-zukuri]] | alternate names = | Pilgrimage Sites = | festival = May 5 | Rites = [[Hatsuuma|Hatsuuma festival]] (First Horse Day of the Second Lunar Month) | map = {{location map|Japan Iwate|lat_deg=39|lat_min=33|lat_sec=32.1|lon_deg=141|lon_min=04|lon_sec=40.2|label=Shiwa Inari Shrine|position=center|width=200|float=center}} | location = {{ill|Shiwa|WD=Q387336|1=Shiwa, Iwate}} | architecture_style = {{ill|East Asian hip-and-gable roof|WD=Q855025|1=East Asian hip-and-gable roof}} }}
'''Shiwa Inari Shrine''' (志和稲荷神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} located on the western foothills of the Masuzawa settlement in [[Shiwa Town]], {{ill|Shiwa District, Iwate|en|Shiwa District, Iwate|WD=Q1131886|lt=Shiwa District|1=Shiwa District, Iwate}}, {{ill|Iwate Prefecture|en|Iwate Prefecture|WD=Q48326|lt=Iwate Prefecture|1=Iwate Prefecture}}, on the left bank of the [[Takinakawa River]]. It is a former prefectural shrine and currently a Beppyo Shrine under the Association of Shinto Shrines.
Locally, it is sometimes considered one of the [[Inari shrine#Three Great Inari Shrines of Japan|Three Great Inari Shrines of Japan]]<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.lafrance.co.jp/company.htm |title=La France Onsen Kaisha|publisher=[[Shiwa Town]] [[Third Sector]] Shiwa Machizukuri Kikaku |accessdate=2018-11-16 }}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.tsukinowa-iwate.com/press/index8p40.html |title=Featured Information on Meijo Kigan |publisher=[[Tsukinowa Sake Brewery]] |accessdate=2018-11-16 }}</ref> or one of the [[Inari shrine#Five Great Inari Shrines of Japan|Five Great Inari Shrines of Japan]]<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.iwatekeizai.org/watching/watching_201801/8066/ |title=Shiwa Inari Shrine in Shiwa Town |publisher=[[Iwate Bank]] Iwate Economic Research Institute |date=2018-01 |accessdate=2018-12-16 }}</ref>{{Citation needed|date=2018-12|title=Sources claiming Shiwa Inari Shrine as one of the Five Great Inari Shrines of Japan}}.
Nearby, there is another shrine called [[Shiwa Koinari Shrine]].
== Enshrined Deities == The enshrined deities are [[Ukanomitama|Ukanomitama no Okami]] (Inari Okami), [[Sarutahiko Ōkami|Sarutahiko no Okami]], and [[Oomiyanome no Okami]]. The shrine considers Oomiyanome no Okami to be another name for [[Ame-no-Uzume|Ame-no-Uzume no Mikoto]].
During the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}, the shrine also worshipped {{ill|Ekādaśamukha|en|Ekādaśamukha|WD=Q11404731|lt=Ekādaśamukha|1=Ekādaśamukha}} as its principal Buddha.<ref>『Goryobun Shato』. This document, compiled in the mid-18th century, lists temples and shrines within the Morioka domain.</ref>
== History == According to tradition, the shrine was founded in {{ill|Tenki|en|Tenki|WD=Q29536617|lt=Tenki|1=Tenki}} 5 (1057) when [[Minamoto no Yoriyoshi]] enshrined a divided spirit from [[:en:Fushimi Inari Taisha|Fushimi Inari Taisha]] in Kyoto to pray for victory in his campaign against the Abe clan during the {{ill|Former Nine Years' War|en|Former Nine Years' War|WD=Q1187448|lt=Former Nine Years' War|1=Former Nine Years' War}}. Later, the Fujiwara clan members who ruled Shiwa District, such as Hiramune Takanari, are said to have rebuilt the shrine.<ref name="Kadokawa">『Kadokawa Japan Place name Dictionary』.</ref> Another tradition claims that [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] rebuilt the shrine during the {{ill|1189|en|1189|WD=Q19735|lt=Oshu War|1=1189}} in 1189.<ref name="Heibonsha">『Iwate Place names』.</ref> There is also a tradition stating that the shrine was founded during the [[:en:Daido|Daido]] period (806-810), and another stating that [[Shiba Ienaga]] enshrined the deity from Fushimi Inari when he came to Shiwa District in {{ill|Kenmu|en|Kenmu|WD=Q1193970|lt=Kenmu|1=Kenmu}} 2 (1335) as the commander of Oshu.<ref name="Heibonsha"/><ref>『Shinto Dictionary』 Vol. 2, Heibonsha, 1939.</ref>
The shrine received patronage from the Shiba clan, who ruled Shiwa District throughout the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period|1=Muromachi period}}. In {{ill|Tensho|en|Tensho|WD=Q7700654|lt=Tensho|1=Tensho}} 16 (1588), [[Shiba Tsunenao]] built the shrine building,<ref>The shrine possesses a building plaque dated May 28, 1588.</ref> but after Tsunenao was ousted by [[Nanbu Nobunao]] that same year, the Nanbu clan became the shrine's patrons. After being attacked by Nobunao, Tsunenao hid in the shrine's affiliated temple, Joju-in, and then fled to Sanno-kai (a small basin in the upper reaches of the Takinakawa River, in present-day Tsuchidate, Shiwa Town).<ref name="Heibonsha"/>
The Nanbu clan's patronage continued, with records showing that Nanbu Nobunao donated over 24 koku of rice fields in [[:en:Keicho|Keicho]] 4 (1599). Additional donations were made, including 20 koku from Hirasawa Village (present-day Hirasawa, Shiwa Town) in 1612, and another 10 koku in 1614. In {{ill|Shoo|en|Shoo|WD=Q7506255|lt=Shoo|1=Shoo}} 1 (1652), Nanbu Nobuaki donated 7 koku of shrine land. By {{ill|Kaei|en|Kaei|WD=Q1146125|lt=Kaei|1=Kaei}} 2 (1849), the total donations from the Nanbu domain amounted to 70 koku.<ref name="Heibonsha"/> festivals and other events were frequently attended by representatives from the Nanbu clan, confirming the shrine's status as a place of worship for the Nanbu family.<ref name="Kadokawa"/> In [[:en:Tenpo|Tenpo]] 5 (1834), Nanbu Toshiharu constructed a pilgrimage road from {{ill|Morioka|en|Morioka|WD=Q200077|lt=Morioka|1=Morioka}} to the shrine, modeled after the [[:en:Nikko Kaido|Nikko Kaido]], a distance of about 15 km (5 ri).
The shrine was Ranked as a [[Village Shrine]] in the early [[Meiji]] period and was elevated to a [[Prefectural Shrine]] in {{ill|Taishō era|en|Taishō era|WD=Q329174|lt=Taishō era|1=Taishō era}} 7 (1918). After World War II, it joined the Association of Shinto Shrines and was designated as a Beppyo Shrine in {{ill|Shōwa|en|Shōwa|WD=Q398996|lt=Shōwa|1=Shōwa}} 32 (1957).
== Rituals == Until the early Meiji period, the shrine was influenced by the syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism and was managed by the Shugen-do temple Joju-in.
== Grounds == The shrine grounds feature a cedar tree over 1,000 years old, known as the "Inariyama Great Cedar," which is considered sacred. There is a custom of searching for white fox hair at the base of the cedar tree to pray for longevity.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== External Links == *[http://www.shiwa-oinarisan.jp Shiwa Inari Shrine] (official site) *[http://www.ganshinsei.jp/shiwachou/118shiwainarijinja.html Shiwa Inari Shrine] (Iwate Prefecture Shinto Youth Association)
{{Shinto-stub}}
== References == {{reflist}}

Later, the Fujiwara clan members who ruled Shiwa District, such as Hiramune Takanari, are said to have rebuilt the shrine.<ref name="Kadokawa">『Kadokawa Japan Place name Dictionary』.</ref> Another tradition claims that [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] rebuilt the shrine during the {{ill|1189|en|1189|WD=Q19735|lt=Oshu War|1=1189}} in 1189.<ref name="Heibonsha">『Iwate Place names』.</ref> There is also a tradition stating that the shrine was founded during the [[:en:Daido|Daido]] period (806-810), and another stating that [[Shiba Ienaga]] enshrined the deity from Fushimi Inari when he came to Shiwa District in {{ill|Kenmu|en|Kenmu|WD=Q1193970|lt=Kenmu|1=Kenmu}} 2 (1335) as the commander of Oshu.<ref name="Heibonsha"/><ref>『Shinto Dictionary』 Vol. 2, Heibonsha, 1939.</ref>
The shrine received patronage from the Shiba clan, who ruled Shiwa District throughout the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period|1=Muromachi period}}. In {{ill|Tensho|en|Tensho|WD=Q7700654|lt=Tensho|1=Tensho}} 16 (1588), [[Shiba Tsunenao]] built the shrine building,<ref>The shrine possesses a building plaque dated May 28, 1588.</ref> but after Tsunenao was ousted by [[Nanbu Nobunao]] that same year, the Nanbu clan became the shrine's patrons. After being attacked by Nobunao, Tsunenao hid in the shrine's affiliated temple, Joju-in, and then fled to Sanno-kai (a small basin in the upper reaches of the Takinakawa River, in present-day Tsuchidate, Shiwa Town).<ref name="Heibonsha"/>
The Nanbu clan's patronage continued, with records showing that Nanbu Nobunao donated over 24 koku of rice fields in [[:en:Keicho|Keicho]] 4 (1599). Additional donations were made, including 20 koku from Hirasawa Village (present-day Hirasawa, Shiwa Town) in 1612, and another 10 koku in 1614. In {{ill|Shoo|en|Shoo|WD=Q7506255|lt=Shoo|1=Shoo}} 1 (1652), Nanbu Nobuaki donated 7 koku of shrine land. By {{ill|Kaei|en|Kaei|WD=Q1146125|lt=Kaei|1=Kaei}} 2 (1849), the total donations from the Nanbu domain amounted to 70 koku.<ref name="Heibonsha"/> festivals and other events were frequently attended by representatives from the Nanbu clan, confirming the shrine's status as a place of worship for the Nanbu family.<ref name="Kadokawa"/> In [[:en:Tenpo|Tenpo]] 5 (1834), Nanbu Toshiharu constructed a pilgrimage road from {{ill|Morioka|en|Morioka|WD=Q200077|lt=Morioka|1=Morioka}} to the shrine, modeled after the [[:en:Nikko Kaido|Nikko Kaido]], a distance of about 15 km (5 ri).
The shrine was Ranked as a [[Village Shrine]] in the early [[Meiji]] period and was elevated to a [[Prefectural Shrine]] in {{ill|Taishō era|en|Taishō era|WD=Q329174|lt=Taishō era|1=Taishō era}} 7 (1918). After World War II, it joined the Association of Shinto Shrines and was designated as a Beppyo Shrine in {{ill|Shōwa|en|Shōwa|WD=Q398996|lt=Shōwa|1=Shōwa}} 32 (1957).
== Rituals == Until the early Meiji period, the shrine was influenced by the syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism and was managed by the Shugen-do temple Joju-in.
== Grounds == The shrine grounds feature a cedar tree over 1,000 years old, known as the "Inariyama Great Cedar," which is considered sacred. There is a custom of searching for white fox hair at the base of the cedar tree to pray for longevity.

'''Shiwa Inari Shrine''' (志和稲荷神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} located on the western foothills of the Masuzawa settlement in [[Shiwa Town]], {{ill|Shiwa District, Iwate|en|Shiwa District, Iwate|WD=Q1131886|lt=Shiwa District|1=Shiwa District, Iwate}}, {{ill|Iwate Prefecture|en|Iwate Prefecture|WD=Q48326|lt=Iwate Prefecture|1=Iwate Prefecture}}, on the left bank of the [[Takinakawa River]]. It is a former prefectural shrine and currently a Beppyo Shrine under the Association of Shinto Shrines.
Locally, it is sometimes considered one of the [[Inari shrine#Three Great Inari Shrines of Japan|Three Great Inari Shrines of Japan]]<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.lafrance.co.jp/company.htm |title=La France Onsen Kaisha|publisher=[[Shiwa Town]] [[Third Sector]] Shiwa Machizukuri Kikaku |accessdate=2018-11-16 }}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.tsukinowa-iwate.com/press/index8p40.html |title=Featured Information on Meijo Kigan |publisher=[[Tsukinowa Sake Brewery]] |accessdate=2018-11-16 }}</ref> or one of the [[Inari shrine#Five Great Inari Shrines of Japan|Five Great Inari Shrines of Japan]]<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.iwatekeizai.org/watching/watching_201801/8066/ |title=Shiwa Inari Shrine in Shiwa Town |publisher=[[Iwate Bank]] Iwate Economic Research Institute |date=2018-01 |accessdate=2018-12-16 }}</ref>{{Citation needed|date=2018-12|title=Sources claiming Shiwa Inari Shrine as one of the Five Great Inari Shrines of Japan}}.
Nearby, there is another shrine called [[Shiwa Koinari Shrine]].
== Enshrined Deities == The enshrined deities are [[Ukanomitama|Ukanomitama no Okami]] (Inari Okami), [[Sarutahiko Ōkami|Sarutahiko no Okami]], and [[Oomiyanome no Okami]]. The shrine considers Oomiyanome no Okami to be another name for [[Ame-no-Uzume|Ame-no-Uzume no Mikoto]].
During the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}, the shrine also worshipped {{ill|Ekādaśamukha|en|Ekādaśamukha|WD=Q11404731|lt=Ekādaśamukha|1=Ekādaśamukha}} as its principal Buddha.<ref>『Goryobun Shato』. This document, compiled in the mid-18th century, lists temples and shrines within the Morioka domain.</ref>

In {{ill|Tensho|en|Tensho|WD=Q7700654|lt=Tensho|1=Tensho}} 16 (1588), [[Shiba Tsunenao]] built the shrine building,<ref>The shrine possesses a building plaque dated May 28, 1588.</ref> but after Tsunenao was ousted by [[Nanbu Nobunao]] that same year, the Nanbu clan became the shrine's patrons. <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Shiwa Inari Shrine]][[ja:志和稲荷神社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Shiwa Inari Shrine]][[ja:志和稲荷神社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[Category:sn_crud|TESTcommons:Category:Shiwa Inari Shrine]][[ja:志和稲荷神社]]
{{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Shiwa Inari Shrine | image = File:志和稲荷神社 拝殿.jpg | location = {{ill|Iwate Prefecture|en|Iwate Prefecture|WD=Q48326|lt=Iwate Prefecture|1=Iwate Prefecture}}{{ill|Shiwa District, Iwate|en|Shiwa District, Iwate|WD=Q1131886|lt=Shiwa District|1=Shiwa District, Iwate}}{{ill|Shiwa, Iwate|en|Shiwa, Iwate|WD=Q387336|lt=Shiwa Town|1=Shiwa, Iwate}} Masuzawa Aza Maehira 17-1 | coordinates = {{Coord|39.558917|N|141.077833|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deity = {{ill|Sarutahiko Ōkami|WD=Q3090037|1=Sarutahiko Ōkami}}; {{ill|Ukanomitama|WD=Q3080728|1=Ukanomitama}}; {{ill|Ōmiya-no-Me|WD=Q24865597|1=Ōmiya-no-Me}} | type = Former Prefectural Shrine, Beppyo Shrine | founded = Traditionally {{ill|Tenki|en|Tenki|WD=Q29536617|lt=Tenki|1=Tenki}} 5 (1057) | main hall= [[Irimoya-zukuri]] | alternate names = | Pilgrimage Sites = | festival = May 5 | Rites = [[Hatsuuma|Hatsuuma festival]] (First Horse Day of the Second Lunar Month) | map = {{location map|Japan Iwate|lat_deg=39|lat_min=33|lat_sec=32.1|lon_deg=141|lon_min=04|lon_sec=40.2|label=Shiwa Inari Shrine|position=center|width=200|float=center}} | location = {{ill|Shiwa|WD=Q387336|1=Shiwa, Iwate}} | architecture_style = {{ill|East Asian hip-and-gable roof|WD=Q855025|1=East Asian hip-and-gable roof}} }}
'''Shiwa Inari Shrine''' (志和稲荷神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} located on the western foothills of the Masuzawa settlement in [[Shiwa Town]], {{ill|Shiwa District, Iwate|en|Shiwa District, Iwate|WD=Q1131886|lt=Shiwa District|1=Shiwa District, Iwate}}, {{ill|Iwate Prefecture|en|Iwate Prefecture|WD=Q48326|lt=Iwate Prefecture|1=Iwate Prefecture}}, on the left bank of the [[Takinakawa River]]. It is a former prefectural shrine and currently a Beppyo Shrine under the Association of Shinto Shrines.
Locally, it is sometimes considered one of the [[Inari shrine#Three Great Inari Shrines of Japan|Three Great Inari Shrines of Japan]]<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.lafrance.co.jp/company.htm |title=La France Onsen Kaisha|publisher=[[Shiwa Town]] [[Third Sector]] Shiwa Machizukuri Kikaku |accessdate=2018-11-16 }}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.tsukinowa-iwate.com/press/index8p40.html |title=Featured Information on Meijo Kigan |publisher=[[Tsukinowa Sake Brewery]] |accessdate=2018-11-16 }}</ref> or one of the [[Inari shrine#Five Great Inari Shrines of Japan|Five Great Inari Shrines of Japan]]<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.iwatekeizai.org/watching/watching_201801/8066/ |title=Shiwa Inari Shrine in Shiwa Town |publisher=[[Iwate Bank]] Iwate Economic Research Institute |date=2018-01 |accessdate=2018-12-16 }}</ref>{{Citation needed|date=2018-12|title=Sources claiming Shiwa Inari Shrine as one of the Five Great Inari Shrines of Japan}}.
Nearby, there is another shrine called [[Shiwa Koinari Shrine]].
== Enshrined Deities == The enshrined deities are [[Ukanomitama|Ukanomitama no Okami]] (Inari Okami), [[Sarutahiko Ōkami|Sarutahiko no Okami]], and [[Oomiyanome no Okami]]. The shrine considers Oomiyanome no Okami to be another name for [[Ame-no-Uzume|Ame-no-Uzume no Mikoto]].
During the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}, the shrine also worshipped {{ill|Ekādaśamukha|en|Ekādaśamukha|WD=Q11404731|lt=Ekādaśamukha|1=Ekādaśamukha}} as its principal Buddha.<ref>『Goryobun Shato』. This document, compiled in the mid-18th century, lists temples and shrines within the Morioka domain.</ref>
== History == According to tradition, the shrine was founded in {{ill|Tenki|en|Tenki|WD=Q29536617|lt=Tenki|1=Tenki}} 5 (1057) when [[Minamoto no Yoriyoshi]] enshrined a divided spirit from [[:en:Fushimi Inari Taisha|Fushimi Inari Taisha]] in Kyoto to pray for victory in his campaign against the Abe clan during the {{ill|Former Nine Years' War|en|Former Nine Years' War|WD=Q1187448|lt=Former Nine Years' War|1=Former Nine Years' War}}. Later, the Fujiwara clan members who ruled Shiwa District, such as Hiramune Takanari, are said to have rebuilt the shrine.<ref name="Kadokawa">『Kadokawa Japan Place name Dictionary』.</ref> Another tradition claims that [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] rebuilt the shrine during the {{ill|1189|en|1189|WD=Q19735|lt=Oshu War|1=1189}} in 1189.<ref name="Heibonsha">『Iwate Place names』.</ref> There is also a tradition stating that the shrine was founded during the [[:en:Daido|Daido]] period (806-810), and another stating that [[Shiba Ienaga]] enshrined the deity from Fushimi Inari when he came to Shiwa District in {{ill|Kenmu|en|Kenmu|WD=Q1193970|lt=Kenmu|1=Kenmu}} 2 (1335) as the commander of Oshu.<ref name="Heibonsha"/><ref>『Shinto Dictionary』 Vol. 2, Heibonsha, 1939.</ref>
The shrine received patronage from the Shiba clan, who ruled Shiwa District throughout the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period|1=Muromachi period}}. In {{ill|Tensho|en|Tensho|WD=Q7700654|lt=Tensho|1=Tensho}} 16 (1588), [[Shiba Tsunenao]] built the shrine building,<ref>The shrine possesses a building plaque dated May 28, 1588.</ref> but after Tsunenao was ousted by [[Nanbu Nobunao]] that same year, the Nanbu clan became the shrine's patrons. After being attacked by Nobunao, Tsunenao hid in the shrine's affiliated temple, Joju-in, and then fled to Sanno-kai (a small basin in the upper reaches of the Takinakawa River, in present-day Tsuchidate, Shiwa Town).<ref name="Heibonsha"/>
The Nanbu clan's patronage continued, with records showing that Nanbu Nobunao donated over 24 koku of rice fields in [[:en:Keicho|Keicho]] 4 (1599). Additional donations were made, including 20 koku from Hirasawa Village (present-day Hirasawa, Shiwa Town) in 1612, and another 10 koku in 1614. In {{ill|Shoo|en|Shoo|WD=Q7506255|lt=Shoo|1=Shoo}} 1 (1652), Nanbu Nobuaki donated 7 koku of shrine land. By {{ill|Kaei|en|Kaei|WD=Q1146125|lt=Kaei|1=Kaei}} 2 (1849), the total donations from the Nanbu domain amounted to 70 koku.<ref name="Heibonsha"/> festivals and other events were frequently attended by representatives from the Nanbu clan, confirming the shrine's status as a place of worship for the Nanbu family.<ref name="Kadokawa"/> In [[:en:Tenpo|Tenpo]] 5 (1834), Nanbu Toshiharu constructed a pilgrimage road from {{ill|Morioka|en|Morioka|WD=Q200077|lt=Morioka|1=Morioka}} to the shrine, modeled after the [[:en:Nikko Kaido|Nikko Kaido]], a distance of about 15 km (5 ri).
The shrine was Ranked as a [[Village Shrine]] in the early [[Meiji]] period and was elevated to a [[Prefectural Shrine]] in {{ill|Taishō era|en|Taishō era|WD=Q329174|lt=Taishō era|1=Taishō era}} 7 (1918). After World War II, it joined the Association of Shinto Shrines and was designated as a Beppyo Shrine in {{ill|Shōwa|en|Shōwa|WD=Q398996|lt=Shōwa|1=Shōwa}} 32 (1957).
== Rituals == Until the early Meiji period, the shrine was influenced by the syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism and was managed by the Shugen-do temple Joju-in.
== Grounds == The shrine grounds feature a cedar tree over 1,000 years old, known as the "Inariyama Great Cedar," which is considered sacred. There is a custom of searching for white fox hair at the base of the cedar tree to pray for longevity.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== External Links == *[http://www.shiwa-oinarisan.jp Shiwa Inari Shrine] (official site) *[http://www.ganshinsei.jp/shiwachou/118shiwainarijinja.html Shiwa Inari Shrine] (Iwate Prefecture Shinto Youth Association)
{{Shinto-stub}}
